\mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O H} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

$$
K _ {\mathrm {a}} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O} ^ {-} \right] \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O H} \right]} = 1. 7 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5}
$$

First, let’s take the logarithm of each term in this equation and multiply through by –1 

$$
- \log K _ {a} = 4. 7 6 = - \log \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] - \log \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O} ^ {-} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O H} \right]}
$$

Now, let’s replace $- \mathrm { l o g } [ \mathrm { H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { O } ^ { + } ]$ with pH and rearrange the equation to obtain the result shown here. 

$$
\mathrm {p H} = 4. 7 6 + \log \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O} ^ {-} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O H} \right]} \tag {6.6.1}
$$

Equation tells us a great deal about the relationship between pH and the relative amounts of acetic acid and acetate at equilibrium. If the concentrations of $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O H }$ and $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O ^ { - } }$ are equal, then Equation reduces to 

$$
\mathrm {p H} = 4. 7 6 + \log (1) = 4. 7 6 + 0 = 4. 7 6
$$

If the concentration of $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O ^ { - } }$ is greater than that of $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O H }$ , then the log term in Equation is positive and the pH is greater than 4.76. This is a reasonable result because we expect the concentration of the conjugate base, $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O ^ { - } }$ , to increase as the pH increases. Similar reasoning will convince you that the pH is less than 4.76 when the concentration of $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O H }$ exceeds that of $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O ^ { - } }$ . 

Now we are ready to construct acetic acid’s ladder diagram (Figure 6.6.1 ). First, we draw a vertical arrow that represents the solution’s pH, with smaller (more acidic) pH levels at the bottom and larger (more basic) pH levels at the top. Second, we draw a horizontal line at a pH equal to acetic acid’s $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ value. This line, or step on the ladder, divides the pH axis into regions where either $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O H }$ or $\mathrm { C H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { C O O ^ { - } }$ is the predominate species. This completes the ladder diagram. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/391ff0b2ae6093c58931b3560eba627a44540c1d5141dfe9e25dd77711a8611c.jpg)



Figure 6.6.1 . Acid–base ladder diagram for acetic acid showing the relative concentrations of $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O H }$ and $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O ^ { - } }$ . A simpler version of this ladder diagram dispenses with the equalities and inequalities and shows only the predominate species in each region.


Using the ladder diagram, it is easy to identify the predominate form of acetic acid at any pH. At a pH of 3.5, for example, acetic acid exists primarily as $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O H }$ . If we add sufficient base to the solution such that the pH increases to 6.5, the predominate form of acetic acid is $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O ^ { - } }$ . 

#  Example 6.6.1

Draw a ladder diagram for the weak base $p$ ‐nitrophenolate and identify its predominate form at a pH of 6.00. 

# Solution

To draw a ladder diagram for a weak base, we simply draw the ladder diagram for its conjugate weak acid. From Appendix 11, the $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ for $p$ ‐nitrophenol is 7.15. The resulting ladder diagram is shown in Figure 6.6.2 . At a pH of 6.00, $p$ ‐nitrophenolate is present primarily in its weak acid form. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/9436050514be93fd303b76c493fc2d619d535fbbf53249b2865a0054eb52970d.jpg)



Figure 6.6.2 . Acid–base ladder diagram for $p$ ‐nitrophenolate.


#  Exercise 6.6.1

Draw a ladder diagram for carbonic acid, $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C O } _ { 3 }$ . Because $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C O } _ { 3 }$ is a diprotic weak acid, your ladder diagram will have two steps. What is the predominate form of carbonic acid when the pH is 7.00? Relevant equilibrium constants are in Appendix 11. 

# Answer

From Appendix 11, the $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ values for $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C O } _ { 3 }$ are 6.352 and 10.329. The ladder diagram for $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C O } _ { 3 }$ is shown below. The predominate form at a pH of 7.00 is $\mathbf { H C O _ { 3 } ^ { - } }$ . 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/bcbbfe666cc7ef92a273ec2b0cfaf2164c68fb10391e6b1ab7d41daf64eb43cd.jpg)


A ladder diagram is particularly useful for evaluating the reactivity between a weak acid and a weak base. Figure 6.6.3 , for example, shows a single ladder diagram for acetic acid/acetate and for $p$ ‐nitrophenol $p$ ‐nitrophenolate. An acid and a base can not co‐exist if their respective areas of predominance do not overlap. If we mix together solutions of acetic acid and sodium p‐ nitrophenolate, the reaction 

$$
\mathrm {C} _ {6} \mathrm {H} _ {4} \mathrm {N O} _ {2} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O H} (a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {C} _ {6} \mathrm {H} _ {4} \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \mathrm {H} (a q) \tag {6.6.2}
$$

occurs because the areas of predominance for acetic acid and $p$ ‐nitrophenolate do not overlap. The solution’s final composition depends on which species is the limiting reagent. The following example shows how we can use the ladder diagram in Figure 6.6.3 to evaluate the result of mixing together solutions of acetic acid and $p$ ‐nitrophenolate. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/7221eca0f4090ca2e71d6757f1f722d2e342de314d140b62977e398d04089ea6.jpg)



Figure 6.6.3 . Acid–base ladder diagram showing the areas of predominance for acetic acid/acetate and for $p$ ‐nitrophenol/p‐ nitrophenolate. The areas shaded in blue shows the pH range where the weak bases are the predominate species; the weak acid forms are the predominate species in the areas shaded in pink.


#  Example 6.6.2

Predict the approximate pH and the final composition after mixing together 0.090 moles of acetic acid and 0.040 moles of $p \mathrm { . }$ ‐ nitrophenolate. 

# Solution

The ladder diagram in Figure 6.6.3 indicates that the reaction between acetic acid and $p$ ‐nitrophenolate is favorable. Because acetic acid is in excess, we assume the reaction of $p$ ‐nitrophenolate to $p$ ‐nitrophenol is complete. At equilibrium essentially no $p$ ‐nitrophenolate remains and there are $0 . 0 4 0 \ \mathrm { m o l }$ of $p$ ‐nitrophenol. Converting $p$ ‐nitrophenolate to $p$ ‐nitrophenol consumes 0.040 moles of acetic acid; thus 

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {m o l e s C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O H} = 0. 0 9 0 - 0. 0 4 0 = 0. 0 5 0 \mathrm {m o l} \\ \mathrm {m o l e s C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O} ^ {-} = 0. 0 4 0 \mathrm {m o l} \\ \end{array}
$$

According to the ladder diagram, the pH is 4.74 when there are equal amounts of $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O H }$ and $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O ^ { - } }$ . Because we have slightly more $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O H }$ than $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O ^ { - } }$ , the pH is slightly less than 4.74. 

#  Exercise 6.6.2

Using Figure 6.6.3 , predict the approximate pH and the composition of the solution formed by mixing together 0.090 moles of $p$ ‐nitrophenolate and 0.040 moles of acetic acid. 

# Answer

The ladder diagram in Figure 6.6.3 indicates that the reaction between acetic acid and $p$ ‐nitrophenolate is favorable. Because $p$ ‐nitrophenolate is in excess, we assume the reaction of acetic acid to acetate is complete. At equilibrium essentially no acetic acid remains and there are 0.040 moles of acetate. Converting acetic acid to acetate consumes 0.040 moles of $p$ ‐nitrophenolate; thus 

$$
\text {m o l e s} p \text {n i t r o p h e n o l a t e} = 0. 0 9 0 - 0. 0 4 0 = 0. 0 5 0 \mathrm {m o l}
$$

moles p-nitrophenol = 0.040 mol 

According to the ladder diagram for this system, the $\mathrm { p H }$ is 7.15 when there are equal concentrations of $p$ ‐nitrophenol and $p \mathrm { . }$ ‐ nitrophenolate. Because we have slightly more $p$ ‐nitrophenolate than we have $p$ ‐nitrophenol, the pH is slightly greater than 7.15. 

If the areas of predominance for an acid and a base overlap, then we do not expect that much of a reaction will occur. For example, if we mix together solutions of $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O ^ { - } }$ and $p$ ‐nitrophenol, we do not expect a significant change in the moles of either reagent. Furthermore, the pH of the mixture must be between 4.76 and 7.15, with the exact pH depending upon the relative amounts of $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O ^ { - } }$ and $p$ ‐nitrophenol. 

We also can use an acid–base ladder diagram to evaluate the effect of pH on other equilibria. For example, the solubility of $\mathrm { C a F } _ { 2 }$ 

$$
\mathrm {C a F} _ {2} (s) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {C a} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {F} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

is affected by $\mathrm { p H }$ because $\mathrm { F ^ { - } }$ is a weak base. From Le Châtelier’s principle, we know that converting $\mathrm { F ^ { - } }$ to HF will increase the solubility of $\mathrm { C a F } _ { 2 }$ . To minimize the solubility of $\mathrm { C a F } _ { 2 }$ we need to maintain the solution’s pH so that $\mathrm { F ^ { - } }$ is the predominate species. The ladder diagram for HF (Figure 6.6.4 ) shows us that maintaining a pH of more than 3.17 will minimize solubility losses. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/150aacb8c9ffc00c40dbead7dff38af1668b359d428c7dc35ec0a9a42275b25a.jpg)



Figure 6.6.4 . Acid–base ladder diagram for HF. To minimize the solubility of $\mathrm { C a F } _ { 2 }$ , we need to keep the pH above 3.17, with more basic pH levels leading to smaller solubility losses. See Chapter 8 for a more detailed discussion.


# Ladder Diagrams for Complexation Equilibria

We can apply the same principles for constructing and interpreting an acid–base ladder diagram to equilibria that involve metal– ligand complexes. For a complexation reaction we define the ladder diagram’s scale using the concentration of uncomplexed, or free ligand, pL. Using the formation of $\mathrm { C d } ( \mathrm { N H } _ { 3 } ) ^ { 2 + }$ as an example 

$$
\mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \mathrm {N H} _ {3} (a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {C d} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) ^ {2 +} (a q)
$$

we can show that $\log K _ { 1 }$ is the dividing line between the areas of predominance for $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ and for $\mathrm { C d } ( \mathrm { N H } _ { 3 } ) ^ { 2 + }$ . 

$$
K _ {1} = 3. 5 5 \times 1 0 ^ {2} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) ^ {2 +} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right]}
$$

$$
\log K _ {1} = \log \left(3. 5 5 \times 1 0 ^ {2}\right) = \log \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) ^ {2 +} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right]} - \log \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right]
$$

$$
\log K _ {1} = 2. 5 5 = \log {\frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) ^ {2 +} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right]}} + \mathrm {p N H} _ {3}
$$

$$
\mathrm {p N H} _ {3} = \log K _ {1} + \log \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) ^ {2 +} \right]} = 2. 5 5 + \log \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) ^ {2 +} \right]}
$$

Thus, $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ is the predominate species when $\mathrm { p N H } _ { 3 }$ is greater than 2.55 (a concentration of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ smaller than $2 . 8 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } \ \mathrm { M } )$ and for a $\mathrm { p N H } _ { 3 }$ value less than 2.55, $\mathrm { C d } ( \mathrm { N H } _ { 3 } ) ^ { 2 + }$ is the predominate species. Figure 6.6.5 shows a complete metal–ligand ladder diagram for $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ that includes additional $\mathrm { C d \mathrm { - N H } _ { 3 } }$ complexes. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/7d6c628e5b788ac8bbc4103e65cd2386b2319ea83dd8a68af08f56ff26c167a3.jpg)



Figure 6.6.5 . Metal–ligand ladder diagram for $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + } { \mathrm { - N H } } _ { 3 }$ complexation reactions. Note that higher‐order complexes form when $\mathrm { p N H } _ { 3 }$ is smaller (which corresponds to larger concentrations of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ ).


#  Example 6.6.3

Draw a single ladder diagram for the $\mathrm { C a } ( \mathrm { E D T A } ) ^ { 2 - }$ and the $\mathrm { M g ( E D T A ) ^ { 2 - } }$ metal–ligand complexes. Use your ladder diagram to predict the result of adding 0.080 moles of $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ to 0.060 moles of $\mathrm { M g ( E D T A ) ^ { 2 - } }$ . EDTA is an abbreviation for the ligand ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid. 

# Solution

Figure 6.6.6 shows the ladder diagram for this system of metal–ligand complexes. Because the predominance regions for $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ and Mg(EDTA) do not overlap, the reaction2‐ 

$$
\mathrm {C a} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \mathrm {M g} (\mathrm {E D T A}) ^ {2 -} (a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {C a} (\mathrm {E D T A}) ^ {2 -} (a q) + \mathrm {M g} ^ {2 +} (a q)
$$

proceeds essentially to completion. Because $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ is the excess reagent, the composition of the final solution is approximately 

$$
\mathrm {m o l e s C a} ^ {2 +} = 0. 0 8 0 - 0. 0 6 0 = 0. 0 2 0 \mathrm {m o l}
$$

$$
\mathrm {m o l e s} \mathrm {C a (E D T A)} ^ {2 -} = 0. 0 6 0 \mathrm {m o l}
$$

$$
\mathrm {m o l e s} \mathrm {M g} ^ {2 +} = 0. 0 6 0 \mathrm {m o l}
$$

$$
\mathrm {m o l e s} \mathrm {M g (E D T A)} ^ {2 -} = 0 \mathrm {m o l}
$$

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/e7ab3da23d5592dcc3f455688430e0f6e89d5ff790cbc0e8abea81ad33df8c63.jpg)



Figure 6.6.6 . Metal–ligand ladder diagram for $\mathrm { C a } ( \mathrm { E D T A } ) ^ { 2 - }$ and for $\mathrm { M g ( E D T A ) ^ { 2 - } }$ . The areas shaded in blue show the pEDTA range where the free metal ions are the predominate species; the metal–ligand complexes are the predominate species in the areas shaded in pink.


The metal–ligand ladder diagram in Figure 6.6.5 uses stepwise formation constants. We also can construct a ladder diagram using cumulative formation constants. For example, the first three stepwise formation constants for the reaction of $Z \mathrm { n } ^ { 2 + }$ with $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ 

$$
\begin{array}{l} Z n ^ {2 +} (a q) + N H _ {3} (a q) \rightleftharpoons Z n \left(N H _ {3}\right) ^ {2 +} (a q) K _ {1} = 1. 6 \times 1 0 ^ {2} \\ Z n \left(N H _ {3}\right) ^ {2 +} (a q) + N H _ {3} (a q) \rightleftharpoons Z n \left(N H _ {3}\right) _ {2} ^ {2 +} (a q) K _ {2} = 1. 9 5 \times 1 0 ^ {2} \\ \mathrm {Z n} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {2} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \mathrm {N H} _ {3} (a q) = \mathrm {Z n} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {3} ^ {2 +} (a q) \quad K _ {3} = 2. 3 \times 1 0 ^ {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

suggests that the formation of $\bf { Z n } ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 3 } ^ { 2 + }$ is more favorable than the formation of $\mathrm { Z n ( N H _ { 3 } ) ^ { 2 + } }$ or $\bf { Z n } ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { 2 + }$ . For this reason, the equilibrium is best represented by the cumulative formation reaction shown here. 

$$
\mathrm {Z n} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 3 \mathrm {N H} _ {3} (a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {Z n} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {3} ^ {2 +} (a q) \quad \beta_ {3} = 7. 2 \times 1 0 ^ {6}
$$

Because $K _ { 3 }$ is greater than $K _ { 2 }$ , which is greater than $K _ { 1 } ,$ , the formation of the metal‐ligand complex $\bf { Z n } ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 3 } ^ { 2 + }$ is more favorable than the formation of the other metal ligand complexes. For this reason, at lower values of $\mathrm { p N H } _ { 3 }$ the concentration of $\bf { Z n } ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 3 } ^ { 2 + }$ is larger than the concentrations of $\mathrm { Z n ( N H _ { 3 } ) ^ { 2 + } }$ or $\mathrm { Z n } ( \mathrm { N H } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { 2 + }$ . The value of $\beta _ { 3 }$ is 

$$
\beta_ {3} = K _ {1} \times K _ {2} \times K _ {3}
$$

To see how we incorporate this cumulative formation constant into a ladder diagram, we begin with the reaction’s equilibrium constant expression. 

$$
\beta_ {3} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {Z n} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {3} ^ {2 +} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {Z n} ^ {2 +} \right] \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right] ^ {3}}
$$

Taking the log of each side 

$$
\log \beta_ {3} = \log \frac {\left[ \mathrm {Z n} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {3} ^ {2 +} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {Z n} ^ {2 +} \right]} - 3 \log \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right]
$$

and rearranging gives 

$$
\mathrm {p N H} _ {3} = \frac {1}{3} \log \beta_ {3} + \frac {1}{3} \log \frac {\left[ \mathrm {Z n} ^ {2 +} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {Z n} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {3} ^ {2 +} \right]}
$$

When the concentrations of Zn and $\bf { Z n } ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 3 } ^ { 2 + }$ are equal, then 

$$
\mathrm {p N H} _ {3} = \frac {1}{3} \log \beta_ {3} = 2. 2 9
$$

In general, for the metal–ligand complex $\mathrm { M L } _ { n } .$ , the step for a cumulative formation constant is 

$$
\mathrm {p L} = \frac {1}{n} \log \beta_ {n}
$$

Figure 6.6.7 shows the complete ladder diagram for the $\mathrm { Z n ^ { 2 + } { - N H _ { 3 } } }$ system. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/92336dc8755eb66e14b5254c536bcae2b8613b7f5f38a99733f0e2d938520396.jpg)



Figure 6.6.7 . Ladder diagram for $\mathrm { Z n ^ { 2 + } { - N H _ { 3 } } }$ metal–ligand complexation reactions showing both a step based on a cumulative formation constant and a step based on a stepwise formation constant.


# Ladder Diagrams for Oxidation/Reduction Equilibria

We also can construct ladder diagrams to help us evaluate redox equilibria. Figure 6.6.8 shows a typical ladder diagram for two half‐reactions in which the scale is the potential, $E$ . 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/4e1bbd5f540ff5f87a89192bc266ae0a673cac50cc28e99311141abf436c9df1.jpg)



Figure 6.6.8 . Redox ladder diagram for $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 3 + } / \mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ and for $\mathrm { S n } ^ { 4 + } / \mathrm { S n } ^ { 2 + }$ . The areas shaded in blue show the potential range where the oxidized forms are the predominate species; the reduced forms are the predominate species in the areas shaded in pink. Note that a more positive potential favors the oxidized form.


The Nernst equation defines the areas of predominance. Using the $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 3 + } / \mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ half‐reaction as an example, we write 

$$
E = E ^ {\circ} - \frac {R T}{n F} \mathrm {l n} \frac {[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} ]}{[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} ]} = 0. 7 7 1 - 0. 0 5 9 1 6 \mathrm {l o g} \frac {[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} ]}{[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} ]}
$$

At a potential more positive than the standard state potential, the predominate species is $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ , whereas $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ predominates at potentials more negative than $E ^ { 0 }$ . When coupled with the step for the $\mathrm { S n } ^ { 4 + } / \mathrm { S n } ^ { 2 + }$ half‐reaction we see that $\mathsf { S n } ^ { 2 + }$ is a useful reducing agent for $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ . If $\mathsf { S } \mathrm { n } ^ { 2 + }$ is in excess, the potential of the resulting solution is near $+ 0 . 1 5 4 \mathrm { V } .$ 

Because the steps on a redox ladder diagram are standard state potentials, a complication arises if solutes other than the oxidizing agent and reducing agent are present at non‐standard state concentrations. For example, the potential for the half‐reaction 

$$
\mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 4 \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + 2 e ^ {-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +} (a q) + 6 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l)
$$

depends on the solution’s pH. To define areas of predominance in this case we begin with the Nernst equation 

$$
E = + 0. 3 2 7 - \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6}{2} \log \frac {[ \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +} ]}{[ \mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} ] [ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} ] ^ {4}}
$$

and factor out the concentration of $\mathrm { H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { O } ^ { + }$ . 

$$
E = + 0. 3 2 7 + \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6}{2} \log \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] ^ {4} - \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6}{2} \log \frac {\left[ \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} \right]}
$$

From this equation we see that the area of predominance for $\mathrm { U O } _ { 2 } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathrm { U } ^ { 4 ^ { + } }$ is defined by a step at a potential where $[ \mathrm { U } ^ { 4 + } ] = [ \mathbf { U } \mathbf { O } _ { 2 } ^ { 2 + } ]$ . 

$$
E = + 0. 3 2 7 + \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6}{2} \log \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] ^ {4} = + 0. 3 2 7 - 0. 1 1 8 3 \mathrm {p H}
$$

Figure 6.6.9 shows how pH affects the step for the $\mathrm { U O _ { 2 } ^ { 2 + } / U ^ { 4 + } }$ half‐reaction. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/23e3e066bdb7ef00172c51fa41de18043f06446bda902c310b40f5dc3e06596a.jpg)



Figure 6.6.9 . Redox ladder diagram for the $\mathrm { \bf U O _ { 2 } ^ { 2 + } / U ^ { 4 + } }$ half‐reaction showing the effect of pH on the step defined by the standard state’s potential.

# 6.7: Solving Equilibrium Problems

Ladder diagrams are a useful tool for evaluating chemical reactivity and for providing a reasonable estimate of a chemical system’s composition at equilibrium. If we need a more exact quantitative description of the equilibrium condition, then a ladder diagram is insufficient; instead, we need to find an algebraic solution. In this section we will learn how to set‐up and solve equilibrium problems. We will start with a simple problem and work toward more complex problems. 

# A Simple Problem: The Solubility of ${ \mathsf { P b } } ( | \boldsymbol { \mathsf { O } } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$

If we place an insoluble compound such as $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ in deionized water, the solid dissolves until the concentrations of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathbf { I O } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ satisfy the solubility product for $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ . At equilibrium the solution is saturated with $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ , which means simply that no more solid can dissolve. How do we determine the equilibrium concentrations of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ , and what is the molar solubility of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ in this saturated solution? 

When we first add solid $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ to water, the concentrations of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ are zero and the reaction quotient, $Q _ { r } ,$ is 

$$
Q _ {r} = \left[ P b ^ {2 +} \right] \left[ I O _ {3} ^ {-} \right] ^ {2} = 0
$$

As the solid dissolves, the concentrations of these ions increase, but $Q _ { r }$ remains smaller than $K _ { \mathrm { s p } }$ . We reach equilibrium and “satisfy the solubility product” when $Q _ { r } = K _ { \mathrm { s p } }$ . 

We begin by writing the equilibrium reaction and the solubility product expression for $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ 

$$
\mathrm {P b} \left(\mathrm {I O} _ {3}\right) _ {2} (s) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {P b} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {I O} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

e 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {s p}} = \left[ \mathrm {P b} ^ {2 +} \right] \left[ \mathrm {I O} _ {3} ^ {-} \right] ^ {2} = 2. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 3} \tag {6.7.1}
$$

As $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ dissolves, two $\mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ ions form for each ion of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ . If we assume that the change in the molar concentration of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ at equilibrium is $\boldsymbol { x }$ , then the change in the molar concentration of $\mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ is $2 x$ . The following table helps us keep track of the initial concentrations, the change in con‐ centrations, and the equilibrium concentrations of $\mathrm { P b 2 + }$ and $\mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . 

<table><tr><td>concentrations</td><td>Pb(IO3)2(s)</td><td>⇌</td><td>Pb2+(aq)</td><td>+</td><td>2IO3-(aq)</td></tr><tr><td>initial</td><td>solid</td><td></td><td>0</td><td></td><td>0</td></tr><tr><td>change</td><td>solid</td><td></td><td>+x</td><td></td><td>+2x</td></tr><tr><td>equilibrium</td><td>solid</td><td></td><td>x</td><td></td><td>2x</td></tr></table>

Because a solid, such as $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ , does not appear in the solubility product expression, we do not need to keep track of its concentration. Remember, however, that the $K _ { \mathrm { s p } }$ value applies only if there is some solid $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ present at equilibrium. 

Substituting the equilibrium concentrations into Equation and solving gives 

$$
(x) (2 x) ^ {2} = 4 x ^ {3} = 2. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 3}
$$

$$
x = 3. 9 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5}
$$

Substituting this value of $x$ back into the equilibrium concentration expressions for $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ gives their concentrations as 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {P b} ^ {2 +} \right] = x = 4. 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \mathrm {M a n d} \left[ \mathrm {I O} _ {3} ^ {-} \right] = 2 x = 7. 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5}
$$

Because one mole of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ contains one mole of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ , the molar solubility of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ is equal to the concentration of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ , or $\mathbf { 4 . 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } M }$ . 

We can express a compound’s solubility in two ways: as its molar solubility (mol/L) or as its mass solubility $\scriptstyle ( { \bf g } / { \bf L } )$ . Be sure to express your answer clearly. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/ba71a0d45d97aad88020df3256d477b4c8f86a74203ac454feb3514cf11c1023.jpg)


# Exercise 6.7.1

Calculate the molar solubility and the mass solubility for $\mathrm { H } \mathbf { g } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C l } _ { 2 }$ , given the following solubility reaction and $K _ { \mathrm { s p } }$ value. 

$$
\mathrm {H g} _ {2} \mathrm {C l} _ {2} (s) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H g} _ {2} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} (a q) \quad K _ {\mathrm {s p}} = 1. 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 8}
$$

# Answer

When $\mathrm { H } \mathbf { g } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C l } _ { 2 }$ dissolves, two $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ are produced for each ion of $\mathbf { H g _ { 2 } ^ { 2 + } }$ . If we assume $x$ is the change in the molar concentration of $\mathbf { H } \mathbf { g } _ { 2 } ^ { 2 + }$ , then the change in the molar concentration of $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ is $2 x$ . The following table helps us keep track of our solution to this problem. 

<table><tr><td>concentration</td><td>Hg2Cl2(s)</td><td>⇌</td><td>Hg2+(aq)</td><td>+</td><td>Cl-(aq)</td></tr><tr><td>initial</td><td>solid</td><td></td><td>0</td><td></td><td>0</td></tr><tr><td>change</td><td>solid</td><td></td><td>+x</td><td></td><td>+2x</td></tr><tr><td>equilibrium</td><td>solid</td><td></td><td>x</td><td></td><td>2x</td></tr></table>

Substituting the equilibrium concentrations into the $K _ { \mathrm { s p } }$ expression for $\cdot \mathrm { H } \mathbf { g } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C l } _ { 2 }$ gives 

$$
\begin{array}{l} K _ {\mathrm {s p}} = \left[ \mathrm {H g} _ {2} ^ {2 +} \right] \left[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \right] ^ {2} = (x) (2 \mathrm {x}) ^ {2} = 4 x ^ {3} = 1. 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 8} \\ x = 6. 6 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \\ \end{array}
$$

Substituting $x$ back into the equilibrium expressions for $\mathbf { H g } _ { 2 } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ gives their concentrations as 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H g} _ {2} ^ {2 +} \right] = x = 6. 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {M} \quad \left[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \right] = 2 x = 1. 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} \mathrm {M}
$$

The molar solubility is equal to $[ \mathbf { H } \mathbf { g } _ { 2 } ^ { 2 + } ]$ , or $\mathbf { 6 . 7 \times 1 0 ^ { - 7 } m o l / L }$ 

# A More Complex Problem: The Common Ion Effect

Calculating the solubility of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ in deionized water is a straightforward problem because the solid’s dissolution is the only source of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . But what if we add $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ to a solution of 0.10 M $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { N O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } \mathrm { ? }$ Before we set‐up and solve this problem algebraically, think about the system’s chemistry and decide whether the solubility of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ will increase, decrease, or remain the same. Beginning a problem by thinking about the likely answer is a good habit to develop. Knowing what answers are reasonable will help you spot errors in your calculations and give you more confidence that your solution to a problem is correct. Because the solution already contains a source of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ , we can use Le Châtelier’s principle to predict that the solubility of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ is smaller than that in our previous problem. 

We begin by setting up a table to help us keep track of the concentrations of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathbf { I O _ { 3 } ^ { - } }$ as this system moves toward and reaches equilibrium. 

<table><tr><td>concentrations</td><td>Pb(IO3)2(s)</td><td>⇌</td><td>Pb2+(aq)</td><td>+</td><td>2IO3-(aq)</td></tr><tr><td>initial</td><td>solid</td><td></td><td>0.10</td><td></td><td>0</td></tr><tr><td>change</td><td>solid</td><td></td><td>+x</td><td></td><td>+2x</td></tr><tr><td>equilibrium</td><td>solid</td><td></td><td>0.10 + x</td><td></td><td>2x</td></tr></table>

Substituting the equilibrium concentrations into Equation 

$$
(0. 1 0 + x) (2 x) ^ {2} = 2. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 3}
$$

and multiplying out the terms on the equation’s left side leaves us with 

$$
4 x ^ {3} + 0. 4 0 x ^ {2} = 2. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 3} \tag {6.7.2}
$$

This is a more difficult equation to solve than that for the solubility of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ in deionized water, and its solution is not immediately obvious. We can find a rigorous solution to Equation using computational software packages and spreadsheets, some of which are described in Chapter 6.10. 

There are several approaches to solving cubic equations, but none are computationally easy using just paper and pencil. 

How might we solve Equation if we do not have access to a computer? One approach is to use our understanding of chemistry to simplify the problem. From Le Châtelier’s principle we know that a large initial concentration of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ will decrease significantly the solubility of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ . One reasonable assumption is that the initial concentration of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ is very close to its equilibrium concentration. If this assumption is correct, then the following approximation is reasonable 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {P b} ^ {2 +} \right] = 0. 1 0 + x \approx 0. 1 0
$$

Substituting this approximation into Equation and solving for $x$ gives 

$$
\begin{array}{l} (0. 1 0) (2 x) ^ {2} = 0. 4 x ^ {2} = 2. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 3} \\ x = 7. 9 1 \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \\ \end{array}
$$

Before we accept this answer, we must verify that our approximation is reasonable. The difference between the actual concentration of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ , which is $0 . 1 0 + x \textbf { M }$ , and our assumption that the concentration of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ is $0 . 1 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ }$ is ${ \bf 7 . 9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 7 } }$ , or $7 . 9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 } ~ \%$ of the assumed concentration. This is a negligible error. If we accept the result of our calculation, we find that the equilibrium concentrations of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ are 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {P b} ^ {2 +} \right] = 0. 1 0 + x \approx 0. 1 0 \mathrm {M} \text {a n d} \left[ \mathrm {I O} _ {3} ^ {-} \right] = 2 x = 1. 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} \mathrm {M}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \% \text {error} = \frac {\text {actual} - \text {assumed}}{\text {assumed}} \times 100 \\ = \frac {(0 . 1 0 + x) - 0 . 1 0}{0 . 1 0} \times 1 0 0 \\ = \frac {7 . 9 1 \times 1 0 ^ {- 7}}{0 . 1 0} \times 1 0 0 \\ = 7.91 \times 10^{-4 \%} \\ \end{array}
$$

The molar solubility of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ is equal to the additional concentration of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ in solution, or $\mathbf { 7 . 9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 } } \mathrm { m o l / L }$ . As expected, we find that $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ is less soluble in the presence of a solution that already contains one of its ions. This is known as the common ion effect. 

As outlined in the following example, if an approximation leads to an error that is unacceptably large, then we can extend the process of making and evaluating approximations. 

One “rule of thumb” when making an approximation is that it should not introduce an error of more than $\pm 5 \%$ . Although this is not an unreasonable choice, what matters is that the error makes sense within the context of the problem you are solving. 

#  Example 6.7.1

Calculate the solubility of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ in $1 . 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 }$ M $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { N O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ . 

Solution 

If we let $\boldsymbol { x }$ equal the change in the concentration of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ , then the equilibrium concentrations of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ are 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {P b} ^ {2 +} \right] = 1. 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} + x \text {a n d} \left[ \mathrm {I O} _ {3} ^ {-} \right] = 2 x
$$

Substituting these concentrations into Equation leaves us with 

$$
\left(1. 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} + x\right) (2 x) ^ {2} = 2. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 3}
$$

To solve this equation for $\boldsymbol { \chi }$ , let’s make the following assumption 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {P b} ^ {2 +} \right] = 1. 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} + x \approx 1. 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {M}
$$

Solving for $x$ gives its value as $\mathbf { 2 . 5 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } }$ ; however, when we substitute this value for $\boldsymbol { x }$ back, we find that the calculated concentration of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ at equilibrium 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {P b} ^ {2 +} \right] = 1. 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} + x = 1. 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} + 2. 5 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} = 1. 2 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {M}
$$

is $2 5 \%$ greater than our assumption of $\mathbf { 1 . 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 } M }$ . This error is unreasonably large. 

Rather than shouting in frustration, let’s make a new assumption. Our first assumption—that the concentration of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ is $\times 1 0 ^ { - 4 }$ M—was too small. The calculated concentration of $\mathbf { 1 . 2 5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { ~ M ~ } }$ , therefore, probably is a too large, but closer to the correct concentration than was our first assumption. For our second approximation, let’s assume that 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {P b} ^ {2 +} \right] = 1. 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} + x = 1. 2 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {M}
$$

Substituting into Equation and solving for $x$ gives its value as $\mathbf { 2 . 2 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } }$ . The resulting concentration of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ is 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {P b} ^ {2 +} \right] = 1. 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} + 2. 2 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} = 1. 2 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {M}
$$

which differs from our assumption of $\mathbf { 1 . 2 5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 } ~ M }$ by $2 . 4 \%$ . Because the original concentration of $\mathrm { P b } ^ { 2 + }$ is given to two significant figure, this is a more reasonable error. Our final solution, to two significant figures, is 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {P b} ^ {2 +} \right] = 1. 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {M} \text {a n d} \left[ \mathrm {I O} _ {3} \right] = 4. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \mathrm {M}
$$

and the molar solubility of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ is $\mathbf { 2 . 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } \ m o l / L }$ . This iterative approach to solving the problems is known as the method of successive approximations. 

#  Exercise 6.7.2

Calculate the molar solubility for $\mathrm { H g } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C l } _ { 2 }$ in 0.10 M NaCl and compare your answer to its molar solubility in deionized water (see Exercise 6.7.1 ). 

# Answer

We begin by setting up a table to help us keep track of the concentrations $\mathbf { H g _ { 2 } ^ { 2 + } }$ and $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ as this system moves toward and reaches equilibrium. 

<table><tr><td>concentration</td><td>Hg2Cl2(s)</td><td>⇌</td><td>Hg2+ (aq)</td><td>+</td><td>Cl-(aq)</td></tr><tr><td>initial</td><td>solid</td><td></td><td>0</td><td></td><td>0.10</td></tr><tr><td>change</td><td>solid</td><td></td><td>+x</td><td></td><td>+2x</td></tr><tr><td>equilibrium</td><td>solid</td><td></td><td>x</td><td></td><td>0.1 + 2x</td></tr></table>

Substituting the equilibrium concentrations into the $K _ { \mathrm { s p } }$ expression for $\mathrm { H } \mathbf { g } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C l } _ { 2 }$ leaves us with a difficult to solve cubic equation. 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {s p}} = \left[ \mathrm {H g} _ {2} ^ {2 +} \right] \left[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \right] ^ {2} = (x) (0. 1 0 + 2 x) ^ {2} = 4 x ^ {3} + 0. 4 0 x ^ {2} + 0. 0 1 0 x
$$

Let’s make an assumption to simplify this problem. Because we expect the value of $x$ to be small, let’s assume that 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \right] = 0. 1 0 + 2 x \approx 0. 1 0
$$

This simplifies our problem to 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {s p}} = \left[ \mathrm {H g} _ {2} ^ {2 +} \right] \left[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \right] ^ {2} = (x) (0. 1 0) ^ {2} = 0. 0 1 0 x = 1. 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 8}
$$

which gives the value of $\boldsymbol { x }$ as $\mathbf { 1 . 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 6 } M }$ . The difference between the actual concentration of $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ , which is $( 0 . 1 0 + 2 x )$ M, and our assumption that it is $0 . 1 0 \mathbf { M }$ introduces an error of $\mathbf { 2 . 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 3 } \% }$ . This is a negligible error. The molar solubility of $\mathrm { H } \mathbf { g } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C l } _ { 2 }$ is the same as the concentration of $\mathbf { H g } _ { 2 } ^ { 2 + }$ , or $\mathbf { 1 . 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 6 } \mathrm { ~ M ~ } }$ . As expected, the molar solubility in 0.10 M NaCl is less than $6 . 7 \times 1 0 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m o l / L }$ , which is its solubility in water (see solution to Exercise 6.7.1 ). 

# A Systematic Approach to Solving Equilibrium Problems

Calculating the solubility of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ in a solution of $\mathrm { P b } ( \mathrm { N O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ is more complicated than calculating its solubility in deionized water. The calculation, however, is still relatively easy to organize and the simplifying assumptions are fairly obvious. This problem is reasonably straightforward because it involves only one equilibrium reaction and one equilibrium constant. 

Determining the equilibrium composition of a system with multiple equilibrium reactions is more complicated. In this section we introduce a systematic approach to setting‐up and solving equilibrium problems. As shown in Table 6.7.1 , this approach involves four steps. 


Table 6.7.1 . Systematic Approach to Solving Equilibrium Problems


<table><tr><td>Step 1
Write all relevant equilibrium reactions and equilibrium constant expressions.</td></tr><tr><td>Step 2
Count the unique species that appear in the equilibrium constant expressions; these are your unknowns. You have enough information to solve the problem if the number of unknowns equals the number of equilibrium constant expressions. If not, add a mass balance equation and/or a charge balance equation. Continue adding equations until the number of equations equals the number of unknowns.</td></tr><tr><td>Step 3
Combine your equations and solve for one unknown. Whenever possible, simplify the algebra by making appropriate assumptions. If you make an assumption, set a limit for its error. This decision influences your evaluation of the assumption.</td></tr><tr><td>Step 4
Check your assumptions. If any assumption proves invalid, return to the previous step and continue solving. The problem is complete when you have an answer that does not violate any of your assumptions.</td></tr></table>

In addition to equilibrium constant expressions, two other equations are important to this systematic approach to solving an equilibrium problem. The first of these equations is a mass balance equation, which simply is a statement that matter is conserved during a chemical reaction. In a solution of acetic acid, for example, the combined concentrations of the conjugate weak acid, $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O H }$ , and the conjugate weak base, $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O ^ { - } }$ , must equal acetic acid’s initial concentration, $C _ { \mathsf { C E _ { 3 } C O O E } }$ . 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O H}} = \left[ \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O H} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O} ^ {-} \right]
$$

You may recall from Chapter 2 that this is the difference between a formal concentration and a molar concentration. The variable $C$ represents a formal concentration. 

The second equation is a charge balance equation, which requires that the total positive charge from the cations equal the total negative charge from the anions. Mathematically, the charge balance equation is 

$$
\sum_ {i = 1} ^ {n} \left(z ^ {+}\right) _ {i} \left[ C ^ {z +} \right] _ {i} = - \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {m} \left(z ^ {-}\right) _ {j} \left[ A ^ {z -} \right] _ {j}
$$

where $[ C ^ { z + } ] _ { i }$ and $[ A ^ { z - } ] _ { j }$ are, respectively, the concentrations of the $i ^ { \mathrm { { t h } } }$ cation and the $j ^ { \mathrm { t h } }$ anion, and $( z ^ { + } ) _ { i }$ and $( z ^ { - } ) _ { j }$ are the charges for the $i ^ { \mathrm { { t h } } }$ cation and the $j ^ { \mathrm { t h } }$ anion. Every ion in solution, even if it does not appear in an equilibrium reaction, must appear in the charge 

balance equation. For example, the charge balance equation for an aqueous solution of $\mathrm { C a } ( \mathrm { N O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ is 

$$
2 \times \left[ \mathrm {C a} ^ {2 +} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] = \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {+} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {N O} _ {3} ^ {-} \right]
$$

Note that we multiply the concentration of $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ by two and that we include the concentrations of $\mathrm { H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { O } ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { O H ^ { - } }$ 

A charge balance is a conservation of a charge. The minus sign in front of the summation term on the right side of the charge balance equation ensures that both summations are positive. There are situations where it is impossible to write a charge balance equation because we do not have enough information about the solution’s composition. For example, suppose we fix a solution’s pH using a buffer. If the buffer’s composition is not specified, then we cannot write a charge balance equation. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/0bd7cefe00ef27e9cbd80fd1dacd975c0d1be788ae7a3af12ad3b1a2a117e54f.jpg)


# Example 6.7.2

Write mass balance equations and a charge balance equation for a 0.10 M solution of ${ \mathrm { N a H C O } } _ { 3 }$ 

# Solution

It is easier to keep track of the species in solution if we write down the reactions that define the solution’s composition. These reactions are the dissolution of a soluble salt 

$$
\mathrm {N a H C O} _ {3} (s) \rightarrow \mathrm {N a} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {H C O} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

and the acid–base dissociation reactions of $\mathbf { H C O _ { 3 } ^ { - } }$ and $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O }$ 

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {H C O} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {C O} _ {3} ^ {2 -} (a q) \\ \mathrm {H C O} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {C O} _ {3} (a q) \\ 2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q) \\ \end{array}
$$

The mass balance equations are 

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0. 1 0 \mathrm {M} = \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {C O} _ {3} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H C O} _ {3} ^ {-} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {C O} _ {3} ^ {2 -} \right] \\ 0. 1 0 \mathrm {M} = \left[ \mathrm {N a} ^ {+} \right] \\ \end{array}
$$

and the charge balance equation is 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {N a} ^ {+} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] = \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H C O} _ {3} ^ {-} \right] + 2 \times \left[ \mathrm {C O} _ {3} ^ {2 -} \right]
$$

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/f3959171b55d682d09cd95f266a8486647d1393f359f48c05c657f6425ecac88.jpg)


# Exercise 6.7.3

Write appropriate mass balance and charge balance equations for a solution containing 0.10 M $\mathrm { K H _ { 2 } P O _ { 4 } }$ and $0 . 0 5 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ }$ ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { H P O } } _ { 4 }$ . 

# Answer

To help us determine what ions are in solution, let’s write down all the reaction needed to prepare the solutions and the equilibrium reactions that take place within these solutions. These reactions are the dissolution of two soluble salts 

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {K H} _ {2} \mathrm {P O} _ {4} (s) \longrightarrow \mathrm {K} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {P O} _ {4} ^ {-} (a q) \\ \mathrm {N a H P O} _ {4} (s) \longrightarrow \mathrm {N a} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {H P O} _ {4} ^ {2 -} (a q) \\ \end{array}
$$

and the acid–base dissociation reactions for $\mathbf { H _ { 2 } P O _ { 4 } ^ { - } }$ , $\mathbf { H P O _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - } }$ . and $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O }$ . 

$$
\mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {P O} _ {4} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {H P O} _ {4} ^ {2 -} (a q)
$$

$$
\mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {P O} _ {4} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {P O} _ {4} (a q)
$$

$$
\mathrm {H P O} _ {4} ^ {2 -} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {P O} _ {4} ^ {3 -} (a q)
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

Note that we did not include the base dissociation reaction for $\mathbf { H P O _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - } }$ because we already accounted for its product, $\mathbf { H _ { 2 } P O _ { 4 } ^ { - } }$ , in another reaction. The mass balance equations for $\mathrm { K } ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { N a ^ { + } }$ are straightforward 

$$
[ \mathrm {K} ^ {+} ] = 0. 1 0 \mathrm {M} \text {a n d} [ \mathrm {N a} ^ {+} ] = 0. 1 0 \mathrm {M}
$$

but the mass balance equation for phosphate takes a bit more thought. Both $\mathbf { H _ { 2 } P O _ { 4 } ^ { - } }$ and $\mathbf { H P O _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - } }$ produce the same ions in solution. We can, therefore, imagine that the solution initially contains 0.15 M ${ \mathrm { K H } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { P O } } _ { 4 }$ , which gives the following mass balance equation. 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {P O} _ {4} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {P O} _ {4} ^ {-} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H P O} _ {4} ^ {2 -} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {P O} _ {4} ^ {3 -} \right] = 0. 1 5 \mathrm {M}
$$

The charge balance equation is 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {K} ^ {+} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {N a} ^ {+} \right] = \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {P O} _ {4} ^ {-} \right] + 2 \times \left[ \mathrm {H P O} _ {4} ^ {2 -} \right] + 3 \times \left[ \mathrm {P O} _ {4} ^ {3 -} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right]
$$

# pH of a Monoprotic Weak Acid

To illustrate the systematic approach to solving equilibrium problems, let’s calculate the pH of 1.0 M HF. Two equilibrium reactions affect the pH. The first, and most obvious, is the acid dissociation reaction for HF 

$$
\mathrm {H F} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {F} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

for which the equilibrium constant expression is 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {a}} = \frac {[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} ] [ \mathrm {F} ^ {-} ]}{[ \mathrm {H F} ]} = 6. 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \tag {6.7.3}
$$

The second equilibrium reaction is the dissociation of water, which is an obvious yet easily neglected reaction 

$$
2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

$$
K _ {w} = \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right] = 1. 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 4} \tag {6.7.4}
$$

Counting unknowns, we find four: [HF], [F ], – $[ \mathrm { H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { O } ^ { + } ] ,$ , and $[ \mathrm { O H ^ { - } } ]$ . To solve this problem we need two additional equations. These equations are a mass balance equation on hydrofluoric acid 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {H F}} = \left[ \mathrm {H F} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {F} ^ {-} \right] = 1. 0 \mathrm {M} \tag {6.7.5}
$$

and a charge balance equation 

$$
\left[ \mathbf {H} _ {3} \mathbf {O} ^ {+} \right] = \left[ \mathbf {O H} ^ {-} \right] + \left[ \mathbf {F} ^ {-} \right] \tag {6.7.6}
$$

With four equations and four unknowns, we are ready to solve the problem. Before doing so, let’s simplify the algebra by making two assumptions. 

Assumption One. Because HF is a weak acid, we know that the solution is acidic. For an acidic solution it is reasonable to assume that 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] > > \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right]
$$

which simplifies the charge balance equation to 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] = \left[ \mathrm {F} ^ {-} \right] \tag {6.7.7}
$$

Assumption Two. Because HF is a weak acid, very little of it dissociates to form $\mathrm { F ^ { - } }$ . Most of the HF remains in its conjugate weak acid form and it is reasonable to assume that 

$$
[ \mathbf {H F} ] > > [ \mathbf {F} ^ {-} ]
$$

which simplifies the mass balance equation to 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {H F}} = [ \mathrm {H F} ] = 1. 0 \mathrm {M} \tag {6.7.8}
$$

For this exercise let’s accept an assumption if it introduces an error of less than $\pm 5 \%$ . 

Substituting Equation and Equation into Equation , and solving for the concentration of $\mathrm { H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { O } ^ { + }$ gives us 

$$
\mathrm {K _ {a}} = \frac {[ \mathrm {H _ {3} O ^ {+}} ] [ \mathrm {F ^ {-}} ]}{[ \mathrm {H F} ]} = \frac {[ \mathrm {H _ {3} O ^ {+}} ] [ \mathrm {H _ {3} O ^ {+}} ]}{\mathrm {C _ {H F}}} = \frac {[ \mathrm {H _ {3} O ^ {+}} ] ^ {2}}{\mathrm {C _ {H F}}} = 6. 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}
$$

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] = \sqrt {K _ {\mathrm {a}} C _ {\mathrm {H F}}} = \sqrt {\left(6 . 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}\right) (1 . 0)} = 2. 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2}
$$

Before accepting this answer, we must verify our assumptions. The first assumption is that $[ \mathrm { O H ^ { - } } ]$ is significantly smaller than $\mathrm { [ H _ { 3 } O ^ { + } ] }$ . Using Equation , we find that 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right] = \frac {K _ {\mathrm {w}}}{\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right]} = \frac {1 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 4}}{2 . 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2}} = 3. 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 3}
$$

Clearly this assumption is acceptable. The second assumption is that $[ \mathrm { F ^ { - } } ]$ is significantly smaller than [HF]. From Equation we have 

$$
\left[ \mathbf {F} ^ {-} \right] = 2. 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathbf {M}
$$

Because $[ \mathrm { F ^ { - } } ]$ is $2 . 6 0 \%$ of $C _ { \mathrm { H F } }$ , this assumption also is acceptable. Given that $\mathrm { [ H _ { 3 } O ^ { + } ] }$ is $\mathbf { 2 . 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 2 } M }$ , the pH of 1.0 M HF is 1.59. 

How does the calculation change if we require that the error introduced in our assumptions be less than $\pm 1 \% 2$ In this case we no longer can assume that $[ \mathrm { H F } ] > > [ \mathrm { F } ^ { - } ]$ and we cannot simplify the mass balance equation. Solving the mass balance equation for [HF] 

$$
[ \mathrm {H F} ] = C _ {\mathrm {H F}} - [ \mathrm {F} ^ {-} ] = C _ {\mathrm {H F}} - [ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} ]
$$

and substituting into the $K _ { \mathfrak { a } }$ expression along with Equation gives 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {a}} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] ^ {2}}{C _ {\mathrm {H F}} - \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right]}
$$

Rearranging this equation leaves us with a quadratic equation 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] ^ {2} + K _ {\mathrm {a}} \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] - K _ {\mathrm {a}} C _ {\mathrm {H F}} = 0
$$

which we solve using the quadratic formula 

$$
x = \frac {- b \pm \sqrt {b ^ {2} - 4 a c}}{2 a}
$$

where a, b, and $c$ are the coefficients in the quadratic equation 

$$
a x ^ {2} + b x + c = 0
$$

Solving a quadratic equation gives two roots, only one of which has chemical significance. For our problem, the equation’s roots are 

$$
x = \frac {- 6 . 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \pm \sqrt {\left(6 . 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}\right) ^ {2} - (4) (1) \left(- 6 . 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}\right)}}{(2) (1)}
$$

$$
x = \frac {- 6 . 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \pm 5 . 2 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2}}{2}
$$

$$
x = 2. 5 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \text {o r} - 2. 6 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2}
$$

Only the positive root is chemically significant because the negative root gives a negative concentration for $\mathrm { H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { O } ^ { + }$ . Thus, $\mathrm { [ H _ { 3 } O ^ { + } ] }$ is $\mathbf { 2 . 5 7 \times 1 0 ^ { - 2 } M }$ and the pH is 1.59. 

You can extend this approach to calculating the $\mathrm { p H }$ of a monoprotic weak base by replacing $K _ { \mathfrak { a } }$ with $K _ { \mathrm { b } }$ , replacing $C _ { \mathrm { H F } }$ with the weak base’s concentration, and solving for $[ \mathrm { O H ^ { - } } ]$ in place of $\mathrm { [ H _ { 3 } O ^ { + } ] }$ . 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/4c6c2f4d730ec213781dc1fbdb86f3988e9ef00b1243aa4fc72dfaae4d1966c4.jpg)


# Exercise 6.7.4

Calculate the pH of $0 . 0 5 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ N H _ { 3 } }$ . State any assumptions you make in solving the problem, limiting the error for any assumption to $\pm 5 \%$ . The $K _ { \mathrm { b } }$ value for $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ is $\mathbf { 1 . 7 5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } }$ . 

# Answer

To determine the pH of 0.050 M $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ , we need to consider two equilibrium reactions: the base dissociation reaction for $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ 

$$
\mathrm {N H} _ {3} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} (a q)
$$

and water’s dissociation reaction. 

$$
2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

These two reactions contain four species whose concentrations we need to consider: $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ , $\mathrm { N H } _ { 4 } ^ { + }$ , $\mathrm { H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { O } ^ { + }$ , and $\mathrm { O H ^ { - } }$ . We need four equations to solve the problem—these equations are the $K _ { \mathrm { b } }$ equation for $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {b}} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right]} = 1. 7 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5}
$$

the $K _ { \mathrm { w } }$ equation for $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O }$ 

$$
K _ {w} = \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right]
$$

a mass balance equation on ammonia 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {N H} _ {3}} = 0. 0 5 0 \mathrm {M} = \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} \right]
$$

and a charge balance equation 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} \right] = \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right]
$$

To solve this problem, we will make two assumptions. Because $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ is a base, our first assumption is 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right] > > \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right]
$$

which simplifies the charge balance equation to 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} \right] = \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right]
$$

Because $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ is a weak base, our second assumption is 

$$
[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} ] > > [ \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} ]
$$

which simplifies the mass balance equation to 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {N H} _ {3}} = 0. 0 5 0 \mathrm {M} = [ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} ]
$$

Substituting the simplified charge balance equation and mass balance equation into the $K _ { \mathrm { b } }$ equation leave us with 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {b}} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right]} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right] \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right]}{C _ {\mathrm {N H} _ {3}}} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right] ^ {2}}{C _ {\mathrm {N H} _ {3}}} = 1. 7 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5}
$$

$$
\left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right] = \sqrt {K _ {\mathrm {b}} C _ {\mathrm {N H} _ {3}}} = \sqrt {\left(1 . 7 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5}\right) (0 . 0 5 0)} = 9. 3 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}
$$

Before we accept this answer, we must verify our two assumptions. The first assumption is that the concentration of $\mathrm { O H ^ { - } }$ is significantly greater than the concentration of $\mathrm { H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { O } ^ { + }$ . Using $K _ { \mathrm { w } }$ , we find that 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] = \frac {K _ {\mathrm {w}}}{\left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right]} = \frac {1 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 4}}{9 . 3 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}} = 1. 0 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 1}
$$

Clearly this assumption is acceptable. Our second assumption is that the concentration of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ is significantly greater than the concentration of $\mathbf { N H } _ { 4 } ^ { + }$ . Using our simplified charge balance equation, we find that 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} \right] = \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right] = 9. 3 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}
$$

Because the concentration of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 4 } ^ { + }$ is $1 . 9 \%$ of $C _ { \overline { { \mathbf { N H } } } _ { 3 } }$ , our second assumption also is reasonable. Given that $\mathrm { [ H _ { 3 } O ^ { + } ] }$ is $\bf { \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 1 } }$ , the pH is 10.97. 

# pH of a Polyprotic Acid or Base

A more challenging problem is to find the pH of a solution that contains a polyprotic weak acid or one of its conjugate species. As an example, consider the amino acid alanine, whose structure is shown in Figure 6.7.1 . The ladder diagram in Figure 6.7.2 shows alanine’s three acid–base forms and their respective areas of predominance. For simplicity, we identify these species as $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { L } ^ { + }$ , HL, and $\mathrm { L } ^ { - }$ . 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/ba48e9ff8ff21fced60705be91eddefd473f3b66dd1a32955a1db9ad8ccdcd17.jpg)



Figure 6.7.1 . Structure of the amino acid alanine, which has $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ values of 2.348 (–COOH) and 9.867 $\left( \mathrm { - N H } _ { 2 } \right)$ .


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/abac3f91cc4737766c357c9eaad3c94d92437e5820385e8e4f9673ace501d19e.jpg)



Figure 6.7.2 . Ladder diagram for alanine.


# pH of 0.10 M Alanine Hydrochloride $( H _ { 2 } L ^ { + } )$

Alanine hydrochloride is the salt of the diprotic weak acid $\mathrm { H _ { 2 } L ^ { + } }$ and $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ . Because $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { L } ^ { + }$ has two acid dissociation reactions, a complete systematic solution to this problem is more complicated than that for a monoprotic weak acid. The ladder diagram in Figure 6.7.2 helps us simplify the problem. Because the areas of predominance for $\mathrm { H _ { 2 } L ^ { + } }$ and $\mathrm { L } ^ { - }$ are so far apart, we can assume that a solution of $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { L } ^ { + }$ will not contain a significant amount of $\mathrm { L } ^ { - }$ . As a result, we can treat $\mathrm { H _ { 2 } L ^ { + } }$ as though it is a monoprotic weak acid. Calculating the pH of $0 . 1 0 \mathbf { M }$ alanine hydrochloride, which is 1.72, is left to the reader as an exercise. 

# pH of 0.10 M Sodium Alaninate $( \mathsf { L } ^ { - } )$

The alaninate ion is a diprotic weak base. Because $\mathrm { L } ^ { - }$ has two base dissociation reactions, a complete systematic solution to this problem is more complicated than that for a monoprotic weak base. Once again, the ladder diagram in Figure 6.7.2 helps us simplify the problem. Because the areas of predominance for $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { L } ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { L } ^ { - }$ are so far apart, we can assume that a solution of $\mathrm { L } ^ { - }$ will not contain a significant amount of $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { L } ^ { + }$ . As a result, we can treat $\mathrm { L } ^ { - }$ as though it is a monoprotic weak base. Calculating the pH of 0.10 M sodium alaninate, which is 11.42, is left to the reader as an exercise. 

# pH of 0.10 M Alanine (HL)

Finding the pH of a solution of alanine is more complicated than our previous two examples because we cannot ignore the presence of either $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { L } ^ { + }$ or $\mathrm { L } ^ { - }$ . To calculate the solution’s pH we must consider alanine’s acid dissociation reaction 

$$
\mathrm {H L} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {L} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

and its base dissociation reaction 

$$
\mathrm {H L} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {L} ^ {+} (a q)
$$

and, as always, we must also consider the dissociation of water 

$$
2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

This leaves us with five unknowns— $\mathrm { [ H _ { 2 } L ^ { + } ] }$ , [HL], $[ \mathrm { L } ^ { - } ]$ , $\mathrm { [ H _ { 3 } O ^ { + } ] }$ , and $[ \mathrm { O H ^ { - } } ] .$ —for which we need five equations. These equations are $K _ { \mathsf { a } 2 }$ and $K _ { \mathrm { b } 2 }$ for alanine 

$$
\begin{array}{l} K _ {\mathrm {a} 2} = \frac {[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} ] [ \mathrm {L} ^ {-} ]}{[ \mathrm {H L} ]} \\ K _ {\mathrm {b} 2} = \frac {K _ {\mathrm {w}}}{K _ {\mathrm {a} 1}} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right] \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {L} ^ {+} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {H L} \right]} \\ \end{array}
$$

the $K _ { \mathrm { w } }$ equation 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {w}} = \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right]
$$

a mass balance equation for alanine 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {H L}} = \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {L} ^ {+} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H L} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {L} ^ {-} \right]
$$

and a charge balance equation 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {L} ^ {+} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] = \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {L} ^ {-} \right]
$$

Because HL is a weak acid and a weak base, it seems reasonable to assume that little of it will dissociate and that 

$$
[ \mathrm {H L} ] > > [ \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {L} ^ {+} ] + [ \mathrm {L} ^ {-} ]
$$

which allows us to simplify the mass balance equation to 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {H L}} = [ \mathrm {H L} ]
$$

Next we solve $K _ { \mathrm { b } 2 }$ for $\mathrm { [ H _ { 2 } L ^ { + } ] }$ 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {L} ^ {+} \right] = \frac {K _ {\mathrm {w}} [ \mathrm {H L} ]}{K _ {\mathrm {a l}} \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right]} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {H L} \right]}{K _ {\mathrm {a l}}} = \frac {C _ {\mathrm {H L}} \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right]}{K _ {\mathrm {a l}}}
$$

and solve $K _ { \mathsf { a } 2 }$ for [L ]– 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {L} ^ {-} \right] = \frac {K _ {a 2} [ \mathrm {H L} ]}{\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right]} = \frac {K _ {a 2} C _ {\mathrm {H L}}}{\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right]}
$$

Substituting these equations for $\mathrm { [ H _ { 2 } L ^ { + } ] }$ and $[ \mathrm { L ^ { - } } ] _ { : }$ , and the equation for $K _ { \mathrm { w } } ,$ into the charge balance equation give us 

$$
\frac {C _ {\mathrm {H L}} \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right]}{K _ {\mathrm {a l}}} + \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] = \frac {K _ {\mathrm {w}}}{\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right]} + \frac {K _ {a 2} C _ {\mathrm {H L}}}{\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right]}
$$

which we simplify to 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] \left(\frac {C _ {\mathrm {H L}}}{K _ {\mathrm {a l}}} + 1\right) = \frac {1}{\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right]} \left(K _ {\mathrm {w}} + K _ {a 2} C _ {\mathrm {H L}}\right)
$$

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] ^ {2} = \frac {\left(K _ {\mathrm {a} 2} C _ {\mathrm {H L}} + K _ {\mathrm {w}}\right)}{\frac {C _ {\mathrm {H L}}}{K _ {\mathrm {a l}}} + 1} = \frac {K _ {\mathrm {a l}} \left(K _ {\mathrm {a} 2} C _ {\mathrm {H L}} + K _ {\mathrm {w}}\right)}{C _ {\mathrm {H L}} + K _ {\mathrm {a l}}}
$$

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] = \sqrt {\frac {\left(K _ {\mathrm {a l}} K _ {\mathrm {a 2}} C _ {\mathrm {H L}} + K _ {\mathrm {a l}} K _ {\mathrm {w}}\right)}{C _ {\mathrm {H L}} + K _ {\mathrm {a l}}}}
$$

We can further simplify this equation if $K _ { \mathrm { a 1 } } K _ { \mathrm { w } } < < K _ { \mathrm { a 1 } } K _ { \mathrm { a 2 } } C _ { \mathrm { H L } } ,$ and if $K _ { \mathrm { a 1 } } < < C _ { \mathrm { H L } }$ , leaving us with 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] = \sqrt {K _ {\mathrm {a 1}} K _ {\mathrm {a 2}}}
$$

For a solution of 0.10 M alanine the $\mathrm { [ H _ { 3 } O ^ { + } ] }$ is 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] = \sqrt {\left(4 . 4 8 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3}\right) \left(1 . 3 5 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 0}\right)} = 7. 8 0 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {M}
$$

or a pH of 6.11. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/bfa71fefedb1528838447b1aacb7a4ccf558cba3493553c32ccaa97712a24754.jpg)


# Exercise 6.7.5

Verify that each assumption in our solution for the $\mathrm { p H }$ of $0 . 1 0 \textbf { M }$ alanine is reasonable, using $\pm 5 \%$ as the limit for the acceptable error. 

# Answer

In solving for the pH of $0 . 1 0 \mathrm { M }$ alanine, we made the following three assumptions: (a) $[ \mathrm { H L } ] > > [ \mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { L } ^ { + } ] + [ \mathrm { L } ^ { - } ]$ ; (b) $K _ { \mathrm { a 1 } } K _ { \mathrm { w } }$ ${ < < K _ { \mathrm { a 1 } } K _ { \mathrm { a 2 } } C _ { \mathrm { H L } } }$ ; and (c) $K _ { \mathrm { a 1 } } < < C _ { \mathrm { H L } }$ . Assumptions (b) and (c) are easy to check. The value of $K _ { \mathrm { a 1 } }$ $( 4 . 4 8 7 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } )$ is $4 . 5 \%$ of $C _ { \mathrm { H L } }$ (0.10), and $K _ { \mathrm { a 1 } } K \mathbf { w }$ $( 4 . 4 8 7 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 7 } )$ ) is $0 . 0 7 4 \%$ of $K _ { \mathrm { a 1 } } K _ { \mathrm { a 2 } } C _ { \mathrm { H L } }$ $\mathbf { 6 . 0 9 3 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 4 } }$ ). Each of these assumptions introduces an error of less than $\pm 5 \%$ . 

To test assumption (a) we need to calculate the concentrations of $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { L } ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { L } ^ { - }$ , which we accomplish using the equations for $K _ { \mathrm { a 1 } }$ and $K _ { \mathrm { a 2 } }$ . 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {L} ^ {+} \right] = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] [ \mathrm {H L} ]}{K _ {\mathrm {a l}}} = \frac {\left(7 . 8 0 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 7}\right) (0 . 1 0)}{4 . 4 8 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3}} = 1. 7 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5}
$$

$$
\left[ \mathrm {L} ^ {-} \right] = \frac {K _ {a 2} [ \mathrm {H L} ]}{\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right]} = \frac {\left(1 . 3 5 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 0}\right) (0 . 1 0)}{7 . 8 0 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 7}} = 1. 7 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5}
$$

Because these concentrations are less than $\pm 5 \%$ of CHL, the first assumption also is acceptable. 

# Effect of Complexation on Solubility

One method for increasing a precipitate’s solubility is to add a ligand that forms soluble complexes with one of the precipitate’s ions. For example, the solubility of AgI increases in the presence of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ due to the formation of the soluble $\mathbf { A g ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } }$ complex. As a final illustration of the systematic approach to solving equilibrium problems, let’s calculate the molar solubility of AgI in $0 . 1 0 \mathbf { M }$ $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ . 

We begin by writing the relevant equilibrium reactions, which includes the solubility of AgI, the acid–base chemistry of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ and $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O }$ , and the metal‐ligand complexation chemistry between ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ . 

$$
\begin{array}{l} \operatorname {A g} \mathrm {I} (s) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q) \\ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} (a q) \\ 2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q) \\ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {N H} _ {3} (a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {A g} (\mathrm {N H} _ {3}) _ {2} ^ {+} (a q) \\ \end{array}
$$

This leaves us with seven unknowns— $\cdot [ \mathrm { A g } ^ { + } ]$ , [I ],  – $\left[ \mathrm { N H } _ { 3 } \right]$ , [ ], [OH ], – $\mathrm { [ H _ { 3 } O ^ { + } ] }$ , and $[ \mathbf { A g ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } } ] .$ —and a need for seven equations. Four of the equations we need to solve this problem are the equilibrium constant expressions 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {s p}} = \left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {I} ^ {-} \right] = 8. 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 7} \tag {6.7.9}
$$

$$
K _ {\mathrm {b}} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right]} = 1. 7 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \tag {6.7.10}
$$

$$
K _ {\mathrm {w}} = \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right] = 1. 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 4} \tag {6.7.11}
$$

$$
\beta_ {2} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {A g} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {2} ^ {+} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right] ^ {2}} = 1. 7 \times 1 0 ^ {7} \tag {6.7.12}
$$

We still need three additional equations. The first of these equations is a mass balance for $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {N H} _ {3}} = \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} \right] + 2 \times \left[ \mathrm {A g} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {2} ^ {+} \right] \tag {6.7.13}
$$

In writing this mass balance equation we multiply the concentration of $\mathbf { A g ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } }$ by two since there are two moles of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ per mole of $\mathbf { A g ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } }$ . The second additional equation is a mass balance between iodide and silver. Because AgI is the only source of $\Gamma ^ { - }$ and ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ , each iodide in solution must have an associated silver ion, which may be ${ \bf A } { \bf g } ^ { + }$ or $\mathbf { A g ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } }$ ; thus 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {I} ^ {-} \right] = \left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {A g} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {2} ^ {+} \right] \tag {6.7.14}
$$

Finally, we include a charge balance equation. 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {A g} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {2} ^ {+} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] = \left[ \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {I} ^ {-} \right] \tag {6.7.15}
$$

Although the problem looks challenging, three assumptions greatly simplify the algebra. 

Assumption One. Because the formation of the $\mathbf { A g ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } }$ complex is so favorable $\beta _ { 2 }$ is $\mathbf { 1 . 7 \times 1 0 ^ { 7 } } .$ ), there is very little free ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ in solution and it is reasonable to assume that 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right] <   <   \left[ \mathrm {A g} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {2} ^ {+} \right]
$$

Assumption Two. Because $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ is a weak base we may reasonably assume that most uncomplexed ammonia remains as $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ ; thus 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {4} ^ {+} \right] <   <   \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right]
$$

Assumption Three. Because $K _ { \mathrm { s p } }$ for AgI is significantly smaller than $\beta _ { 2 }$ for $\mathbf { A g ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } }$ , the solubility of AgI probably is small enough that very little ammonia is needed to form the metal–ligand complex; thus 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {A g} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {2} ^ {+} \right] <   <   \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right]
$$

As we use these assumptions to simplify the algebra, let’s set $\pm 5 \%$ as the limit for error. 

Assumption two and assumption three suggest that the concentration of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ is much larger than the concentrations of either $\mathbf { N H } _ { 4 } ^ { + }$ or $\mathbf { A g ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } }$ , which allows us to simplify the mass balance equation for $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ to 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {N H} _ {3}} = \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right] \tag {6.7.16}
$$

Finally, using assumption one, which suggests that the concentration of ${ \bf A g ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } }$ is much larger than the concentration of ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ , we simplify the mass balance equation for $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ to 

$$
\left[ \mathbf {I} ^ {-} \right] = \left[ \mathrm {A g} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {2} ^ {+} \right] \tag {6.7.17}
$$

Now we are ready to combine equations and to solve the problem. We begin by solving Equation for $[ \mathrm { A g } ^ { + } ]$ and substitute it into $\beta _ { 2 }$ (Equation ), which leaves us with 

$$
\beta_ {2} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {A g} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {2} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {I} ^ {-} \right]}{K _ {\mathrm {s p}} \left[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \right] ^ {2}} \tag {6.7.18}
$$

Next we substitute Equation and Equation into Equation , obtaining 

$$
\beta_ {2} = \frac {\left[ I ^ {-} \right] ^ {2}}{K _ {\mathrm {s p}} \left(C _ {\mathrm {N H} _ {3}}\right) ^ {2}} \tag {6.7.19}
$$

Solving Equation for [I ] gives– 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {I} ^ {-} \right] = C _ {\mathrm {N H} _ {3}} \sqrt {\beta_ {2} K _ {s p}} =
$$

$$
(0. 1 0) \sqrt {\left(1 . 7 \times 1 0 ^ {7}\right) \left(8 . 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 7}\right)} = 3. 7 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} \mathrm {M}
$$

Because one mole of AgI produces one mole of $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ , the molar solubility of AgI is the same as the $[ \mathrm { I ^ { - } } ]$ , or $\mathbf { 3 . 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m o l / L } }$ . 

Before we accept this answer we need to check our assumptions. Substituting $[ \mathrm { I ^ { - } } ]$ into Equation , we find that the concentration of ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ is 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right] = \frac {K _ {\mathrm {p}}}{\left[ \mathrm {I} ^ {-} \right]} = \frac {8 . 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 7}}{3 . 7 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6}} = 2. 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 1} \mathrm {M}
$$

Substituting the concentrations of $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ and $\mathbf { A } \mathbf { g } ^ { + }$ into the mass balance equation for iodide (Equation ), gives the concentration of ${ \bf A g ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } }$ as 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {A g} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {2} ^ {+} \right] = \left[ \mathrm {I} ^ {-} \right] - \left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right] = 3. 7 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} - 2. 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 1} = 3. 7 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} \mathrm {M}
$$

Our first assumption that $[ \mathrm { A g } ^ { + } ]$ is significantly smaller than the $[ \mathbf { A g } ( \mathbf { N H _ { 3 } } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } ]$ is reasonable. 

Substituting the concentrations of ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ and $\mathbf { A g ( N H _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } }$ into Equation and solving for $\left[ \mathrm { N H } _ { 3 } \right]$ , gives 

$$
[ \mathrm {N H} _ {3} ] = \sqrt {\frac {[ \mathrm {A g (N H} _ {3}) _ {2} ^ {+} ]}{[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} ] \beta_ {2}}} = \sqrt {\frac {3 . 7 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6}}{\left(2 . 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 1}\right) \left(1 . 7 \times 1 0 ^ {7}\right)}} = 0. 1 0 \mathrm {M}
$$

From the mass balance equation for NH3 (Equation ) we see that $[ \mathbf { N H } _ { 4 } ^ { + } ]$ is negligible, verifying our second assumption that $[ \mathrm { N H } _ { 4 } ^ { + } ]$ is significantly smaller than $\left[ \mathrm { N H } _ { 3 } \right]$ . Our third assumption that $[ \mathbf { A g } ( \mathbf { N H _ { 3 } } ) _ { 2 } ^ { + } ]$ is significantly smaller than $\left[ \mathrm { N H } _ { 3 } \right]$ also is reasonable. 

Did you notice that our solution to this problem did not make use of Equation , the charge balance equation? The reason for this is that we did not try to solve for the concentration of all seven species. If we need to know the reaction mixture’s complete composition at equilibrium, then we will need to incorporate the charge balance equation into our solution.
