Example 9.5 shows how we can use an acid–base titration to determine the forms of alkalinity in waters and their concentrations. We can extend this approach to other systems. For example, if we titrate a sample to the methyl orange end point and the phenolphthalein end point using either a strong acid or a strong base, we can determine which of the following species are present and their concentrations: $\mathrm { H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { P O } _ { 4 }$ , $\mathbf { H _ { 2 } P O _ { 4 } ^ { - } }$ , $\mathbf { H P O _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - } }$ , $\pmb { \mathrm { P O } } _ { 4 } ^ { 3 - }$ , HCl, and NaOH. As outlined in Table 9.2.8 , each species or mixture of species has a unique relationship between the volumes of titrant needed to reach these two end points. Note that mixtures containing three or more these species are not possible. 

Use a ladder diagram to convince yourself that mixtures containing three or more of these species are unstable. 

Table 9.2.8 . Relationship Between End Point Volumes for Mixtures of Phosphate Species with HCl and NaOH 

<table><tr><td>solution composition</td><td>relationship between end point volumes with strong base titrant</td><td>relationship between end point volumes with strong acid titrant</td></tr><tr><td>H3PO4</td><td>VPH=2 × VMO</td><td>—</td></tr><tr><td>H2PO4-</td><td>VPH&gt;0; VMO=0</td><td>—</td></tr><tr><td>HPO42-</td><td>—</td><td>VMO&gt;0; VPH=0</td></tr><tr><td>PO43-</td><td>—</td><td>VMO=2 × VPH</td></tr><tr><td>HCl</td><td>VPH=VMO</td><td>—</td></tr><tr><td>NaOH</td><td>—</td><td>VMO=VPH</td></tr><tr><td>HCl and H3PO4</td><td>VPH&lt;2 × VMO</td><td>—</td></tr><tr><td>H3PO4and H2PO4-</td><td>VPH&gt;2 × VMO</td><td>—</td></tr><tr><td>H2PO4- and HPO42-</td><td>VPH&gt;0; VMO=0</td><td>VMO&gt;0; VPH=0</td></tr><tr><td>HPO42- and PO43-</td><td>—</td><td>VMO&gt;2 × VPH</td></tr><tr><td>PO43- and NaOH</td><td>—</td><td>VMO&lt;2 × VPH</td></tr><tr><td colspan="3">VPH and VMO are, respectively, the volume of titrant at the phenolphthalein and methyl orange end points when no information is provided, the volume at each end point is zero</td></tr></table>

# Characterization Applications

In addition to a quantitative analysis and a qualitative analysis, we also can use an acid–base titration to characterize the chemical and physical properties of matter. Two useful characterization applications are the determination of a compound’s equivalent weight and the determination of its acid dissociation constant or its base dissociation constant. 

# Equivalent Weights

Suppose we titrate a sample of an impure weak acid to a well-defined end point using a monoprotic strong base as the titrant. If we assume the titration involves the transfer of $n$ protons, then the moles of titrant needed to reach the end point is 

$$
\text {m o l e s t i t r a n t} = \frac {n \text {m o l e s t i t r a n t}}{\text {m o l e s a n a l y t e}} \times \text {m o l e s a n a l y t e}
$$

If we know the analyte’s identity, we can use this equation to determine the amount of analyte in the sample 

$$
\text {g r a m s a n a l y t e} = \text {m o l e s t i t r a n t} \times \frac {1 \text {m o l e a n a l y t e}}{n \text {m o l e s a n a l y t e}} \times F W \text {a n a l y t e}
$$

where FW is the analyte’s formula weight. 

But what if we do not know the analyte’s identify? If we titrate a pure sample of the analyte, we can obtain some useful information that may help us establish its identity. Because we do not know the number of protons that are titrated, we let $n = 1$ and replace the analyte’s formula weight with its equivalent weight (EW) 

$$
\text {g r a m s a n a l y t e} = \text {m o l e s t i t r a n t} \times \frac {\text {1 e q u i v a l e n t a n a l y t e}}{\text {1 m o l e a n a l y t e}} = E W \text {a n a l y t e}
$$

where 

$$
F W = n \times E W
$$

#  Example 9.2.6

A 0.2521-g sample of an unknown weak acid is titrated with 0.1005 M NaOH, requiring 42.68 mL to reach the phenolphthalein end point. Determine the compound’s equivalent weight. Which of the following compounds is most likely to be the unknown 

weak acid? 

<table><tr><td>acid</td><td>formula</td><td>formula weight (g/mol)</td><td>type</td></tr><tr><td>ascorbic acid</td><td>C8H8O6</td><td>176.1</td><td>monoprotic</td></tr><tr><td>malonic acid</td><td>C3H4O4</td><td>104.1</td><td>diprotic</td></tr><tr><td>succinic acid</td><td>C4H6O4</td><td>118.1</td><td>diprotic</td></tr><tr><td>citric acid</td><td>C6H8O7</td><td>192.1</td><td>triprotic</td></tr></table>

# Solution

The moles of NaOH needed to reach the end point is 

$$
(0. 1 0 0 5 \mathrm {M} \mathrm {N a O H}) (0. 0 4 2 6 8 \mathrm {L} \mathrm {N a O H}) = 4. 2 8 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {N a O H}
$$

The equivalents of weak acid are the same as the moles of NaOH used in the titration; thus, he analyte’s equivalent weight is 

$$
E W = \frac {0 . 2 5 2 1 \mathrm {g}}{4 . 2 8 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {e q u i v a l e n t s}} = 5 8. 7 8 \mathrm {g / e q u i v a l e n t}
$$

The possible formula weights for the weak acid are $5 8 . 7 8 ~ \mathrm { g / m o l }$ $( n = 1 )$ , $1 1 7 . 6 ~ \mathrm { g / m o l }$ $( n = 2 )$ ), and $1 7 6 . 3 \ \mathrm { g / m o l }$ $( n = 3 )$ ). If the analyte is a monoprotic weak acid, then its formula weight is $5 8 . 7 8 ~ \mathrm { g / m o l }$ , eliminating ascorbic acid as a possibility. If it is a diprotic weak acid, then the analyte’s formula weight is either $5 8 . 7 8 ~ \mathrm { g / m o l }$ or $1 1 7 . 6 ~ \mathrm { g / m o l }$ , depending on whether the weak acid was titrated to its first or its second equivalence point. Succinic acid, with a formula weight of 118.1 g/mole is a possibility, but malonic acid is not. If the analyte is a triprotic weak acid, then its formula weight is $5 8 . 7 8 ~ \mathrm { g / m o l }$ , $1 1 7 . 6 \ \mathrm { g / m o l } .$ , or $1 7 6 . 3 \ : \mathrm { g / m o l }$ . None of these values is close to the formula weight for citric acid, eliminating it as a possibility. Only succinic acid provides a possible match. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/6aef55d8243a980037fa875945f37a2adc6168282c3176e5ee073e9a74d90d0f.jpg)



Figure 9.2.16 . Titration curve for Exercise 9.2.10 .


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/b116e76d799a5d83059e847aff69ce9e30b18459ec7cff04191c70243963112c.jpg)


# Exercise 9.2.10

Figure 9.2.16 shows the potentiometric titration curve for the titration of a 0.500-g sample an unknown weak acid. The titrant is 0.1032 M NaOH. What is the weak acid’s equivalent weight? 

# Answer

The first of the two visible end points is approximately 37 mL of NaOH. The analyte’s equivalent weight, therefore, is 

$$
(0. 1 0 3 2 \mathrm {M} \mathrm {N a O H}) (0. 0 3 7 \mathrm {L}) \times \frac {1 \text {e q u i v a l e n t}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {N a O H}} = 3. 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \text {e q u i v a l e n t s}
$$

$$
E W = \frac {0 . 5 0 0 0 \mathrm {g}}{3 . 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {e q u i v a l e n t s}} = 1. 3 \times 1 0 ^ {2} \mathrm {g / e q u i v a l e n t}
$$

# Equilibrium Constants

Another application of acid–base titrimetry is the determination of a weak acid’s or a weak base’s dissociation constant. Consider, for example, a solution of acetic acid, $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O H }$ , for which the dissociation constant is 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {a}} = \frac {[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} ] [ \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O} ^ {-} ]}{[ \mathrm {C H} _ {3} \mathrm {C O O H} ]}
$$

When the concentrations of $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O H }$ and $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O ^ { - } }$ are equal, the $K _ { \mathfrak { a } }$ expression reduces to $K _ { \mathrm { a } } = \mathrm { [ H _ { 3 } O ^ { + } ] }$ , or $\mathrm { p H } = \mathrm { p } K _ { \mathrm { a } }$ . If we titrate a solution of acetic acid with NaOH, the pH equals the $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ when the volume of NaOH is approximately $1 2 V _ { e q }$ . As shown in Figure 9.2.17 , a potentiometric titration curve provides a reasonable estimate of acetic acid’s $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ . 

Recall that $\mathrm { p H } = \mathrm { p } K _ { \mathrm { a } }$ is a step on a ladder diagram, which divides the pH axis into two regions, one where the weak acid is the predominate species, and one where its conjugate weak base is the predominate species. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/7e02917a9919a80caef73d66b0103f0ff5826548136485b3bc8c94b8361d7e3f.jpg)



Figure 9.2.17 . Estimating acetic acid’s $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ using its potentiometric titration curve.


This method provides a reasonable estimate for a weak acid’s $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ if the acid is neither too strong nor too weak. These limitations are easy to appreciate if we consider two limiting cases. For the first limiting case, let’s assume the weak acid, HA, is more than $5 0 \%$ dissociated before the titration begins (a relatively large $K _ { \mathfrak { a } }$ value); in this case the concentration of HA before the equivalence point is always less than the concentration of $\mathrm { A } ^ { - }$ and there is no point on the titration curve where $[ \mathrm { H A } ] = [ \mathrm { A } ^ { - } ]$ . At the other extreme, if the acid is too weak, then less than $5 0 \%$ of the weak acid reacts with the titrant at the equivalence point. In this case the concentration of HA before the equivalence point is always greater than that of $\mathrm { A } ^ { - }$ . Determining the $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ by the half-equivalence point method overestimates its value if the acid is too strong and underestimates its value if the acid is too weak. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/10ca0c8782b2c77ce1b33a710b6b797198c784ef08da11646319d247d41a8c20.jpg)


# Exercise 9.2.11

Use the potentiometric titration curve in Figure 9.2.16 to estimate the $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ values for the weak acid in Exercise 9.2.10 . 

# Answer

At $1 2 V _ { e q } ,$ or approximately $1 8 . 5 \mathrm { m L }$ , the pH is approximately 2.2; thus, we estimate that the analyte’s $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ is 2.2. 

A second approach for determining a weak acid’s $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ is to use a Gran plot. For example, earlier in this chapter we derived the following equation for the titration of a weak acid with a strong base. 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {O} ^ {+} \right] \times V _ {b} = K _ {a} V _ {e q} - K _ {a} V _ {b}
$$

A plot of $[ \mathrm { H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { O } ^ { + } ] \times V _ { b }$ versus $V _ { b }$ for volumes less than the equivalence point yields a straight line with a slope of $- K _ { \mathrm { a } }$ . Other linearizations have been developed that use the entire titration curve or that require no assumptions [(a) Gonzalez, A. G.; Asuero, A. G. Anal. Chim. Acta 1992, 256, 29–33; (b) Papanastasiou, G.; Ziogas, I.; Kokkindis, G. Anal. Chim. Acta 1993, 277, 119–135]. This approach to determining an acidity constant has been used to study the acid–base properties of humic acids, which are naturally occurring, large molecular weight organic acids with multiple acidic sites. In one study a humic acid was found to have six titratable sites, three which were identified as carboxylic acids, two which were believed to be secondary or tertiary amines, and 

one which was identified as a phenolic group [Alexio, L. M.; Godinho, O. E. S.; da Costa, W. F. Anal. Chim. Acta 1992, 257, 35– 39]. 

Values of $K _ { \mathfrak { a } }$ determined by this method may have a substantial error if the effect of activity is ignored. See Chapter 6.9 for a discussion of activity. 

# Evaluation of Acid–Base Titrimetry

# Scale of Operation

In an acid–base titration, the volume of titrant needed to reach the equivalence point is proportional to the moles of titrand. Because the pH of the titrand or the titrant is a function of its concentration, the change in pH at the equivalence point—and thus the feasibility of an acid–base titration—depends on their respective concentrations. Figure 9.2.18 , for example, shows a series of titration curves for the titration of several concentrations of HCl with equimolar solutions NaOH. For titrand and titrant concentrations smaller than $1 0 ^ { - 3 } \mathbf { M }$ , the change in pH at the end point is too small to provide an accurate and a precise result. 

Acid–base titrimetry is an example of a total analysis technique in which the signal is proportional to the absolute amount of analyte. See Chapter 3 for a discussion of the difference between total analysis techniques and concentration techniques. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/b77bfda677e33b760050b55fbb240c787e29fd55596d345f63af213b24692764.jpg)



Figure 9.2.18 . Titration curves for $2 5 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of (a) $1 0 ^ { - 1 }$ $1 0 ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { M } \mathrm { H } \mathrm { C l }$ , (b) 10 M HCl, (c) –2 $1 0 ^ { - 3 }$ M HCl, (d) $1 0 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { M } \mathrm { H } \mathrm { C l }$ , and (e) $1 0 ^ { - 5 } \mathrm { { M } }$ HCl. In each case the titrant is an equimolar solution of NaOH.


A minimum concentration of $1 0 ^ { - 3 } \textbf { M }$ places limits on the smallest amount of analyte we can analyze successfully. For example, suppose our analyte has a formula weight of $1 2 0 ~ \mathrm { g / m o l }$ . To successfully monitor the titration’s end point using an indicator or a pH probe, the titrand needs an initial volume of approximately $2 5 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ . If we assume the analyte’s formula weight is $1 2 0 ~ \mathrm { g / m o l }$ , then each sample must contain at least $3 ~ \mathrm { m g }$ of analyte. For this reason, acid–base titrations generally are limited to major and minor analytes. We can extend the analysis of gases to trace analytes by pulling a large volume of the gas through a suitable collection solution. 

We need a volume of titrand sufficient to cover the tip of the pH probe or to allow for an easy observation of the indicator’s color. A volume of $2 5 \mathrm { m L }$ is not an unreasonable estimate of the minimum volume. 

One goal of analytical chemistry is to extend analyses to smaller samples. Here we describe two interesting approaches to titrating $\mu \mathrm { L }$ and pL samples. In one experimental design (Figure 9.2.19 ), samples of $2 0 – 1 0 0 \mu \mathrm { L }$ are held by capillary action between a flatsurface pH electrode and a stainless steel sample stage [Steele, A.; Hieftje, G. M. Anal. Chem. 1984, 56, 2884–2888]. The titrant is added using the oscillations of a piezoelectric ceramic device to move an angled glass rod in and out of a tube connected to a reservoir that contains the titrant. Each time the glass tube is withdrawn an approximately 2 nL microdroplet of titrant is released. The microdroplets are allowed to fall onto the sample, with mixing accomplished by spinning the sample stage at 120 rpm. A total of 450 microdroplets, with a combined volume of $0 . 8 1 { - } 0 . 8 4 ~ \mu \mathrm { L }$ , is dispensed between each pH measurement. In this fashion a titration curve is constructed. This method has been used to titrate solutions of 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M $\mathrm { C H _ { 3 } C O O H }$ with $0 . 1 \textbf { M }$ 

NaOH. Absolute errors ranged from a minimum of $+ 0 . 1 \%$ to a maximum of $- 4 . 1 \%$ , with relative standard deviations from $0 . 1 5 \%$ to $4 . 7 \%$ . Samples as small as $2 0 \mu \mathrm { L }$ were titrated successfully. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/d36cd1a5db7af213cf2459ac0dd3c4534cf6fd82a2c2d5ebb7735751bcc57d6d.jpg)



Figure 9.2.19 . Experimental design for a microdroplet titration apparatus.


Another approach carries out the acid–base titration in a single drop of solution [(a) Gratzl, M.; Yi, C. Anal. Chem. 1993, 65, 2085– 2088; (b) Yi, C.; Gratzl, M. Anal. Chem. 1994, 66, 1976–1982; (c) Hui, K. Y.; Gratzl, M. Anal. Chem. 1997, 69, 695–698; (d) Yi, C.; Huang, D.; Gratzl, M. Anal. Chem. 1996, 68, 1580–1584; (e) Xie, H.; Gratzl, M. Anal. Chem. 1996, 68, 3665–3669]. The titrant is delivered using a microburet fashioned from a glass capillary micropipet (Figure 9.2.20 ). The microburet has a $1 { \cdot } 2 ~ { \mu } \mathrm { m }$ tip filled with an agar gel membrane. The tip of the microburet is placed within a drop of the sample solution, which is suspended in heptane, and the titrant is allowed to diffuse into the sample. The titration’s progress is monitored using an acid–base indicator and the time needed to reach the end point is measured. The rate of the titrant’s diffusion from the microburet is determined by a prior calibration. Once calibrated the end point time is converted to an end point volume. Samples usually consist of picoliter volumes $1 0 ^ { - 1 2 }$ liters), with the smallest sample being 0.7 pL. The precision of the titrations is about $2 \%$ . 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/84781e688a7be56fe76dc1c67a65ea3df01da4ac0c9d3e40811fe84b7d2aa1ce.jpg)



Figure 9.2.20 . Experimental set-up for a diffusional microtitration. The indicator is a mixture of bromothymol blue and bromocresol purple.


Titrations conducted with microliter or picoliter sample volumes require a smaller absolute amount of analyte. For example, diffusional titrations have been conducted on as little as 29 femtomoles $1 0 ^ { - 1 5 }$ moles) of nitric acid. Nevertheless, the analyte must be present in the sample at a major or minor level for the titration to give accurate and precise results. 

# Accuracy

When working with a macro–major or a macro–minor sample, an acid–base titration can achieve a relative error of $0 . 1 { - } 0 . 2 \%$ . The principal limitation to accuracy is the difference between the end point and the equivalence point. 

# Precision

An acid–base titration’s relative precision depends primarily on the precision with which we can measure the end point volume and the precision in detecting the end point. Under optimum conditions, an acid–base titration has a relative precision of $0 . 1 { - } 0 . 2 \%$ . We can improve the relative precision by using the largest possible buret and by ensuring we use most of its capacity in reaching the end point. A smaller volume buret is a better choice when using costly reagents, when waste disposal is a concern, or when we must complete the titration quickly to avoid competing chemical reactions. An automatic titrator is particularly useful for titrations that require small volumes of titrant because it provides significantly better precision (typically about $\pm 0 . 0 5 \%$ of the buret’s volume). 

The precision of detecting the end point depends on how it is measured and the slope of the titration curve at the end point. With an indicator the precision of the end point signal usually is $\pm 0 . 0 3 { - } 0 . 1 0 \mathrm { m L }$ . Potentiometric end points usually are more precise. 

# Sensitivity

For an acid–base titration we can write the following general analytical equation to express the titrant’s volume in terms of the amount of titrand 

$$
\text {v o l u m e} = k \times \text {m o l e s}
$$

where $k ,$ , the sensitivity, is determined by the stoichiometry between the titrand and the titrant. Consider, for example, the determination of sulfurous acid, $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S O } _ { 3 }$ , by titrating with NaOH to the first equivalence point 

$$
\mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {3} (a q) + \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q) \rightarrow \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) + \mathrm {H S O} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

At the equivalence point the relationship between the moles of NaOH and the moles of $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S O } _ { 3 }$ is 

$$
\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {N a O H} = \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {3}
$$

Substituting the titrant’s molarity and volume for the moles of NaOH and rearranging 

$$
M _ {\mathrm {N a O H}} \times V _ {\mathrm {N N O H}} = \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {3}
$$

$$
V _ {\mathrm {N a O H}} = \frac {1}{M _ {\mathrm {N a O H}}} \times \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {3}
$$

we find that $k$ is 

$$
k = \frac {1}{M _ {\mathrm {N a O H}}}
$$

There are two ways in which we can improve a titration’s sensitivity. The first, and most obvious, is to decrease the titrant’s concentration because it is inversely proportional to the sensitivity, $k$ . The second approach, which applies only if the titrand is multiprotic, is to titrate to a later equivalence point. If we titrate $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S O } _ { 3 }$ to its second equivalence point 

$$
\mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {3} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) + \mathrm {S O} _ {3} ^ {2 -} (a q)
$$

then each mole of $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S O } _ { 3 }$ consumes two moles of NaOH 

$$
\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {N a O H} = 2 \times \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {3}
$$

and the sensitivity becomes 

$$
k = \frac {2}{M _ {\mathrm {N a O H}}}
$$

In practice, however, any improvement in sensitivity is offset by a decrease in the end point’s precision if a larger volume of titrant requires us to refill the buret. For this reason, standard acid–base titrimetric procedures are written to ensure that a titration uses $6 0 { - } 1 0 0 \%$ of the buret’s volume. 

# Selectivity

Acid–base titrants are not selective. A strong base titrant, for example, reacts with all acids in a sample, regardless of their individual strengths. If the titrand contains an analyte and an interferent, then selectivity depends on their relative acid strengths. Let’s consider two limiting situations. 

If the analyte is a stronger acid than the interferent, then the titrant will react with the analyte before it begins reacting with the interferent. The feasibility of the analysis depends on whether the titrant’s reaction with the interferent affects the accurate location of the analyte’s equivalence point. If the acid dissociation constants are substantially different, the end point for the analyte can be determined accurately. Conversely, if the acid dissociation constants for the analyte and interferent are similar, then there may not be an accurate end point for the analyte. In the latter case a quantitative analysis for the analyte is not possible. 

In the second limiting situation the analyte is a weaker acid than the interferent. In this case the volume of titrant needed to reach the analyte’s equivalence point is determined by the concentration of both the analyte and the interferent. To account for the 

interferent’s contribution to the end point, an end point for the interferent must be available. Again, if the acid dissociation constants for the analyte and interferent are significantly different, then the analyte’s determination is possible. If the acid dissociation constants are similar, however, there is only a single equivalence point and we cannot separate the analyte’s and the interferent’s contributions to the equivalence point volume. 

# Time, Cost, and Equipment

Acid–base titrations require less time than most gravimetric procedures, but more time than many instrumental methods of analysis, particularly when analyzing many samples. With an automatic titrator, however, concerns about analysis time are less significant. When performing a titration manually our equipment needs—a buret and, perhaps, a pH meter—are few in number, inexpensive, routinely available, and easy to maintain. Automatic titrators are available for between $\$ 3000$ and $\$ 10000$ .

# 9.3: Complexation Titrations

The earliest examples of metal–ligand complexation titrations are Liebig’s determinations, in the 1850s, of cyanide and chloride using, respectively, ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { H g } ^ { 2 + }$ as the titrant. Practical analytical applications of complexation titrimetry were slow to develop because many metals and ligands form a series of metal–ligand complexes. Liebig’s titration of $\mathrm { C N ^ { - } }$ with ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ was successful because they form a single, stable complex of $\mathbf { A g ( C N ) _ { 2 } ^ { - } }$ , which results in a single, easily identified end point. Other metal–ligand complexes, such as $\mathrm { C d } \mathtt { I } _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - }$ , are not analytically useful because they form a series of metal–ligand complexes ( $\mathrm { E d I } ^ { + }$ , CdI (aq), 2 and $\mathbf { C d I } _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - }$ ) that produce a sequence of poorly defined end points. 

Recall that an acid–base titration curve for a diprotic weak acid has a single end point if its two $K _ { \mathfrak { a } }$ values are not sufficiently different. See Figure 9.2.6 for an example. 

In 1945, Schwarzenbach introduced aminocarboxylic acids as multidentate ligands. The most widely used of these new ligands— ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, or EDTA—forms a strong 1:1 complex with many metal ions. The availability of a ligand that gives a single, easily identified end point made complexation titrimetry a practical analytical method. 

# Chemistry and Properties of EDTA

Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, or EDTA, is an aminocarboxylic acid. EDTA, the structure of which is shown in Figure 9.3.1 a in its fully deprotonated form, is a Lewis acid with six binding sites—the four negatively charged carboxylate groups and the two tertiary amino groups—that can donate up to six pairs of electrons to a metal ion. The resulting metal–ligand complex, in which EDTA forms a cage-like structure around the metal ion (Figure 9.3.1 b), is very stable. The actual number of coordination sites depends on the size of the metal ion, however, all metal–EDTA complexes have a 1:1 stoichiometry. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/f8869449eb9df623dfdc52703b1d6446d3018ca2069ef3fe68239ab78df6dc5d.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/64db316160dcb5ea4b93ad6c241ccc7fb5ca8ed3bb4dfd41d0a91d6a5af62357.jpg)



Figure 9.3.1 . Structures of (a) EDTA, in its fully deprotonated form, and (b) in a six-coordinate metal–EDTA complex with a divalent metal ion.


# Metal–EDTA Formation Constants

To illustrate the formation of a metal–EDTA complex, let’s consider the reaction between $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ and EDTA 

$$
\mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \mathrm {Y} ^ {4 -} (a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} (a q) \tag {9.3.1}
$$

where ${ \mathrm { Y } } ^ { 4 - }$ is a shorthand notation for the fully deprotonated form of EDTA shown in Figure 9.3.1 a. Because the reaction’s formation constant 

$$
K _ {f} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] \left[ \mathrm {Y} ^ {4 -} \right]} = 2. 9 \times 1 0 ^ {1 6} \tag {9.3.2}
$$

is large, its equilibrium position lies far to the right. Formation constants for other metal–EDTA complexes are found in Appendix 12. 

# EDTA is a Weak Acid

In addition to its properties as a ligand, EDTA is also a weak acid. The fully protonated form of EDTA, $\mathrm { H } _ { 6 } \mathrm { Y } ^ { 2 + }$ , is a hexaprotic weak acid with successive $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } }$ values of 

$$
\mathrm {p} K _ {\mathrm {a} 1} = 0. 0 \quad \mathrm {p} K _ {\mathrm {a} 2} = 1. 5 \quad \mathrm {p} K _ {\mathrm {a} 3} = 2. 0
$$

$$
\mathrm {p} K _ {\mathrm {a} 4} = 2. 6 6 \quad \mathrm {p} K _ {\mathrm {a} 5} = 6. 1 6 \quad \mathrm {p} K _ {\mathrm {a} 6} = 1 0. 2 4
$$

The first four values are for the carboxylic acid protons and the last two values are for the ammonium protons. Figure 9.3.2 shows a ladder diagram for EDTA. The specific form of EDTA in reaction is the predominate species only when the pH is more basic than 10.24. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/05354ec58aead0973733d3374d10003552c605c906f2bba0345e541200f6e47b.jpg)



Figure 9.3.2 . Ladder diagram for EDTA.


# Conditional Metal–Ligand Formation Constants

The formation constant for $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ in Equation assumes that EDTA is present as ${ \mathrm { Y } } ^ { 4 - }$ . Because EDTA has many forms, when we prepare a solution of EDTA we know it total concentration, $C _ { \mathrm { E D T A } }$ , not the concentration of a specific form, such as $\Upsilon ^ { 4 - }$ . To use Equation , we need to rewrite it in terms of $C _ { \mathrm { E D T A } }$ . 

At any pH a mass balance on EDTA requires that its total concentration equal the combined concentrations of each of its forms. 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} = \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {6} \mathrm {Y} ^ {2 +} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {5} \mathrm {Y} ^ {+} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {4} \mathrm {Y} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {3} \mathrm {Y} ^ {-} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {Y} ^ {2 -} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {H Y} ^ {3 -} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {Y} ^ {4 -} \right]
$$

To correct the formation constant for EDTA’s acid–base properties we need to calculate the fraction, ${ \pmb { \alpha } } _ { \mathbf { Y 4 - } }$ , of EDTA that is present as $\mathrm { Y } ^ { 4 - }$ . 

$$
\alpha_ {\mathrm {Y} ^ {4 -}} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {Y} ^ {4 -} \right]}{C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} \tag {9.3.3}
$$

Table 9.3.1 provides values of $\pmb { \alpha } _ { \mathbf { Y } ^ { 4 - } }$ for selected pH levels. Solving Equation for $\textstyle [ \mathbf { Y } ^ { 4 - } ]$ and substituting into Equation for the $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ formation constant 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {f}} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] \left(\alpha_ {\mathrm {Y 4 -}}\right) C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}}
$$

and rearranging gives 

$$
K _ {f} ^ {\prime} = K _ {f} \times \alpha_ {\mathrm {Y} ^ {4 -}} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} \tag {9.3.4}
$$

where $K _ { f } ^ { \prime }$ is a pH-dependent conditional formation constant. As shown in Table 9.3.2 , the conditional formation constant for $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ becomes smaller and the complex becomes less stable at more acidic pHs. 

Table 9.3.1 . Values of $\pmb { \alpha } _ { \mathbf { Y } ^ { 4 - } }$ for EDTA at Selected pH Levels

<table><tr><td>pH</td><td>Kf&#x27;</td><td>pH</td><td>Kf&#x27;</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>5.6 × 10-2</td><td>8</td><td>1.6 × 1014</td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td>1.0 × 103</td><td>9</td><td>1.6 × 1015</td></tr><tr><td>3</td><td>7.7 × 105</td><td>10</td><td>1.1 × 1016</td></tr><tr><td>4</td><td>1.1 × 108</td><td>11</td><td>2.5 × 1016</td></tr><tr><td>5</td><td>1.1 × 1010</td><td>12</td><td>2.9 × 1016</td></tr><tr><td>6</td><td>6.9 × 1011</td><td>13</td><td>2.9 × 1016</td></tr><tr><td>7</td><td>1.5 × 1013</td><td>14</td><td>2.9 × 1016</td></tr></table>

# EDTA Competes With Other Ligands

To maintain a constant pH during a complexation titration we usually add a buffering agent. If one of the buffer’s components is a ligand that binds with $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ , then EDTA must compete with the ligand for $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ . For example, an $\mathrm { N H _ { 4 } ^ { + } / N H _ { 3 } }$ buffer includes $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 } .$ , which forms several stable $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + } { \mathrm { - N H } } _ { 3 }$ complexes. Because EDTA forms a stronger complex with $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ than does $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ , it displaces $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ ; however, the stability of the $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + } .$ –EDTA complex decreases. 

We can account for the effect of an auxiliary complexing agent, such as $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ , in the same way we accounted for the effect of pH. Before adding EDTA, the mass balance on $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ , $C _ { \mathrm { C d } } ,$ is 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {C d}} = \left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {C d} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) ^ {2 +} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {C d} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {2} ^ {2 +} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {C d} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {3} ^ {2 +} \right] + \left[ \mathrm {C d} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {4} ^ {2 +} \right]
$$

and the fraction of uncomplexed $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ , $\pmb { \alpha } _ { C d ^ { 2 } } +$ , is 

$$
\alpha_ {\mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +}} = \frac {[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} ]}{C _ {\mathrm {C d}}} \tag {9.3.5}
$$

The value of $\pmb { \alpha } _ { \mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + } }$ depends on the concentration of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ . Contrast this with $\pmb { \alpha } _ { \mathbf { Y ^ { 4 - } } }$ , which depends on pH. 

Solving Equation for $[ \mathsf { C d } ^ { 2 + } ]$ and substituting into Equation gives 

$$
K _ {f} ^ {\prime} = K _ {f} \times \alpha_ {Y +} = \frac {[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} ]}{\alpha_ {\mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +}} C _ {\mathrm {C d}} C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}}
$$

Because the concentration of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ in a buffer essentially is constant, we can rewrite this equation 

$$
K _ {f} ^ {\prime \prime} = K _ {f} \times \alpha_ {\mathrm {Y} ^ {4 -}} \times \alpha_ {\mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +}} = \frac {[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} ]}{C _ {\mathrm {C d}} C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} \tag {9.3.6}
$$

to give a conditional formation constant, $\kappa _ { f } ^ { \prime \prime }$ , that accounts for both pH and the auxiliary complexing agent’s concentration. Table 9.3.3 provides values of $\alpha _ { \mathbf { M } ^ { 2 + } }$ for several metal ion when $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ is the complexing agent.

# Complexometric EDTA Titration Curves

Now that we know something about EDTA’s chemical properties, we are ready to evaluate its usefulness as a titrant. To do so we need to know the shape of a complexometric titration curve. In chapter 9.2 we learned that an acid–base titration curve shows how the titrand’s pH changes as we add titrant. The analogous result for a complexation titration shows the change in pM, where M is the metal ion’s concentration, as a function of the volume of EDTA. In this section we will learn how to calculate a titration curve using the equilibrium calculations from Chapter 6. We also will learn how to sketch a good approximation of any complexation titration curve using a limited number of simple calculations. 

$$
\mathrm {p M} = - \log [ \mathrm {M} ^ {2 +} ]
$$

# Calculating the Titration Curve

Let’s calculate the titration curve for $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of $5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { ~ M ~ C d } ^ { 2 + }$ using a titrant of 0.0100 M EDTA. Furthermore, let’s assume the titrand is buffered to a pH of 10 using a buffer that is $0 . 0 1 0 0 \mathbf { M }$ in $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ . 

Because the pH is 10, some of the EDTA is present in forms other than ${ \mathrm { Y } } ^ { 4 - }$ . In addition, EDTA will compete with $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ for the $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ . To evaluate the titration curve, therefore, we first need to calculate the conditional formation constant for $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ . From Table 9.3.1 and Table 9.3.3 we find that ${ \pmb { \alpha } } _ { \mathbf { Y 4 - } }$ is 0.367 at a pH of 10, and that $\alpha _ { \mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + } }$ is 0.0881 when the concentration of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ is 0.0100 M. Using these values, the conditional formation constant is 

$$
K _ {f} ^ {\prime \prime} = K _ {f} \times \alpha_ {\mathrm {Y ^ {4 -}}} \times \alpha_ {\mathrm {C d ^ {2 +}}} = \left(2. 9 \times 1 0 ^ {1 6}\right) (0. 3 6 7) (0. 0 8 8 1) = 9. 4 \times 1 0 ^ {1 4}
$$

Because $K _ { f } ^ { \prime \prime }$ is so large, we can treat the titration reaction 

$$
\mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \mathrm {Y} ^ {4 -} (a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm {C d} \mathrm {Y} ^ {2 -} (a q)
$$

as if it proceeds to completion. 

The next task is to determine the volume of EDTA needed to reach the equivalence point. At the equivalence point we know that the moles of EDTA added must equal the moles of $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ in our sample; thus 

$$
\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A} = M _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} \times V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} = M _ {\mathrm {C d}} \times V _ {\mathrm {C d}} = \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +}
$$

Substituting in known values, we find that it requires 

$$
V _ {e q} = V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {C d}} V _ {\mathrm {C d}}}{M _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} = \frac {\left(5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}\right) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{0 . 0 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}} = 2 5. 0 \mathrm {m L}
$$

of EDTA to reach the equivalence point. 

Before the equivalence point, $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ is present in excess and pCd is determined by the concentration of unreacted $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ . Because not all unreacted $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ is free—some is complexed with $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ —we must account for the presence of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ . For example, after adding 5.0 mL of EDTA, the total concentration of $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ is 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {C d}} = \frac {(\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +}) _ {\mathrm {i n i t i a l}} - (\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}) _ {\mathrm {a d d e d}}}{\mathrm {t o t a l v o l u m e}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {C d}} V _ {\mathrm {C d}} - M _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}}{V _ {\mathrm {C d}} + V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}}
$$

$$
C _ {\mathrm {C d}} = \frac {\left(5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}\right) \left(5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}\right) - \left(0 . 0 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}\right) \left(5 . 0 \mathrm {m L}\right)}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 5 . 0 \mathrm {m L}}
$$

$$
C _ {\mathrm {C d}} = 3. 6 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}
$$

To calculate the concentration of free $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ we use Equation 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] = \alpha_ {\mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +}} \times C _ {\mathrm {C d}} = (0. 0 8 8 1) \left(3. 6 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}\right) = 3. 2 1 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {M}
$$

which gives a pCd of 

$$
\mathrm {p C d} = - \log [ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} ] = - \log (3. 2 1 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}) = 3. 4 9
$$

At the equivalence point all $\mathrm { C d ^ { 2 + } }$ initially in the titrand is now present as ${ \mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } } }$ . The concentration of $\mathrm { C d ^ { 2 + } }$ , therefore, is determined by the dissociation of the $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ complex. First, we calculate the concentration of $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ . 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right] = \frac {\left(\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +}\right) _ {\mathrm {i n i t i a l}}}{\text {t o t a l v o l u m e}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {C d}} V _ {\mathrm {C d}}}{V _ {\mathrm {C d}} + V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}}
$$

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right] = \frac {\left(5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}\right) \left(5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}\right)}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 2 5 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 3. 3 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}
$$

Next, we solve for the concentration of $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ in equilibrium with $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {f}} ^ {\prime \prime} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right]}{C _ {\mathrm {C d}} C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} = \frac {3 . 3 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} - x}{(x) (x)} = 9. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {1 4}
$$

$$
x = C _ {\mathrm {C d}} = 1. 8 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 9} \mathrm {M}
$$

In calculating that $\mathrm { [ C d Y ^ { 2 - } ] }$ at the equivalence point is $\mathbf { 3 . 3 3 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } M }$ , we assumed the reaction between $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ and EDTA went to completion. Here we let the system relax back to equilibrium, increasing $C _ { \mathrm { C d } }$ and $C _ { \mathrm { E D T A } }$ from 0 to $X _ { i }$ , and decreasing the concentration of ${ \mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } } }$ by $x$ . 

Once again, to find the concentration of uncomplexed Cd we must account for the presence of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ ; thus 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] = \alpha_ {\mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +}} \times C _ {\mathrm {C d}} = (0. 0 8 8 1) \left(1. 8 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 9} \mathrm {M}\right) = 1. 6 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 0} \mathrm {M}
$$

and pCd is 9.78 at the equivalence point. 

After the equivalence point, EDTA is in excess and the concentration of $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ is determined by the dissociation of the $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ complex. First, we calculate the concentrations of $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ and of unreacted EDTA. For example, after adding $3 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of EDTA the concentration of ${ \mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } } }$ is 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right] = \frac {\left(\mathrm {m o l C d} ^ {2 +}\right) _ {\mathrm {i n i t i a l}}}{\mathrm {t o t a l v o l u m e}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {C d}} V _ {\mathrm {C d}}}{V _ {\mathrm {C d}} + V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}}
$$

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right] = \frac {\left(5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}\right) \left(5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}\right)}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 3 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 3. 1 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}
$$

and the concentration of EDTA is 

$$
C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} = \frac {(\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}) _ {\mathrm {a d d e d}} - (\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +}) _ {\mathrm {i n i t i a l}}}{\text {t o t a l v o l u m e}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} - M _ {\mathrm {C d}} V _ {\mathrm {C d}}}{V _ {\mathrm {C d}} + V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}}
$$

$$
C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} = \frac {(0 . 0 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (3 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}) - (5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 3 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}}
$$

$$
C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} = 6. 2 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {M}
$$

Substituting into Equation and solving for $[ \mathsf { C d } ^ { 2 + } ]$ gives 

$$
\frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right]}{C _ {\mathrm {C d}} C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} = \frac {3 . 1 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}}{C _ {\mathrm {C d}} \left(6 . 2 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {M}\right)} = 9. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {1 4}
$$

$$
C _ {\mathrm {C d}} = 5. 2 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 5} \mathrm {M}
$$

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] = \alpha_ {\mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +}} \times C _ {\mathrm {C d}} = (0. 0 8 8 1) (5. 2 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 5} \mathrm {M}) = 4. 6 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 6} \mathrm {M}
$$

a pCd of 15.33. Table 9.3.4 and Figure 9.3.3 show additional results for this titration. 

After the equilibrium point we know the equilibrium concentrations of $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ and of EDTA in all its forms, C . We canEDTA solve for $C _ { \mathrm { C d } }$ using $K _ { f } ^ { \prime \prime }$ and then calculate $[ \mathsf { C } \mathsf { d } ^ { 2 + } ]$ using $\alpha _ { \mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + } }$ . Because we used the same conditional formation constant, $K _ { f } ^ { \prime \prime }$ , for other calculations in this section, this is the approach used here as well. There is a second method for calculating $[ \mathsf { C } \mathsf { d } ^ { 2 + } ]$ after the equivalence point. Because the calculation uses only $\operatorname { \mathrm { [ C d Y ^ { 2 - } ] } }$ and $C _ { \mathrm { E D T A } }$ , we can use $K _ { f } ^ { \prime }$ instead of $K _ { f } ^ { \prime \prime }$ ; thus 

$$
\frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} = \alpha_ {\mathrm {Y} +} \times K _ {\mathrm {f}}
$$

$$
\frac {3 . 1 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] \left(6 . 2 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}\right)} = (0. 3 6 7) \left(2. 9 \times 1 0 ^ {1 6}\right)
$$

Solving gives $[ \mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + } ] = 4 . 7 1 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 6 } \mathrm { ~ M ~ }$ and a pCd of 15.33. We will use this approach when we learn how to sketch a complexometric titration curve. 


Table 9.3.4 . Titration of $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of ${ \bf 5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } M C d ^ { 2 + } }$ with 0.0100 M EDTA at a pH of 10 and in the Presence of 0.0100 M


<table><tr><td>volume of EDTA (mL)</td><td>pCd</td><td>volume of EDTA (mL)</td><td>pCd</td></tr><tr><td>0.00</td><td>3.36</td><td>27.0</td><td>14.95</td></tr><tr><td>5.00</td><td>3.49</td><td>30.0</td><td>15.33</td></tr><tr><td>10.0</td><td>3.66</td><td>35.0</td><td>15.61</td></tr><tr><td>15.0</td><td>3.87</td><td>40.0</td><td>15.76</td></tr><tr><td>20.0</td><td>4.20</td><td>45.0</td><td>15.86</td></tr><tr><td>23.0</td><td>4.62</td><td>50.0</td><td>15.94</td></tr><tr><td>25.0</td><td>9.78</td><td></td><td></td></tr></table>

The red points correspond to the data in Table 9.3.4 . The blue line shows the complete titration curve.


#  Exercise 9.3.1

Calculate titration curves for the titration of $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of ${ \bf 5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } M C d ^ { 2 + } }$ $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ with 0.0100 M EDTA (a) at a pH of 10 and (b) at a pH of 7. Neither titration includes an auxiliary complexing agent. Compare your results with Figure 9.3.3 and comment on the effect of pH on the titration of $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ with EDTA. 

# Answer

Let’s begin with the calculations at a pH of 10 where some of the EDTA is present in forms other than $\mathrm { Y } ^ { 4 - }$ . To evaluate the titration curve, therefore, we need the conditional formation constant for $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ , which, from Table 9.3.2 is $K _ { f } ^ { \prime } = 1 . 1 \times 1 0 ^ { 1 6 }$ . Note that the conditional formation constant is larger in the absence of an auxiliary complexing agent. 

The titration’s equivalence point requires 

$$
V _ {e q} = V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {C d}} V _ {\mathrm {C d}}}{M _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} = \frac {\left(5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}\right) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{(0 . 0 1 0 0 \mathrm {M})} = 2 5. 0 \mathrm {m L}
$$

of EDTA. 

Before the equivalence point, $\mathrm { C d ^ { 2 + } }$ is present in excess and pCd is determined by the concentration of unreacted $\mathrm { C d ^ { 2 + } }$ . For example, after adding $5 . 0 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of EDTA, the total concentration of $\mathrm { C d ^ { 2 + } }$ is 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] = \frac {\left(5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}\right) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}) - (0 . 0 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 . 0 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 5 . 0 0 \mathrm {m L}}
$$

which gives $[ \mathsf { C } \mathsf { d } ^ { 2 + } ]$ as $\mathbf { 3 . 6 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } }$ and pCd as 2.43. 

At the equivalence point all $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ initially in the titrand is now present as $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ . The concentration of $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ , therefore, is determined by the dissociation of the $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ complex. First, we calculate the concentration of $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ . 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C d Y ^ {2 -}} \right] = \frac {\left(5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}\right) \left(5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}\right)}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 2 5 . 0 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 3. 3 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}
$$

Next, we solve for the concentration of $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ in equilibrium with $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ . 

$$
K _ {f} ^ {\prime} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} = \frac {3 . 3 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} - x}{(x) (x)} = 1. 1 \times 1 0 ^ {1 6}
$$

Solving gives $[ \mathsf { C } \mathsf { d } ^ { 2 + } ]$ as $\mathbf { 5 . 5 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 0 } M }$ or a pCd of 9.26 at the equivalence point. 

After the equivalence point, EDTA is in excess and the concentration of $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ is determined by the dissociation of the $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ complex. First, we calculate the concentrations of $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ and of unreacted EDTA. For example, after adding 30.0 mL of EDTA 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C d Y ^ {2 -}} \right] = \frac {\left(5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}\right) \left(5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}\right)}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 3 0 . 0 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 3. 1 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}
$$

$$
C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} = \frac {(0 . 0 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (3 0 . 0 0 \mathrm {m L}) - (5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 3 0 . 0 0 \mathrm {m L}}
$$

$$
C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} = 6. 2 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {M}
$$

Substituting into the equation for the conditional formation constant 

$$
K _ {f} ^ {\prime} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} = \frac {3 . 1 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}}{(\mathrm {x}) (6 . 2 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {M})} = 1. 1 \times 1 0 ^ {1 6}
$$

and solving for $[ \mathsf { C d } ^ { 2 + } ]$ gives $\mathbf { 4 . 5 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 6 } \mathrm { ~ M ~ } }$ or a pCd of 15.34. 

The calculations at a pH of 7 are identical, except the conditional formation constant for ${ \mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } } }$ is $\mathbf { 1 . 5 \times 1 0 ^ { 1 3 } }$ instead of $\bf { \times 1 0 ^ { 1 6 } }$ . The following table summarizes results for these two titrations as well as the results from Table 9.3.4 for the titration of $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ at a pH of 10 in the presence of 0.0100 M $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ as an auxiliary complexing agent. 

<table><tr><td>Volume of EDTA (mL)</td><td>pCd at pH 10</td><td>pCd at pH 10 w/ 0.0100 M NH3</td><td>pCd at pH 7</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>2.30</td><td>3.36</td><td>2.30</td></tr><tr><td>5.00</td><td>2.43</td><td>3.49</td><td>2.43</td></tr><tr><td>10.0</td><td>2.60</td><td>3.66</td><td>2.60</td></tr><tr><td>15.0</td><td>2.81</td><td>3.87</td><td>2.81</td></tr><tr><td>20.0</td><td>3.15</td><td>4.20</td><td>3.15</td></tr><tr><td>23.0</td><td>3.56</td><td>4.62</td><td>3.56</td></tr><tr><td>25.0</td><td>9.26</td><td>9.77</td><td>7.83</td></tr><tr><td>27.0</td><td>14.94</td><td>14.95</td><td>12.08</td></tr><tr><td>30.0</td><td>15.34</td><td>15.33</td><td>12.48</td></tr><tr><td>35.0</td><td>15.61</td><td>15.61</td><td>12.78</td></tr><tr><td>40.0</td><td>15.76</td><td>15.76</td><td>12.95</td></tr><tr><td>45.0</td><td>15.86</td><td>15.86</td><td>13.08</td></tr><tr><td>50.0</td><td>15.94</td><td>15.94</td><td>13.18</td></tr></table>

Examining these results allows us to draw several conclusions. First, in the absence of an auxiliary complexing agent the titration curve before the equivalence point is independent of pH (compare columns 2 and 4). Second, for any pH, the titration curve after the equivalence point is the same regardless of whether an auxiliary complexing agent is present (compare columns 2 and 3). Third, the largest change in pH through the equivalence point occurs at higher pHs and in the absence of an auxiliary complexing agent. For example, from $2 3 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ to $2 7 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of EDTA the change in pCd is 11.38 at a pH of 10, 10.33 at a pH of 10 in the presence of $0 . 0 1 0 0 \mathrm { M } \mathrm { N H _ { 3 } }$ $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ , and 8.52 at a pH of 7. 

# Sketching an EDTA Titration Curve

To evaluate the relationship between a titration’s equivalence point and its end point, we need to construct only a reasonable approximation of the exact titration curve. In this section we demonstrate a simple method for sketching a complexation titration curve. Our goal is to sketch the titration curve quickly, using as few calculations as possible. Let’s use the titration of $5 0 . 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of ${ \bf 5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } M C d ^ { 2 + } }$ with 0.0100 M EDTA in the presence of 0.0100 M $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ to illustrate our approach. This is the same example we 

used in developing the calculations for a complexation titration curve. You can review the results of that calculation in Table 9.3.4 and Figure 9.3.3 . 

We begin by calculating the titration’s equivalence point volume, which, as we determined earlier, is 25.0 mL. Next, we draw our axes, placing pCd on the y-axis and the titrant’s volume on the $x$ -axis. To indicate the equivalence point’s volume, we draw a vertical line that intersects the $\boldsymbol { x }$ -axis at $2 5 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of EDTA. Figure 9.3.4 a shows the result of the first step in our sketch. 

Before the equivalence point, $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ is present in excess and pCd is determined by the concentration of unreacted $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ . Because not all unreacted $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ is free—some is complexed with $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ —we must account for the presence of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ . The calculations are straightforward, as we saw earlier. Figure 9.3.4 b shows the pCd after adding 5.00 mL and 10.0 mL of EDTA. 

The third step in sketching our titration curve is to add two points after the equivalence point. Here the concentration of $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + }$ is controlled by the dissociation of the $\mathrm { C d } ^ { 2 + } .$ –EDTA complex. Beginning with the conditional formation constant 

$$
K _ {f} ^ {\prime} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} = \alpha_ {\mathrm {Y} ^ {4 -}} \times K _ {f} = (0. 3 6 7) \left(2. 9 \times 1 0 ^ {1 6}\right) = 1. 1 \times 1 0 ^ {1 6}
$$

we take the log of each side and rearrange, arriving at 

$$
\begin{array}{l} \log K _ {f} ^ {\prime} = - \log \left[ \mathrm {C d} ^ {2 +} \right] + \log \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} \right]}{C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} \\ \mathrm {p C d} = \log K _ {f} ^ {\prime} + \log \frac {C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}}{[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2} - ]} \\ \end{array}
$$

Recall that we can use either of our two possible conditional formation constants, $\pmb { K } _ { f } ^ { \prime }$ or $K _ { f } ^ { \prime \prime }$ , to determine the composition of the system at equilibrium. 

Note that after the equivalence point, the titrand is a metal–ligand complexation buffer, with pCd determined by $C _ { \mathrm { E D T A } }$ and $\mathrm { [ C d Y ^ { 2 - } ] }$ . The buffer is at its lower limit of $\mathbf { p C d } = \log K _ { f } ^ { \prime } - 1$ when 

$$
\frac {C _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}}{[ \mathrm {C d Y} ^ {2 -} ]} = \frac {(\mathrm {m o l e E D T A}) _ {\mathrm {a d d e d}} - (\mathrm {m o l C d} ^ {2 +}) _ {\mathrm {i n i t i a l}}}{(\mathrm {m o l C d} ^ {2 +}) _ {\mathrm {i n i t i a l}}} = \frac {1}{1 0}
$$

Making appropriate substitutions and solving, we find that 

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {M _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} - M _ {\mathrm {C d}} V _ {\mathrm {C d}}}{M _ {\mathrm {C d}} V _ {\mathrm {C d}}} = \frac {1}{1 0} \\ M _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} - M _ {\mathrm {C d}} V _ {\mathrm {C d}} = 0. 1 \times M _ {\mathrm {C d}} V _ {\mathrm {C d}} \\ V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} = \frac {1 . 1 \times M _ {\mathrm {C d}} V _ {\mathrm {C d}}}{M _ {\mathrm {E D T A}}} = 1. 1 \times V _ {e q} \\ \end{array}
$$

Thus, when the titration reaches $1 1 0 \%$ of the equivalence point volume, pCd is $\log K _ { f } ^ { \prime } - 1$ . A similar calculation should convince you that pCd is $\log K _ { f } ^ { \prime } + 1$ when the volume of EDTA is $2 \times V _ { \mathrm { e q } }$ . 

Figure 9.3.4 c shows the third step in our sketch. First, we add a ladder diagram for the $\mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } }$ complex, including its buffer range, using its $\log K _ { f } ^ { \prime }$ value of 16.04. Next, we add two points, one for pCd at $1 1 0 \%$ of $V _ { \mathrm { e q } }$ (a pCd of 15.04 at $2 7 . 5 \mathrm { m L }$ ) and one for pCd at $2 0 0 \%$ of $V _ { \mathrm { e q } }$ (a pCd of 16.04 at 50.0 mL). 

Next, we draw a straight line through each pair of points, extending each line through the vertical line that indicates the equivalence point’s volume (Figure 9.3.4 d). Finally, we complete our sketch by drawing a smooth curve that connects the three straight-line segments (Figure 9.3.4 e). A comparison of our sketch to the exact titration curve (Figure 9.3.4 f) shows that they are in close agreement. 

Our treatment here is general and applies to any complexation titration using EDTA as a titrant.
