Figure 9.3.4 . Illustrations showing the steps in sketching an approximate titration curve for the titration of $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of $\mathbf { 5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } }$ M $\mathrm { \dot { C } d ^ { 2 + } }$ with 0.0100 M EDTA in the presence of 0.0100 M $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ : (a) locating the equivalence point volume; (b) plotting two points before the equivalence point; (c) plotting two points after the equivalence point; (d) preliminary approximation of titration curve using straight-lines; (e) final approximation of titration curve using a smooth curve; (f) comparison of approximate titration curve (solid black line) and exact titration curve (dashed red line). See the text for additional details.


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/3ebc08cbd7bc10ec0b42609686d8da2f28eecf9a2b6f3f827a241d1084e0563b.jpg)


# Exercise 9.3.2

Sketch titration curves for the titration of $5 0 . 0 \ \mathrm { m L }$ of ${ \bf 5 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } M C d ^ { 2 + } }$ with 0.0100 M EDTA (a) at a pH of 10 and (b) at a pH of 7. Compare your sketches to the calculated titration curves from Exercise 9.3.1 . 

# Answer

The figure below shows a sketch of the titration curves. The two black points before the equivalence point $( V _ { \mathrm { E D T A } } = 5 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ , $\mathrm { p C d } \mathrm { = } 2 . 4 3$ and $V _ { \mathrm { E D T A } } = 1 5 \mathrm { m L }$ , $\mathbf { p C d } \mathbf { = } 2 . 8 1 $ ) are the same for both pHs and taken from the results of Exercise 9.3.1 . The two black points after the equivalence point for a $\mathrm { p H }$ of 7 $\mathrm { \Delta V _ { E D T A } } = 2 7 . 5 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ , $\mathrm { p C d } = 1 2 . 2$ and $V _ { \mathrm { E D T A } } = 5 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ , $\mathrm { p C d } = 1 3 . 2 \rangle$ ) are plotted using the $\log K _ { f } ^ { \prime }$ of 13.2 for ${ \mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 - } } }$ . The two points after the equivalence point for a pH of 10 - $\mathrm { \Delta V _ { E D T A } } = 2 7 . 5 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ , $\mathrm { p C d } = 1 5 . 0$ andV = 50 mL, EDTA $\mathrm { p C d } = 1 6 . 0 $ ) are plotted using the $\log K _ { f } ^ { \prime }$ of 16.0 for ${ \mathrm { C d Y ^ { 2 } } } \cdot$ . The points in red are thecalculations from Exercise 9.3.1 for a $\mathrm { p H }$ of 10, and the points in green are the calculations from Exercise 9.3.1 for a pH of 7. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/5a411d7e4147eb0fd68c5cbf943a0f952eab2467e28816ea573b547481147af1.jpg)


# Selecting and Evaluating the Endpoint

The equivalence point of a complexation titration occurs when we react stoichiometrically equivalent amounts of the titrand and titrant. As is the case for an acid–base titration, we estimate the equivalence point for a complexation titration using an experimental end point. A variety of methods are available for locating the end point, including indicators and sensors that respond to a change in the solution conditions. 

# Finding the End Point with an Indicator

Most indicators for complexation titrations are organic dyes—known as metallochromic indicators—that form stable complexes with metal ions. The indicator, $\mathrm { I n } ^ { m - }$ , is added to the titrand’s solution where it forms a stable complex with the metal ion, $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { I n } ^ { n - }$ . As we add EDTA it reacts first with free metal ions, and then displaces the indicator from $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { I n } ^ { n - }$ . 

$$
\operatorname {M I n} ^ {n -} (a q) + \mathrm {Y} ^ {4 -} (a q) \rightarrow \mathrm {M Y} ^ {2 -} (a q) + \operatorname {I n} ^ {m -} (a q)
$$

If $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { I n } ^ { n - }$ and $\mathrm { I n } ^ { m - }$ have different colors, then the change in color signals the end point. 

The accuracy of an indicator’s end point depends on the strength of the metal–indicator complex relative to the strength of the metal–EDTA complex. If the metal–indicator complex is too strong, the change in color occurs after the equivalence point. If the metal–indicator complex is too weak, however, the end point occurs before we reach the equivalence point. 

Most metallochromic indicators also are weak acids. One consequence of this is that the conditional formation constant for the metal–indicator complex depends on the titrand’s pH. This provides some control over an indicator’s titration error because we can adjust the strength of a metal–indicator complex by adjusted the pH at which we carry out the titration. Unfortunately, because the indicator is a weak acid, the color of the uncomplexed indicator also may change with pH. Figure 9.3.5 , for example, shows the color of the indicator calmagite as a function of pH and pMg, where $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { I n } ^ { - }$ , $\mathrm { H I n } ^ { 2 - }$ , and $\mathrm { I n } ^ { 3 - }$ are different forms of the uncomplexed indicator, and $\mathrm { M g I n ^ { - } }$ is the $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + } .$ –calmagite complex. Because the color of calmagite’s metal–indicator complex is red, its use as a metallochromic indicator has a practical $\mathrm { p H }$ range of approximately 8.5–11 where the uncomplexed indicator, $\mathrm { H I n } ^ { 2 - }$ , has a blue color. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/dbcba581d9e63f2317e994f99a58952aa13e69a2b4457d91489c8637451d2994.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/879f107f27680ccfed862c97977c46f410abc00414ab648c5f3b329599ed0142.jpg)



Figure 9.3.5 . (a) Predominance diagram for the metallochromic indicator calmagite showing the most important forms and colors of calmagite as a function of pH and pMg, where $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { I n } ^ { - }$ , $\mathrm { H I n } ^ { 2 - }$ , and $\mathrm { I n } ^ { 3 - }$ are uncomplexed forms of calmagite, and MgIn is its– complex with $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ . Conditions to the right of the dashed line, where $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ precipitates as $\mathrm { M g ( O H ) } _ { 2 }$ , are not analytically useful for a complexation titration. A red to blue end point is possible if we maintain the titrand’s pH in the range 8.5–11. (b) Diagram showing the relationship between the concentration of $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ (as pMg) and the indicator’s color. The ladder diagram defines pMg values where MgIn and– $\mathrm { H I n } ^ { 2 - }$ are predominate species. The indicator changes color when pMg is between $\mathrm { l o g } K _ { \mathrm { f } } - 1$ and $\mathrm { l o g } K _ { \mathrm { f } } + 1$ . This figure is essentially a two-variable ladder diagram. The solid lines are equivalent to a step on a conventional ladder diagram, indicating conditions where two (or three) species are equal in concentration.


Table 9.3.5 provides examples of metallochromic indicators and the metal ions and pH conditions for which they are useful. Even if a suitable indicator does not exist, it often is possible to complete an EDTA titration by introducing a small amount of a secondary metal–EDTA complex if the secondary metal ion forms a stronger complex with the indicator and a weaker complex with EDTA than the analyte. For example, calmagite has a poor end point when titrating $\mathsf { C a ^ { 2 + } }$ with EDTA. Adding a small amount of $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + } .$ –EDTA to the titrand gives a sharper end point. Because $\mathsf { C a ^ { 2 + } }$ forms a stronger complex with EDTA, it displaces $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ , which then forms the red-colored $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + } .$ –calmagite complex. At the titration’s end point, EDTA displaces $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ from the $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + } .$ – calmagite complex, signaling the end point by the presence of the uncomplexed indicator’s blue form. 


Table 9.3.5 . Selected Metallochromic Indicators


<table><tr><td>indicator</td><td>pH range</td><td>metal ions</td><td>indicator</td><td>pH range</td><td>metal ions</td></tr><tr><td>calmagite</td><td>8.5–11</td><td>Ba, Ca, Mg, Zn</td><td>eriochrome Black T</td><td>7.5–10.5</td><td>Ba, Ca, Mg, Zn</td></tr><tr><td>eriochrome Blue Black R</td><td>8–12</td><td>Ca, Mg, Zn, Cu</td><td>PAN</td><td>2–11</td><td>Cd, Cu, Zn</td></tr><tr><td>murexide</td><td>6–13</td><td>Ca, Ni, Cu</td><td>salicylic acid</td><td>2–3</td><td>Fe</td></tr><tr><td colspan="6">all metal ions carry a +2 charge expect for iron, which is +3 
metal ions in italic font have poor end points</td></tr></table>

# Finding the End Point By Monitoring Absorbance

An important limitation when using a metallochromic indicator is that we must be able to see the indicator’s change in color at the end point. This may be difficult if the solution is already colored. For example, when titrating ${ \mathrm { C u } } ^ { 2 + }$ with EDTA, ammonia is used to adjust the titrand’s pH. The intensely colored $\mathrm { C u } ( \mathrm { N H } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { 4 + }$ complex obscures the indicator’s color, making an accurate determination of the end point difficult. Other absorbing species present within the sample matrix may also interfere. This often is a problem when analyzing clinical samples, such as blood, or environmental samples, such as natural waters. 

If at least one species in a complexation titration absorbs electromagnet- ic radiation, then we can identify the end point by monitoring the titrand’s absorbance at a carefully selected wavelength. For example, we can identify the end point for a titration of $\mathrm { C u } ^ { 2 + }$ with EDTA in the presence of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ by monitoring the titrand’s absorbance at a wavelength of $7 4 5 ~ \mathrm { n m }$ , where the $\mathrm { C u } ( \mathrm { N H } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { 4 + }$ complex absorbs strongly. At the beginning of the titration the absorbance is at a maximum. As we add EDTA, however, the reaction 

$$
\mathrm {C u} \left(\mathrm {N H} _ {3}\right) _ {4} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \mathrm {Y} ^ {4 -} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {C u Y} ^ {2 -} (a q) + 4 \mathrm {N H} _ {3} (a q)
$$

decreases the concentration of $\mathrm { C u } ( \mathrm { N H } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 } ^ { 4 + }$ and decreases the absorbance until we reach the equivalence point. After the equivalence point the absorbance essentially remains unchanged. The resulting spectrophotometric titration curve is shown in Figure 9.3.6 a. 

Note that the titration curve’s y-axis is not the measured absorbance, $A _ { \mathrm { { m e a s } } }$ , but a corrected absorbance, $A _ { \mathrm { c o r r } }$ 

$$
A _ {\text {c o r r}} = A _ {\text {m e a s}} \times \frac {V _ {\mathrm {E D T A}} + V _ {\mathrm {C u}}}{V _ {\mathrm {C u}}}
$$

where $V _ { \mathrm { E D T A } }$ and $V _ { \mathrm { C u } }$ are, respectively, the volumes of EDTA and Cu. Correcting the absorbance for the titrand’s dilution ensures that the spectrophotometric titration curve consists of linear segments that we can extrapolate to find the end point. Other common spectrophotometric titration curves are shown in Figures 9.3.6 b-f. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/b3f6b3fa4c74c2e0a93eacee6af45347ad535c7b9f02fcfaca3796cdc353b4ed.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/8de64f2fd1c2c43af53eadcf8e5b9ed9418be7d7696464f3a02ab900c2309b1c.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/7c1978544d0d861589e56d09eb04ee4be8d7731611c7b58c130ad32e8343e849.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/1a05386cc2a117bdc44577fabb8b0e6f2768b0812ec1381890d8c06bc82660b5.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/5c96df5be6c62b3092d9128f1941229861cffd8521b2f4f8471bc5f8d6270bb4.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/c8b51aa3691f9eb296ce051fc167c138129ab1c60d49f00cb6592770559818c4.jpg)



Figure 9.3.6 . Examples of spectrophotometric titration curves: (a) only the titrand absorbs; (b) only the titrant absorbs; (c) only the product of the titration reaction absorbs; (d) both the titrand and the titrant absorb; (e) both the titration reaction’s product and the titrant absorb; (f ) only the indicator absorbs. The red arrows indicate the end points for each titration curve.


# Representative Method 9.3.1: Determination of Hardness of Water and Wastewater

The best way to appreciate the theoretical and the practical details discussed in this section is to carefully examine a typical complexation titrimetric method. Although each method is unique, the following description of the determination of the hardness of water provides an instructive example of a typical procedure. The description here is based on Method 2340C as published in Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater, 20th Ed., American Public Health Association: Washington, D. C., 1998. 

# Description of the Method

The operational definition of water hardness is the total concentration of cations in a sample that can form an insoluble complex with soap. Although most divalent and trivalent metal ions contribute to hardness, the two most important metal ions are $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ . Hardness is determined by titrating with EDTA at a buffered pH of 10. Calmagite is used as an indicator. Hardness is reported as $\mathrm { m g \ C a C O _ { 3 } / L }$ . 

# Procedure

Select a volume of sample that requires less than $1 5 \mathrm { m L }$ of titrant to keep the analysis time under 5 minutes and, if necessary, dilute the sample to $5 0 \mathrm { m L }$ with distilled water. Adjust the sample’s pH by adding 1–2 mL of a pH 10 buffer that contains a small amount of $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + } .$ –EDTA. Add 1–2 drops of indicator and titrate with a standard solution of EDTA until the red-to-blue end point is reached (Figure 9.3.7 ). 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/73b0147bed028258a2be0bfe120e94d2f9da2f0238407cfc6fd3e5f64ec97acf.jpg)



Figure 9.3.7 . End point for the titration of hardness with EDTA using calmagite as an indicator; the indicator is: (a) red prior to the end point due to the presence of the $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ –indicator complex; (b) purple at the titration’s end point; and (c) blue after the end point due to the presence of uncomplexed indicator.


# Questions

1. Why is the sample buffered to a pH of 10? What problems might you expect at a higher pH or a lower pH? 

Of the two primary cations that contribute to hardness, $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ forms the weaker complex with EDTA and is the last cation to react with the titrant. Calmagite is a useful indicator because it gives a distinct end point when titrating $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ (see Table 9.3.5 ). Because of calmagite’s acid–base properties, the range of $\mathsf { p M g }$ values over which the indicator changes color depends on the titrand’s pH (Figure 9.3.5 ). Figure 9.3.8 shows the titration curve for a 50-mL solution of $1 0 ^ { - 3 } { \mathrm { ~ M ~ M g } } ^ { 2 + }$ with $1 0 ^ { - 2 } \textbf { M }$ EDTA at pHs of 9, 10, and 11. Superimposed on each titration curve is the range of conditions for which the average analyst will observe the end point. At a pH of 9 an early end point is possible, which results in a negative determinate error. A late end point and a positive determinate error are possible if the pH is 11. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/6ffd53642cf2e4e7d2340f5d2b99a342e6956e6e24a2ada0a4a2f75df2ca1208.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/4e6e7e6b2b87f011fa79687ec24953cc5338fe59cd70b646dce187024a3f4033.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/5f58106a9c9d6ad4849dd697f39340019e825c9f899331802cf3c8e45160fe57.jpg)



Figure 9.3.8 . Titration curves for $5 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of $1 0 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { M } \mathrm { M } \mathrm { g } ^ { 2 + }$ with $1 0 ^ { - 3 } \mathbf { M }$ EDTA at pHs 9, 10, and 11 using calmagite as an indicator. The range of pMg and volume of EDTA over which the indicator changes color is shown for each titration curve.


2. Why is a small amount of the $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + } .$ –EDTA complex added to the buffer? 

The titration’s end point is signaled by the indicator calmagite. The indicator’s end point with $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ is distinct, but its change in color when titrating $\mathrm { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ does not provide a good end point (see Table 9.3.5 ). If the sample does not contain any $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ as a source of hardness, then the titration’s end point is poorly defined, which leads to an inaccurate and imprecise result. Adding a small amount of $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + } .$ –EDTA to the buffer ensures that the titrand includes at least some $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ . Because $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ forms a stronger complex with EDTA, it displaces $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ from the $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + } .$ –EDTA complex, freeing the $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ to bind with the indicator. This displacement is stoichiometric, so the total concentration of hardness cations remains unchanged. The displacement by EDTA of $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ from the $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + } .$ –indicator complex signals the titration’s end point. 

3. Why does the procedure specify that the titration take no longer than 5 minutes? 

A time limitation suggests there is a kinetically-controlled interference, possibly arising from a competing chemical reaction. In this case the interference is the possible precipitation of $\mathrm { C a C O } _ { 3 }$ at a pH of 10. 

# Quantitative Applications

Although many quantitative applications of complexation titrimetry have been replaced by other analytical methods, a few important applications continue to find relevance. In the section we review the general application of complexation titrimetry with 

an emphasis on applications from the analysis of water and wastewater. First, however, we discuss the selection and standardization of complexation titrants. 

# Selection and Standardization of Titrants

EDTA is a versatile titrant that can be used to analyze virtually all metal ions. Although EDTA is the usual titrant when the titrand is a metal ion, it cannot be used to titrate anions, for which ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ or $\mathrm { H g } ^ { 2 + }$ are suitable titrants. 

Solutions of EDTA are prepared from its soluble disodium salt, $\mathrm { N a _ { 2 } H _ { 2 } Y \bullet 2 H _ { 2 } O }$ , and standardized by titrating against a solution made from the primary standard $\mathrm { C a C O } _ { 3 }$ . Solutions of ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { H g } ^ { 2 + }$ are prepared using $\mathsf { A g N O } _ { 3 }$ and $\mathrm { H g } ( \mathrm { N O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ , both of which are secondary standards. Standardization is accomplished by titrating against a solution prepared from primary standard grade NaCl. 

# Inorganic Analytes

Complexation titrimetry continues to be listed as a standard method for the determination of hardness, $\mathsf { C a ^ { 2 + } }$ , $\mathrm { C N ^ { - } }$ , and $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ in waters and wastewaters. The evaluation of hardness was described earlier in Representative Method 9.3.1. The determination of $\mathrm { C a ^ { 2 + } }$ is complicated by the presence of $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ , which also reacts with EDTA. To prevent an interference the pH is adjusted to 12–13, which precipitates $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ as $\mathrm { M g ( O H ) } _ { 2 }$ . Titrating with EDTA using murexide or Eriochrome Blue Black R as the indicator gives the concentration of $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ . 

Cyanide is determined at concentrations greater than $1 \ \mathrm { m g / L }$ by making the sample alkaline with NaOH and titrating with a standard solution of $\mathrm { A g N O } _ { 3 }$ to form the soluble $\mathbf { A g ( C N ) _ { 2 } ^ { - } }$ complex. The end point is determined using $p \cdot$ - dimethylaminobenzalrhodamine as an indicator, with the solution turning from a yellow to a salmon color in the presence of excess ${ \bf A } { \bf g } ^ { + }$ . 

Chloride is determined by titrating with $\mathrm { H g } ( \mathrm { N O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ , forming $\mathrm { H g C l } _ { 2 } ( a q )$ . The sample is acidified to a pH of 2.3–3.8 and diphenylcarbazone, which forms a colored complex with excess $\mathrm { H g } ^ { 2 + }$ , serves as the indicator. The pH indicator xylene cyanol FF is added to ensure that the pH is within the desired range. The initial solution is a greenish blue, and the titration is carried out to a purple end point. 

# Quantitative Calculations

The quantitative relationship between the titrand and the titrant is determined by the titration reaction’s stoichiometry. For a titration using EDTA, the stoichiometry is always 1:1. 

# Example 9.3.1

The concentration of a solution of EDTA is determined by standardizing against a solution of $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ prepared using a primary standard of $\mathrm { C a C O } _ { 3 }$ . A 0.4071-g sample of $\mathrm { C a C O } _ { 3 }$ is transferred to a $5 0 0 { \cdot } \mathrm { m L }$ volumetric flask, dissolved using a minimum of 6 M HCl, and diluted to volume. After transferring a $5 0 . 0 0 { \cdot } \mathrm { m L }$ portion of this solution to a 250-mL Erlenmeyer flask, the pH is adjusted by adding 5 mL of a $\mathrm { p H } 1 0 \ \mathrm { N H _ { 3 } } – \mathrm { N H _ { 4 } C l }$ buffer that contains a small amount of $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + } .$ –EDTA. After adding calmagite as an indicator, the solution is titrated with the EDTA, requiring $4 2 . 6 3 \mathrm { \ m L }$ to reach the end point. Report the molar concentration of EDTA in the titrant. 

# Solution

The primary standard of $\mathrm { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ has a concentration of 

$$
\frac {0 . 4 0 7 1 \mathrm {g C a C O} _ {3}}{0 . 5 0 0 0 \mathrm {L}} \times \frac {1 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C a} ^ {2 +}}{1 0 0 . 0 9 \mathrm {g C a C O} _ {3}} = 8. 1 3 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M C a} ^ {2 +}
$$

The moles of $\mathrm { C a ^ { 2 + } }$ in the titrand is 

$$
8. 1 3 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M C a} ^ {2 +} \times 0. 0 5 0 0 0 \mathrm {L} = 4. 0 6 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C a} ^ {2 +}
$$

which means that $\pm . 0 6 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 }$ moles of EDTA are used in the titration. The molarity of EDTA in the titrant is 

$$
\frac {4 . 0 6 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C a} ^ {2 +}}{0 . 0 4 2 6 3 \mathrm {L}} = 9. 5 4 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M E D T A}
$$

#  Exercise 9.3.3

A 100.0-mL sample is analyzed for hardness using the procedure outlined in Representative Method 9.3.1, requiring 23.63 mL of 0.0109 M EDTA. Report the sample’s hardness as $\mathrm { m g \ C a C O _ { 3 } / L }$ . 

# Answer

In an analysis for hardness we treat the sample as if $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ is the only metal ion that reacts with EDTA. The grams of $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ in the sample, therefore, are 

$$
\begin{array}{l} (0. 0 1 0 9 \mathrm {M E D T A}) (0. 0 2 3 6 3 \mathrm {L}) \times \frac {1 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C a} ^ {2 +}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}} = 2. 5 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C a} ^ {2 +} \\ 2. 5 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l C a ^ {2 +}} \times \frac {1 \mathrm {m o l C a C O _ {3}}}{\mathrm {m o l C a ^ {2 +}}} \times \frac {1 0 0 . 0 9 \mathrm {g C a C O _ {3}}}{\mathrm {m o l C a C O _ {3}}} = 0. 0 2 5 8 \mathrm {g C a C O _ {3}} \\ \end{array}
$$

and the sample’s hardness is 

$$
\frac {0 . 0 2 5 8 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {C a C O} _ {3}}{0 . 1 0 0 0 \mathrm {L}} \times \frac {1 0 0 0 \mathrm {m g}}{\mathrm {g}} = 2 5 8 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {C a C O} _ {3} / \mathrm {L}
$$

As shown in the following example, we can extended this calculation to complexation reactions that use other titrants. 

#  Example 9.3.2

The concentration of $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ in a $1 0 0 . 0 { \cdot } \mathrm { m L }$ sample of water from a freshwater aquifer is tested for the encroachment of sea water by titrating with 0.0516 M $\mathrm { H g } ( \mathrm { N O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ . The sample is acidified and titrated to the diphenylcarbazone end point, requiring 6.18 mL of the titrant. Report the concentration of $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ , in $\mathrm { m g / L }$ , in the aquifer. 

# Solution

The reaction between $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ and $\mathrm { H g } ^ { 2 + }$ produces a metal–ligand complex of $\mathrm { H g C l } _ { 2 } ( a q )$ . Each mole of $\mathrm { H g } ^ { 2 } +$ reacts with 2 moles of $\Sigma ^ { \mathrm { { l ^ { - } } } }$ ; thus 

$$
\frac {0 . 0 5 1 6 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {H g} (\mathrm {N O} _ {3}) _ {2}}{\mathrm {L}} \times 0. 0 0 6 1 8 \mathrm {L} \times \frac {2 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C l} ^ {-}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {H g} (\mathrm {N O} _ {3}) _ {2}} \times \frac {3 5 . 4 5 3 \mathrm {g C l} ^ {-}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C l} ^ {-}} = 0. 0 2 2 6 \mathrm {g C l} ^ {-}
$$

are in the sample. The concentration of $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ in the sample is 

$$
\frac {0 . 0 2 2 6 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {C l} ^ {-}}{0 . 1 0 0 0 \mathrm {L}} \times \frac {1 0 0 0 \mathrm {m g}}{\mathrm {g}} = 2 2 6 \mathrm {m g} / \mathrm {L}
$$

#  Exercise 9.3.4

A 0.4482-g sample of impure NaCN is titrated with 0.1018 M AgNO , requiring 3 $3 9 . 6 8 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ to reach the end point. Report the purity of the sample as %w/w NaCN. 

# Answer

The titration of $\mathrm { C N ^ { - } }$ with ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ produces the metal-ligand complex $\mathbf { A g ( C N ) } _ { 2 } ^ { - }$ ; thus, each mole of ${ \tt A g N O } _ { 3 }$ reacts with two moles of NaCN. The grams of NaCN in the sample is 

$$
(0. 1 0 1 8 \mathrm {M} \mathrm {A g N O} _ {3}) (0. 0 3 9 6 8 \mathrm {L}) \times \frac {2 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {N a C N}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {A g N O} _ {3}} \times \frac {4 9 . 0 1 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {N a C N}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {N a C N}} = 0. 3 9 5 9 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {N a C N}
$$

and the purity of the sample is 

$$
\frac {0 . 3 9 5 9 \mathrm {g N a C N}}{0 . 4 4 8 2 \mathrm {g s a m p l e}} \times 1 0 0 = 8 8. 3 3
$$

Finally, complex titrations involving multiple analytes or back titrations are possible. 

#  Example 9.3.3

An alloy of chromel that contains Ni, Fe, and Cr is analyzed by a complexation titration using EDTA as the titrant. A 0.7176-g sample of the alloy is dissolved in $\mathrm { H N O } _ { 3 }$ and diluted to $2 5 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ in a volumetric flask. A 50.00-mL aliquot of the sample, treated with pyrophosphate to mask the Fe and Cr, requires 26.14 mL of 0.05831 M EDTA to reach the murexide end point. A second 50.00-mL aliquot is treated with hexamethylenetetramine to mask the Cr. Titrating with 0.05831 M EDTA requires 35.43 mL to reach the murexide end point. Finally, a third 50.00-mL aliquot is treated with 50.00 mL of 0.05831 M EDTA, and back titrated to the murexide end point with $6 . 2 1 \ \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.06316 M $\mathrm { C u } ^ { 2 + }$ . Report the weight percents of Ni, Fe, and Cr in the alloy. 

# Solution

The stoichiometry between EDTA and each metal ion is 1:1. For each of the three titrations, therefore, we can write an equation that relates the moles of EDTA to the moles of metal ions that are titrated. 

titration 1: mol $\mathrm { N i } = \mathrm { m o l }$ EDTA 

titration 2: mol Ni +mol Fe = mol EDTA 

titration 3: mol Ni + mol Fe + mol Cr + mol Cu = mol EDTA 

We use the first titration to determine the moles of Ni in our $5 0 . 0 0 { \cdot } \mathrm { m L }$ portion of the dissolved alloy. The titration uses 

$$
\frac {0 . 0 5 8 3 1 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}}{\mathrm {L}} \times 0. 0 2 6 1 4 \mathrm {L} = 1. 5 2 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}
$$

which means the sample contains $\mathbf { 1 . 5 2 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } }$ mol Ni. 

Having determined the moles of EDTA that react with Ni, we use the second titration to determine the amount of Fe in the sample. The second titration uses 

$$
\frac {0 . 0 5 8 3 1 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}}{\mathrm {L}} \times 0. 0 3 5 4 3 \mathrm {L} = 2. 0 6 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}
$$

of which $\mathbf { 1 . 5 2 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } }$ mol are used to titrate Ni. This leaves $\mathbf { 5 . 4 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 } }$ mol of EDTA to react with Fe; thus, the sample contains $\mathbf { 5 . 4 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 } m o l }$ of Fe. 

Finally, we can use the third titration to determine the amount of Cr in the alloy. The third titration uses 

$$
\frac {0 . 0 5 8 3 1 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}}{\mathrm {L}} \times 0. 0 5 0 0 \mathrm {L} = 3. 9 2 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}
$$

of which $1 . 5 2 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 }$ mol are used to titrate Ni and ${ \bf 5 . 4 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 } }$ mol are used to titrate Fe. This leaves $8 . 5 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 }$ mol of EDTA to react with Cu and Cr. The amount of EDTA that reacts with Cu is 

$$
\frac {0 . 0 6 3 1 6 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C u} ^ {2 +}}{\mathrm {L}} \times 0. 0 0 6 2 1 1 \mathrm {L} \times \frac {1 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C u} ^ {2 +}} = 3. 9 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}
$$

leaving $\phantom { + } 4 . 5 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m o l }$ of EDTA to react with Cr. The sample, therefore, contains $4 . 5 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 }$ mol of Cr. 

Having determined the moles of Ni, Fe, and Cr in a $5 0 . 0 0 { \cdot } \mathrm { m L }$ portion of the dissolved alloy, we can calculate the %w/w of each analyte in the alloy. 

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {1 . 5 2 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l N i}}{5 0 . 0 0 \mathrm {m L}} \times \frac {5 8 . 6 9 \mathrm {g N i}}{\mathrm {m o l N i}} = 0. 4 4 7 2 \mathrm {g N i} \\ \frac {0 . 4 4 7 2 \mathrm {g N i}}{0 . 7 1 7 6 \mathrm {g s a m p l e}} \times 1 0 0 = 6 2. 3 2 \\ \frac {5 . 4 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {F e}}{5 0 . 0 0 \mathrm {m L}} \times \frac {5 5 . 8 4 5 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {F e}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {F e}} = 0. 1 5 1 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {F e} \\ \frac {0 . 1 5 1 \mathrm {g F e}}{0 . 7 1 7 6 \mathrm {g s a m p l e}} \times 1 0 0 = 2 1. 0 \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {4 . 5 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l C r}}{5 0 . 0 0 \mathrm {m L}} \times \frac {5 1 . 9 9 6 \mathrm {g C r}}{\mathrm {m o l C r}} = 0. 1 1 9 \mathrm {g C r} \\ \frac {0 . 1 1 9 \mathrm {g C r}}{0 . 7 1 7 6 \mathrm {g s a m p l e}} \times 1 0 0 = 1 6. 6 \\ \end{array}
$$

#  Exercise 9.3.5

An indirect complexation titration with EDTA can be used to determine the concentration of sulfate, $\mathsf { S O } _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - }$ , in a sample. A 0.1557-g sample is dissolved in water and any sulfate present is precipitated as $\mathrm { B a S O _ { 4 } }$ by adding $\mathrm { B a } ( \mathrm { N O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ . After filtering and rinsing the precipitate, it is dissolved in $2 5 . 0 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.02011 M EDTA. The excess EDTA is titrated with $0 . 0 1 1 1 3 \mathrm { ~ M ~ M ~ g ~ } ^ { 2 + }$ $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ , requiring 4.23 mL to reach the end point. Calculate the %w/w $\mathrm { N a } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S O } _ { 4 }$ in the sample. 

# Answer

The total moles of EDTA used in this analysis is 

$$
(0. 0 2 0 1 1 \mathrm {M E D T A}) (0. 0 2 5 0 0 \mathrm {L}) = 5. 0 2 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}
$$

Of this, 

$$
(0. 0 1 1 1 3 \mathrm {M} \mathrm {M g} ^ {2 +}) (0. 0 0 4 2 3 \mathrm {L}) \times \frac {1 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {M g} ^ {2 +}} = 4. 7 0 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {E D T A}
$$

are consumed in the back titration with $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ , which means that 

$$
5. 0 2 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l E D T A} - 4. 7 0 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \mathrm {m o l E D T A} = 4. 5 5 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l E D T A}
$$

react with the $\mathrm { B a S O _ { 4 } }$ . Each mole of $\mathrm { B a S O _ { 4 } }$ reacts with one mole of EDTA; thus 

$$
\begin{array}{l} 4. 5 5 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l E D T A} \times \frac {1 \mathrm {m o l B a S O} _ {4}}{\mathrm {m o l E D T A}} \times \frac {1 \mathrm {m o l N a} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {4}}{\mathrm {m o l B a S O} _ {4}} \times \frac {1 4 2 . 0 4 \mathrm {g N a} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {4}}{\mathrm {m o l N a} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {4}} = 0. 0 6 4 7 3 \mathrm {g N a} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {4} \\ \frac {0 . 0 6 4 7 3 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {N a} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {4}}{0 . 1 5 5 7 \mathrm {g} \text {s a m p l e}} \times 1 0 0 = 4 1. 5 7 _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {4} \\ \end{array}
$$

# Evaluation of Complexation Titrimetry

The scale of operations, accuracy, precision, sensitivity, time, and cost of a complexation titration are similar to those described earlier for acid–base titrations. Complexation titrations, however, are more selective. Although EDTA forms strong complexes with most metal ion, by carefully controlling the titrand’s pH we can analyze samples that contain two or more analytes. The reason we can use pH to provide selectivity is shown in Figure 9.3.9 a. A titration of $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ at a pH of 9 has a distinct break in the titration curve because the conditional formation constant for $\mathrm { C a Y ^ { 2 - } }$ of $\mathbf { 2 . 6 \times 1 0 ^ { 9 } }$ is large enough to ensure that the reaction of $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ and EDTA goes to completion. At a pH of 3, however, the conditional formation constant of 1.23 is so small that very little $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ reacts with the EDTA. Suppose we need to analyze a mixture of $\mathrm { N i } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ . Both analytes react with EDTA, but their conditional formation constants differ significantly. If we adjust the pH to 3 we can titrate $\mathrm { N i } ^ { 2 + }$ with EDTA without titrating $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ (Figure 9.3.9 b). When the titration is complete, we adjust the titrand’s pH to 9 and titrate the $\mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ with EDTA. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/999f3d64f0ef27258858c85960d56feb01d2740e7f09be9b3edeedbbc92f8e2c.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/5eeeb7cd2806a88540b7b6a2b2b186bc1933b811e634d707be5032a7d6f2e0b3.jpg)



Figure 9.3.9 . Titration curves illustrating how we can use the titrand’s pH to control EDTA’s selectivity. (a) Titration of $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of $0 . { \overset { - } { 0 } } 1 0  { \mathrm { ~ M ~ C a } } ^ { 2 + }$ at a pH of 3 and a pH of 9 using 0.010 M EDTA. At a pH of 3 the $\mathrm { C a Y ^ { 2 - } }$ complex is too weak to titrate successfully. (b) Titration of a 50.0 mL mixture of $0 . 0 1 0 \bar { \mathrm { M } } \mathsf { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ and $0 . 0 1 0 \mathrm { M N i ^ { 2 \bar { + } } }$ at a pH of 3 and at a pH of 9 using 0.010 M EDTA. At a pH of 3 EDTA reacts only with $\mathrm { N i } ^ { 2 + }$ . When the titration is complete, raising the pH to 9 allows for the titration of $\mathrm { C a } ^ { 2 + }$ .


A spectrophotometric titration is a particularly useful approach for analyzing a mixture of analytes. For example, as shown in Figure 9.3.10 , we can determine the concentration of a two metal ions if there is a difference between the absorbance of the two metal-ligand complexes. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/5d20ff83ea1dad9329833676e6059a19a2cd00f95bea3cc0752c4682dfc7ac87.jpg)



Figure 9.3.10 . Spectrophotometric titration curve for the complexation titration of a mixture of two analytes. The red arrows indicate the end points for each analyte.

# 9.4: Redox Titrations

Analytical titrations using oxidation–reduction reactions were introduced shortly after the development of acid–base titrimetry. The earliest redox titration took advantage of chlorine’s oxidizing power. In 1787, Claude Berthollet introduced a method for the quantitative analysis of chlorine water (a mixture of $\mathrm { C l } _ { 2 }$ , HCl, and HOCl) based on its ability to oxidize indigo, a dye that is colorless in its oxidized state. In 1814, Joseph Gay-Lussac developed a similar method to determine chlorine in bleaching powder. In both methods the end point is a change in color. Before the equivalence point the solution is colorless due to the oxidation of indigo. After the equivalence point, however, unreacted indigo imparts a permanent color to the solution. 

The number of redox titrimetric methods increased in the mid-1800s with the introduction of $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ , $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } 0 _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ , and $\mathrm { I } _ { 2 }$ as oxidizing titrants, and of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathsf { S _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - } }$ as reducing titrants. Even with the availability of these new titrants, redox titrimetry was slow to develop due to the lack of suitable indicators. A titrant can serve as its own indicator if its oxidized and its reduced forms differ significantly in color. For example, the intensely purple $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ ion serves as its own indicator since its reduced form, $\mathrm { M n } ^ { 2 + }$ , is almost colorless. Other titrants require a separate indicator. The first such indicator, diphenylamine, was introduced in the 1920s. Other redox indicators soon followed, increasing the applicability of redox titrimetry. 

# Redox Titration Curves

To evaluate a redox titration we need to know the shape of its titration curve. In an acid–base titration or a complexation titration, the titration curve shows how the concentration of $\mathrm { H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { O } ^ { + }$ (as pH) or $\mathbf { M } ^ { n + }$ (as pM) changes as we add titrant. For a redox titration it is convenient to monitor the titration reaction’s potential instead of the concentration of one species. 

You may recall from Chapter 6 that the Nernst equation relates a solution’s potential to the concentrations of reactants and products that participate in the redox reaction. Consider, for example, a titration in which a titrand in a reduced state, $A _ { r e d } .$ reacts with a titrant in an oxidized state, $B _ { o x }$ . 

$$
A _ {r e d} + B _ {o x} \rightleftharpoons B _ {r e d} + A _ {o x}
$$

where $A _ { o x }$ is the titrand’s oxidized form, $B _ { r e d }$ is the titrant’s reduced form, and the stoichiometry between the two is 1:1. The reaction’s potential, $E _ { \mathrm { r x n } } ,$ is the difference between the reduction potentials for each half-reaction. 

$$
E _ {r x n} = E _ {B _ {o z} / B _ {r e d}} - E _ {A _ {o z} / A _ {r e d}}
$$

After each addition of titrant the reaction between the titrand and the titrant reaches a state of equilibrium. Because the potential at equilibrium is zero, the titrand’s and the titrant’s reduction potentials are identical. 

$$
E _ {B _ {\text {o s}} / B _ {\text {r e d}}} = E _ {A _ {\text {o s}} / A _ {\text {r e d}}}
$$

This is an important observation as it allows us to use either half-reaction to monitor the titration’s progress. 

Before the equivalence point the titration mixture consists of appreciable quantities of the titrand’s oxidized and reduced forms. The concentration of unreacted titrant, however, is very small. The potential, therefore, is easier to calculate if we use the Nernst equation for the titrand’s half-reaction 

$$
E = E _ {A _ {o x} / A _ {r e d}} = E _ {A _ {o x} / A _ {r e d}} ^ {\circ} - \frac {R T}{n F} \ln \frac {[ A _ {r e d} ]}{[ A _ {o x} ]}
$$

After the equivalence point it is easier to calculate the potential using the Nernst equation for the titrant’s half-reaction. 

$$
E = E _ {B _ {o x} / B _ {r e d}} = E _ {B _ {o x} / B _ {r e d}} ^ {\circ} - \frac {R T}{n F} \ln \frac {[ B _ {r e d} ]}{[ B _ {o x} ]}
$$

Although the Nernst equation is written in terms of the half-reaction’s standard state potential, a matrix-dependent formal potential often is used in its place. See Appendix 13 for the standard state potentials and formal potentials for selected halfreactions. 

# Calculating the Titration Curve

Let’s calculate the titration curve for the titration of $5 0 . 0 \ \mathrm { m L }$ of $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ F e } ^ { 2 + }$ with $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { M } \mathrm { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ in a matrix of 1 M HClO . The4 reaction in this case is 

$$
\mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} (a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +} (a q) + \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} (a q) \tag {9.4.1}
$$

Because the equilibrium constant for reaction is very large—it is approximately ${ \bf 6 } \times { \bf 1 0 ^ { 1 5 } }$ —we may assume that the analyte and titrant react completely. 

I $\mathrm { 1 1 M H C l O _ { 4 } }$ , the formal potential for the reduction of $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ to $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ is $+ 0 . 7 6 7 \mathrm { V } ,$ and the formal potential for the reduction of $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ to $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 3 + }$ is $+ 1 . 7 0 \mathrm { V } .$ 

The first task is to calculate the volume of Ce needed to reach the titration’s equivalence point. From the reaction’s stoichiometry we know that 

$$
\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} = M _ {\mathrm {F e}} V _ {\mathrm {F e}} = M _ {\mathrm {C e}} V _ {\mathrm {C e}} = \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +}
$$

Solving for the volume of $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ gives the equivalence point volume as 

$$
V _ {e q} = V _ {\mathrm {C e}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {F e}} V _ {\mathrm {F e}}}{M _ {\mathrm {C e}}} = \frac {(0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{(0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M})} = 5 0. 0 \mathrm {m L}
$$

Before the equivalence point, the concentration of unreacted $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ and the concentration of $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ are easy to calculate. For this reason we find the potential using the Nernst equation for the $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 3 + } / \mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ half-reaction. 

$$
E = + 0. 7 6 7 \mathrm {V} - 0. 0 5 9 1 6 \log \frac {\left[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} \right]} \tag {9.4.2}
$$

For example, the concentrations of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ after adding $1 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of titrant are 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} \right] = \frac {\left(\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}\right) _ {\text {i n i t i a l}} - \left(\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +}\right) _ {\text {a d d e d}}}{\text {t o t a l v o l u m e}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {F e}} V _ {\mathrm {F e}} - M _ {\mathrm {C e}} V _ {\mathrm {C e}}}{V _ {\mathrm {F e}} + V _ {\mathrm {C e}}}
$$

$$
[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} ] = \frac {(0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}) - (0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 6. 6 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {M}
$$

$$
\left[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} \right] = \frac {\left(\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +}\right) _ {\text {a d d e d}}}{\text {t o t a l v o l u m e}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {C e}} V _ {\mathrm {C e}}}{V _ {\mathrm {F e}} + V _ {\mathrm {C e}}}
$$

$$
[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} ] = \frac {(0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 1. 6 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {M}
$$

Substituting these concentrations into Equation gives the potential as 

$$
E = + 0. 7 6 7 \mathrm {V} - 0. 0 5 9 1 6 \log \frac {6 . 6 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2}}{1 . 6 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2}} = + 0. 7 3 1 \mathrm {V}
$$

After the equivalence point, the concentration of $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 3 + }$ and the concentration of excess $\mathsf { C e ^ { 4 + } }$ are easy to calculate. For this reason we find the potential using the Nernst equation for the $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 4 + } / \mathsf { C e } ^ { 3 + }$ half-reaction in a manner similar to that used above to calculate potentials before the equivalence point. 

$$
E = + 1. 7 0 \mathrm {V} - 0. 0 5 9 1 6 \log \frac {\left[ \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +} \right]}{\left[ \mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} \right]} \tag {9.4.3}
$$

For example, after adding $6 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of titrant, the concentrations of $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 3 + }$ and $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ are 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +} \right] = \frac {\left(\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}\right) _ {\text {i n i t i a l}}}{\text {t o t a l v o l u m e}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {F e}} V _ {\mathrm {F e}}}{V _ {\mathrm {F e}} + V _ {\mathrm {C e}}}
$$

$$
[ \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +} ] = \frac {(0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 6 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 4. 5 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {M}
$$

$$
[ \mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} ] = \frac {(\mathrm {m o l C e} ^ {4 +}) _ {\mathrm {a d d e d}} - (\mathrm {m o l F e} ^ {2 +}) _ {\mathrm {i n i t i a l}}}{\mathrm {t o t a l v o l u m e}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {C e}} V _ {\mathrm {C e}} - M _ {\mathrm {F e}} V _ {\mathrm {F e}}}{V _ {\mathrm {F e}} + V _ {\mathrm {C e}}}
$$

$$
[ \mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} ] = \frac {(0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (6 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}) - (0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 6 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 9. 0 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}
$$

Substituting these concentrations into Equation gives a potential of 

$$
E = + 1. 7 0 \mathrm {V} - 0. 0 5 9 1 6 \log \frac {4 . 5 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {M}}{9 . 0 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {M}} = + 1. 6 6 \mathrm {V}
$$

At the titration’s equivalence point, the potential, $E _ { \mathrm { e q } } ,$ in Equation and Equation are identical. Adding the equations together to gives 

$$
2 E _ {e q} = E _ {\mathrm {F e ^ {3 +} / F e ^ {2 +}}} ^ {\circ} + E _ {\mathrm {C e ^ {4 +} / C e ^ {3 +}}} ^ {\circ} - 0. 0 5 9 1 6 \log \frac {[ \mathrm {F e ^ {2 +}} ] [ \mathrm {C e ^ {3 +}} ]}{[ \mathrm {F e ^ {3 +}} ] [ \mathrm {C e ^ {4 +}} ]}
$$

Because $[ \mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + } ] = [ \mathrm { C e } ^ { 4 + } ]$ and $[ \mathsf { C e } ^ { 3 + } ] = [ \mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + } ]$ at the equivalence point, the log term has a value of zero and the equivalence point’s potential is 

$$
E _ {e q} = \frac {E _ {\mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} / \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}} ^ {\circ} + E _ {\mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} / \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +}} ^ {\circ}}{2} = \frac {0 . 7 6 7 \mathrm {V} + 1 . 7 0 \mathrm {V}}{2} = + 1. 2 3 \mathrm {V}
$$

Additional results for this titration curve are shown in Table 9.4.1 and Figure 9.4.1 . 


Table 9.4.1 . Data for the Titration of $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.100 M with 0.100 M


<table><tr><td>volume of Ce4+(mL)</td><td>E (V)</td><td>volume of Ce4+(mL)</td><td>E (V)</td></tr><tr><td>10.0</td><td>0.731</td><td>60.0</td><td>1.66</td></tr><tr><td>20.0</td><td>0.757</td><td>70.0</td><td>1.68</td></tr><tr><td>30.0</td><td>0.777</td><td>80.0</td><td>1.69</td></tr><tr><td>40.0</td><td>0.803</td><td>90.0</td><td>1.69</td></tr><tr><td>50.0</td><td>1.23</td><td>100.0</td><td>1.70</td></tr></table>

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/ee6e341310a37a22a1700c0b8429a80098efc4e1149c59f9d2a55b8f7af0e74d.jpg)



Figure 9.4.1 . Titration curve for the titration of $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ F e } ^ { 2 + }$ with $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { M } \mathrm { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ . The red points correspond to the data in Table 9.4.1 . The blue line shows the complete titration curve.


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/4c82f7bc31dfbf28296348282ea7d74b4812a25c4c8d270196ab15ffec10d72c.jpg)


# Exercise 9.4.1

Calculate the titration curve for the titration of $5 0 . 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.0500 M $S \mathrm { n } ^ { 2 + }$ with $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ T l } ^ { 3 + }$ . Both the titrand and the titrant are $1 . 0 \mathbf { M }$ in HCl. The titration reaction is 

$$
\operatorname {S n} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \operatorname {T l} ^ {3 +} \rightleftharpoons \operatorname {T l} ^ {+} (a q) + \operatorname {S n} ^ {4 +} (a q)
$$

# Answer

The volume of $\mathrm { T l } ^ { 3 + }$ needed to reach the equivalence point is 

$$
V _ {e q} = V _ {\mathrm {T l}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {S n}} V _ {\mathrm {S n}}}{M _ {\mathrm {T l}}} = \frac {(0 . 0 5 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{(0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M})} = 2 5. 0 \mathrm {m L}
$$

Before the equivalence point, the concentration of unreacted $\mathsf { S n } ^ { 2 + }$ and the concentration of $\mathsf { S n } ^ { 4 + }$ are easy to calculate. For this reason we find the potential using the Nernst equation for the $\mathrm { S n } ^ { 4 + } / \mathrm { S n } ^ { 2 + }$ half-reaction. For example, the concentrations of $\mathsf { S n } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathsf { S n ^ { 4 + } }$ after adding $1 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of titrant are 

$$
\begin{array}{l} [ \mathrm {S n} ^ {2 +} ] = \frac {(0 . 0 5 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}) - (0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 0. 0 2 5 0 \mathrm {M} \\ [ \mathrm {S n} ^ {4 +} ] = \frac {(0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 0. 0 1 6 7 \mathrm {M} \\ \end{array}
$$

and the potential is 

$$
E = + 0. 1 3 9 \mathrm {V} - \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6}{2} \log \frac {0 . 0 2 5 0 \mathrm {M}}{0 . 0 1 6 7 \mathrm {M}} = + 0. 1 3 4 \mathrm {V}
$$

After the equivalence point, the concentration of $\mathrm { T l ^ { + } }$ and the concentration of excess $\mathrm { T l } ^ { 3 + }$ are easy to calculate. For this reason we find the potential using the Nernst equation for the $\mathrm { T l } ^ { 3 + } / \mathrm { T l } ^ { + }$ half-reaction. For example, after adding $4 0 . 0 \ \mathrm { m L }$ of titrant, the concentrations of $\mathrm { T l } ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { T l } ^ { 3 + }$ are 

$$
\begin{array}{l} [ \mathrm {T l} ^ {+} ] = \frac {(0 . 0 5 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 4 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 0. 0 2 7 8 \mathrm {M} \\ [ \mathrm {T l} ^ {3 +} ] = \frac {(0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (4 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}) - (0 . 0 5 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 4 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 0. 0 1 6 7 \mathrm {M} \\ \end{array}
$$

and the potential is 

$$
E = + 0. 7 7 \mathrm {V} - \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6}{2} \log \frac {0 . 0 2 7 8 \mathrm {M}}{0 . 0 1 6 7 \mathrm {M}} = + 0. 7 6 \mathrm {V}
$$

At the titration’s equivalence point, the potential, $E _ { \mathrm { e q } } ,$ potential is 

$$
E _ {e q} = \frac {0 . 1 3 9 \mathrm {V} + 0 . 7 7 \mathrm {V}}{2} = + 0. 4 5 \mathrm {V}
$$

Some additional results are shown here. 

<table><tr><td>volume of Tl3+(mL)</td><td>E (V)</td><td>volume of Tl3+(mL)</td><td>E (V)</td></tr><tr><td>5.00</td><td>0.121</td><td>30.0</td><td>0.75</td></tr><tr><td>10.0</td><td>0.134</td><td>35.0</td><td>0.75</td></tr><tr><td>15.0</td><td>0.144</td><td>40.0</td><td>0.76</td></tr><tr><td>20.0</td><td>0.157</td><td>45.0</td><td>0.76</td></tr><tr><td>25.0</td><td>0.45</td><td></td><td></td></tr></table>

# Sketching a Redox Titration Curve

To evaluate the relationship between a titration’s equivalence point and its end point we need to construct only a reasonable approximation of the exact titration curve. In this section we demonstrate a simple method for sketching a redox titration curve. Our goal is to sketch the titration curve quickly, using as few calculations as possible. Let’s use the titration of $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of $0 . 1 0 0 \mathbf { M }$ $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ with $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { M } \mathrm { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ in a matrix of $1 \mathrm { M H C l O } _ { 4 }$ . 

This is the same example that we used in developing the calculations for a redox titration curve. You can review the results of that calculation in Table 9.4.1 and Figure 9.4.1 . 

We begin by calculating the titration’s equivalence point volume, which, as we determined earlier, is 50.0 mL. Next, we draw our axes, placing the potential, E, on the $y$ -axis and the titrant’s volume on the $\boldsymbol { x }$ -axis. To indicate the equivalence point’s volume, we draw a vertical line that intersects the $x$ -axis at $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ . Figure 9.4.2 a shows the result of the first step in our sketch. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/e03fee5a55ba82dde6d48aa8f4d7c233c2fdd25ac40d13690123b2be38ab942e.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/c8d5248f7203dfac6496ebef350cb8513b96c43fbcd3f97e27fac7e120614a75.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/b16c42c1d204673a32fd530da2520dbe05ac9ec995a10b81a92705be9a59a826.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/b3f93ba3ac0be5011b9b1b43217ef0629b965a378949ff78494eaceaa6ce529c.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/4316c355b0bf84237b86a9659438f8e9a008906f47646a98c7f3dbd4486b45e9.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/41d8adf0474df559253b6a112bf9685ec74fe77050fceadfd85c8fe5062b2ce2.jpg)



Figure 9.4.2 . Illustrations showing the steps in sketching an approximate titration curve for the titration of $5 0 . 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.100 M $\mathrm { F e } ^ { \overline { { 2 } } + }$ with $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ C e } ^ { 4 + }$ in $1 \ \mathrm { M } \ \mathrm { H C l O } _ { 4 } ;$ : (a) locating the equivalence point volume; (b) plotting two points before the equivalence point; (c) plotting two points after the equivalence point; (d) preliminary approximation of titration curve using straight-lines; (e) final approximation of titration curve using a smooth curve; (f ) comparison of approximate titration curve (solid black line) and exact titration curve (dashed red line). See the text for additional details.


Before the equivalence point, the potential is determined by a redox buffer of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ . Although we can calculate the potential using the Nernst equation, we can avoid this calculation if we make a simple assumption. You may recall from Chapter 6 that a redox buffer operates over a range of potentials that extends approximately $\pm ( 0 . 0 5 9 1 6 / n )$ unit on either side of $E _ { \mathrm { F e } ^ { 3 + } / \mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + } } ^ { \circ }$ . The potential at the buffer’s lower limit is 

$$
E = E _ {\mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} / \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}} ^ {\circ} - 0. 0 5 9 1 6
$$

when the concentration of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ is $\mathbf { 1 0 \times }$ greater than that of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ . The buffer reaches its upper potential of 

$$
E = E _ {\mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} / \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}} ^ {\circ} + 0. 0 5 9 1 6
$$

when the concentration of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ is ${ \bf 1 0 \times }$ smaller than that of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ . The redox buffer spans a range of volumes from approximately $1 0 \%$ of the equivalence point volume to approximately $9 0 \%$ of the equivalence point volume. Figure $9 . 4 . 2 \mathrm { ~ b ~ }$ shows the second step in our sketch. First, we superimpose a ladder diagram for Fe on the $y$ -axis, using its $E _ { \mathrm { F e } ^ { 3 + } / \mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + } } ^ { \circ }$ value of 0.767 V and including the buffer’s range of potentials. Next, we add points for the potential at $1 0 \%$ of $V _ { e q }$ (a potential of $0 . 7 0 8 \mathrm { ~ V ~ }$ at $5 . 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ ) and for the potential at $9 0 \%$ of $V _ { e q }$ (a potential of 0.826 V at 45.0 mL). 

We used a similar approach when sketching the acid–base titration curve for the titration of acetic acid with NaOH; see Chapter 9.2 for details. 

The third step in sketching our titration curve is to add two points after the equivalence point. Here the potential is controlled by a redox buffer of $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 3 + }$ and ${ \mathsf { C e } } ^ { 4 + }$ . The redox buffer is at its lower limit of 

$$
E = E _ {\mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} / \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +}} ^ {\circ} - 0. 0 5 9 1 6
$$

when the titrant reaches $1 1 0 \%$ of the equivalence point volume and the potential is $E _ { \mathrm { C e } ^ { 4 + } / \mathrm { C e } ^ { 3 + } } ^ { \circ }$ when the volume of Ce is $2 \times V _ { e q }$ . 

We used a similar approach when sketching the complexation titration curve for the titration of $\mathbf { M } \mathbf { g } ^ { 2 + }$ with EDTA; see Chapter 9.3 for details. 

Figure 9.4.2 c shows the third step in our sketch. First, we superimpose a ladder diagram for Ce on the y-axis, using its $E _ { \mathrm { C e ^ { 4 + } / C e ^ { 3 + } } } ^ { \circ }$ value of $1 . 7 0 \mathrm { V }$ and including the buffer’s range. Next, we add points representing the potential at $1 1 0 \%$ of $V _ { e q }$ (a value of 1.66 V at $5 5 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ ) and at $2 0 0 \%$ of $V _ { e q }$ (a value of 1.70 V at $1 0 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ ). 

Next, we draw a straight line through each pair of points, extending the line through the vertical line that indicates the equivalence point’s volume (Figure 9.4.2 d). Finally, we complete our sketch by drawing a smooth curve that connects the three straight-line segments (Figure 9.4.2 e). A comparison of our sketch to the exact titration curve (Figure 9.4.2 f) shows that they are in close agreement. 

#  Exercise 9.4.2

Sketch the titration curve for the titration of $5 0 . 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.0500 M $\mathsf { S n } ^ { 4 + }$ with $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ T l ^ { + } }$ . Both the titrand and the titrant are $1 . 0 \mathbf { M }$ in HCl. The titration reaction is 

$$
\operatorname {S n} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \operatorname {T l} ^ {3 +} (a q) \rightleftharpoons \operatorname {T l} ^ {+} (a q) + \operatorname {S n} ^ {4 +} (a q)
$$

Compare your sketch to your calculated titration curve from Exercise 9.4.1 . 

# Answer

The figure below shows a sketch of the titration curve. The two points before the equivalence point 

$$
V _ {\mathrm {T l}} = 2. 5 \mathrm {m L}, E = + 0. 1 0 9 \mathrm {V} \text {a n d} V _ {\mathrm {T l}} = 2 2. 5 \mathrm {m L}, E = + 0. 1 6 9 \mathrm {V}
$$

are plotted using the redox buffer for $\mathrm { S n } ^ { 4 + } / \mathrm { S n } ^ { 2 + }$ , which spans a potential range of $+ 0 . 1 3 9 \pm 0 . 5 9 1 6 / 2$ . The two points after the equivalence point 

$$
V _ {\mathrm {T I}} = 2 7. 5 \mathrm {m L}, E = + 0. 7 4 \mathrm {V} \text {a n d} V _ {\mathrm {T I}} = 5 0 \mathrm {m L}, E = + 0. 7 7 \mathrm {V}
$$

are plotted using the redox buffer for $\mathrm { T l } ^ { 3 + } / \mathrm { T l } ^ { + }$ , which spans the potential range of $+ 0 . 1 3 9 \pm 0 . 5 9 1 6 / 2$ . The black dots and curve are the approximate sketch of the titration curve. The points in red are the calculations from Exercise 9.4.1 . 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/0d28b0f4b1ffa915923095a75c649087b5f48ea344f9f229f46d802615ac69c4.jpg)


# Selecting and Evaluating the End Point

A redox titration’s equivalence point occurs when we react stoichiometrically equivalent amounts of titrand and titrant. As is the case for acid–base titrations and complexation titrations, we estimate the equivalence point of a redox titration using an experimental end point. A variety of methods are available for locating a redox titration’s end point, including indicators and sensors that respond to a change in the solution conditions. 

# Where is the Equivalence Point

For an acid–base titration or a complexometric titration the equivalence point is almost identical to the inflection point on the steeply rising part of the titration curve. If you look back at Figure 9.2.2 and Figure 9.3.3, you will see that the inflection point is in the middle of this steep rise in the titration curve, which makes it relatively easy to find the equivalence point when you sketch these titration curves. We call this a symmetric equivalence point. If the stoichiometry of a redox titration is 1:1—that is, one mole of titrant reacts with each mole of titrand—then the equivalence point is symmetric. If the titration reaction’s stoichiometry is not 1:1, then the equivalence point is closer to the top or to the bottom of the titration curve’s sharp rise. In this case we have an asymmetric equivalence point. 

#  Example 9.4.1

Derive a general equation for the equivalence point’s potential when titrating $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ with $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ 

$$
5 \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \mathrm {M n O} _ {4} ^ {-} (a q) + 8 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) \rightarrow 5 \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} (a q) + \mathrm {M n} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 4 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l)
$$

# Solution

The half-reactions for the oxidation of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ and the reduction of $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ are 

$$
\mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} (a q) \rightarrow \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} (a q) + e ^ {-}
$$

$$
\mathrm {M n O} _ {4} ^ {-} (a q) + 8 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) + 5 e ^ {-} \rightarrow \mathrm {M n} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 4 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l)
$$

for which the Nernst equations are 

$$
E = E _ {\mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} / \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}} ^ {\circ} - 0. 5 9 1 6 \log \frac {[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} ]}{[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} ]}
$$

$$
E = E _ {\mathrm {M n O _ {4} ^ {-} / M n ^ {2 +}}} ^ {\circ} - \frac {0 . 5 9 1 6}{5} \log \frac {[ \mathrm {M n ^ {2 +}} ]}{[ \mathrm {M n O _ {4} ^ {-}} ] [ \mathrm {H ^ {+}} ] ^ {8}}
$$

Before we add together these two equations we must multiply the second equation by 5 so that we can combine the log terms; thus 

$$
6 E _ {e q} = E _ {\mathrm {F e ^ {3 +} / F e ^ {2 +}}} ^ {\circ} + 5 E _ {\mathrm {M n O _ {4} ^ {-} / M n ^ {2 +}}} ^ {\circ} - 0. 0 5 9 1 6 \log \frac {[ \mathrm {F e ^ {2 +}} ] [ \mathrm {M n ^ {2 +}} ]}{[ \mathrm {F e ^ {3 +}} ] [ \mathrm {M n O _ {4} ^ {-}} ] [ \mathrm {H ^ {+}} ] ^ {8}}
$$

At the equivalance point we know that 

$$
[ \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} ] = 5 \times [ \mathrm {M n O} _ {4} ^ {-} ] \text {a n d} [ \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} ] = 5 \times [ \mathrm {M n} ^ {2 +} ]
$$

Substituting these equalities into the previous equation and rearranging gives us a general equation for the potential at the equivalence point. 

$$
6 E _ {e q} = E _ {\mathrm {F e ^ {3 +} / F e ^ {2 +}}} ^ {\circ} + 5 E _ {\mathrm {M n O _ {4} ^ {-} / M n ^ {2 +}}} ^ {\circ} - 0. 0 5 9 1 6 \log \frac {5 [ \mathrm {M n O _ {4} ^ {-}} ] [ \mathrm {M n ^ {2 +}} ]}{5 [ \mathrm {M n ^ {2 +}} ] [ \mathrm {M n O _ {4} ^ {-}} ] [ \mathrm {H ^ {+}} ] ^ {8}}
$$

$$
E _ {e q} = \frac {E _ {\mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} / \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}} ^ {\circ} + 5 E _ {\mathrm {M n O} _ {4} ^ {-} / \mathrm {M n} ^ {2 +}} ^ {\circ}}{6} - \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6}{6} \log \frac {1}{\left[ \mathrm {H} ^ {+} \right] ^ {8}}
$$

$$
E _ {e q} = \frac {E _ {\mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} / \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}} ^ {\circ} + 5 E _ {\mathrm {M n O} _ {4} ^ {-} / \mathrm {M n} ^ {2 +}} ^ {\circ}}{6} + \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6 \times 8}{6} \log [ \mathrm {H} ^ {+} ]
$$

$$
E _ {e q} = \frac {E _ {\mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} / \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}} ^ {\circ} + 5 E _ {\mathrm {M n O} _ {4} ^ {-} / \mathrm {M n} ^ {2 +}} ^ {\circ}}{6} - 0. 0 7 8 8 8 \mathrm {p H}
$$

Our equation for the equivalence point has two terms. The first term is a weighted average of the titrand’s and the titrant’s standard state potentials, in which the weighting factors are the number of electrons in their respective half-reactions. The second term shows that $E _ { e q }$ for this titration is pH-dependent. At a pH of 1 (in $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S O } _ { 4 } ,$ ), for example, the equivalence point has a potential of 

$$
E _ {e q} = \frac {0 . 7 6 8 + 5 \times 1 . 5 1}{6} - 0. 0 7 8 8 8 \times 1 = 1. 3 1 \mathrm {V}
$$

Figure 9.4.3 shows a typical titration curve for titration of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ with $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ . Note that the titration’s equivalence point is asymmetrical. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/b3a30fafb0cafe644dbb833fd163b5113be5dac1354a6f70686b926c07539856.jpg)



Figure 9.4.3 . Titration curve for the titration of $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ F e } ^ { 2 + }$ with 0.0200 M $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ at a fixed pH of 1 (using $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } S 0 _ { 4 } )$ . The equivalence point is shown by the red dot.


#  Exercise 9.4.3

Derive a general equation for the equivalence point’s potential for the titration of ${ \mathrm { U } } ^ { 4 + }$ with $\mathsf { C e ^ { 4 + } }$ . The unbalanced reaction is 

$$
\mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} (a q) + \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +} (a q) \rightarrow \mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +} (a q)
$$

What is the equivalence point’s potential if the pH is 1? 

# Answer

The two half reactions are 

$$
\mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} (a q) + e ^ {-} \rightarrow \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +} (a q)
$$

$$
\mathrm {U} ^ {4 +} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightarrow \mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} (a q)) + 4 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) + 2 e ^ {-}
$$

for which the Nernst equations are 

$$
E = E _ {\mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} / \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +}} ^ {\circ} - 0. 0 5 9 1 6 \log \frac {[ \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +} ]}{[ \mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} ]}
$$

$$
E = E _ {\mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} / \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +}} ^ {\circ} - \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6}{2} \log \frac {[ \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +} ]}{[ \mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} ] [ \mathrm {H} ^ {+} ] ^ {4}}
$$

Before adding these two equations together we must multiply the second equation by 2 so that we can combine the log terms; thus 

$$
3 E = E _ {\mathrm {C e ^ {4 +} / C e ^ {3 +}}} ^ {\circ} + 2 E _ {\mathrm {U O _ {2} ^ {2 +} / U ^ {4 +}}} ^ {\circ} - 0. 0 5 9 1 6 \log {\frac {[ \mathrm {C e ^ {3 +}} ] [ \mathrm {U ^ {4 +}} ]}{[ \mathrm {C e ^ {4 +}} ] [ \mathrm {U O _ {2} ^ {2 +}} ] [ \mathrm {H ^ {+}} ] ^ {4}}}
$$

At the equivalence point we know that 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +} \right] = 2 \times \left[ \mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} \right] \text {a n d} \left[ \mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} \right] = 2 \times \left[ \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +} \right]
$$

Substituting these equalities into the previous equation and rearranging gives us a general equation for the potential at the equivalence point. 

$$
3 E = E _ {\mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} / \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +}} ^ {\circ} + 2 E _ {\mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} / \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +}} ^ {\circ} - 0. 0 5 9 1 6 \log \frac {2 [ \mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} ] [ \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +} ]}{2 [ \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +} ] [ \mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} ] [ \mathrm {H} ^ {+} ] ^ {4}}
$$

$$
E = \frac {E _ {\mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} / \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +}} ^ {\circ} + 2 E _ {\mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} / \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +}} ^ {\circ}}{3} - \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6}{3} \log \frac {1}{\left[ \mathrm {H} ^ {+} \right] ^ {4}}
$$

$$
E = \frac {E _ {\mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} / \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +}} ^ {\circ} + 2 E _ {\mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} / \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +}} ^ {\circ}}{3} + \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6 \times 4}{3} \log [ \mathrm {H} ^ {+} ] ^ {4}
$$

$$
E = \frac {E _ {\mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} / \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +}} ^ {\circ} + 2 E _ {\mathrm {U O} _ {2} ^ {2 +} / \mathrm {U} ^ {4 +}} ^ {\circ}}{3} - 0. 0 7 8 8 8 \mathrm {p H}
$$

At a pH of 1 the equivalence point has a potential of 

$$
E = \frac {1 . 7 2 + 2 \times 0 . 3 2 7}{3} - 0. 0 7 8 8 8 \times 1 = + 0. 7 1 2 \mathrm {V}
$$

# Finding the End Point With an Indicator

Three types of indicators are used to signal a redox titration’s end point. The oxidized and reduced forms of some titrants, such as $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ , have different colors. A solution of $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ is intensely purple. In an acidic solution, however, permanganate’s reduced form, $\mathrm { { M n ^ { 2 + } } }$ , is nearly colorless. When using $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ as a titrant, the titrand’s solution remains colorless until the equivalence point. The first drop of excess $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ produces a permanent tinge of purple, signaling the end point. 

Some indicators form a colored compound with a specific oxidized or reduced form of the titrant or the titrand. Starch, for example, forms a dark purple complex with $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . We can use this distinct color to signal the presence of excess $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ as a titrant—a change in color from colorless to purple—or the completion of a reaction that consumes $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ as the titrand— a change in color from purple to colorless. Another example of a specific indicator is thiocyanate, $\mathsf { S C N ^ { - } }$ , which forms the soluble red-colored complex of $\mathrm { F e } ( \mathsf { S C N } ) ^ { 2 + }$ in the presence of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ . 

The most important class of indicators are substances that do not participate in the redox titration, but whose oxidized and reduced forms differ in color. When we add a redox indicator to the titrand, the indicator imparts a color that depends on the solution’s potential. As the solution’s potential changes with the addition of titrant, the indicator eventually changes oxidation state and changes color, signaling the end point. 

To understand the relationship between potential and an indicator’s color, consider its reduction half-reaction 

$$
\ln_ {\mathrm {o x}} + n e ^ {-} \rightleftharpoons \ln_ {\mathrm {r e d}}
$$

where $\mathrm { I n } _ { \mathrm { o x } }$ and $\mathrm { I n } _ { \mathrm { r e d } }$ are, respectively, the indicator’s oxidized and reduced forms. 

For simplicity, $\mathrm { I n } _ { \mathrm { o x } }$ and $\mathrm { I n } _ { \mathrm { r e d } }$ are shown without specific charges. Because there is a change in oxidation state, $\mathrm { I n } _ { \mathrm { o x } }$ and $\mathrm { I n } _ { \mathrm { r e d } }$ cannot both be neutral. 

The Nernst equation for this half-reaction is 

$$
E = E _ {\mathrm {I n} _ {\mathrm {o x}} / \mathrm {I n} _ {\mathrm {r e d}}} ^ {\circ} - \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6}{n} \log \frac {[ \mathrm {I n} _ {\mathrm {r e d}} ]}{[ \mathrm {I n} _ {\mathrm {o x}} ]}
$$

As shown in Figure 9.4.4 , if we assume the indicator’s color changes from that of $\mathrm { I n } _ { \mathrm { o x } }$ to that of $\mathrm { I n } _ { \mathrm { r e d } }$ when the ratio $[ \mathrm { I n } _ { \mathrm { r e d } } ] / [ \mathrm { I m } _ { 0 \mathrm { x } } ]$ changes from 0.1 to 10, then the end point occurs when the solution’s potential is within the range 

$$
E = E _ {\mathrm {I n} _ {\mathrm {o x}} / \mathrm {I n} _ {\mathrm {r e d}}} ^ {\circ} \pm \frac {0 . 0 5 9 1 6}{n}
$$

This is the same approach we took in considering acid–base indicators and complexation indicators. 

A partial list of redox indicators is shown in Table 9.4.2 . Examples of an appropriate and an inappropriate indicator for the titration of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ with $\mathsf { C e ^ { 4 + } }$ are shown in Figure 9.4.5 . 


Table 9.4.2 . Selected Examples of Redox Indicators


<table><tr><td>indicator</td><td>color Inox</td><td>color of Inred</td><td>E° (V)</td></tr><tr><td>indigo tetrasulfate</td><td>blue</td><td>colorless</td><td>0.36</td></tr><tr><td>methylene blue</td><td>blue</td><td>colorless</td><td>0.53</td></tr><tr><td>diphenylamine</td><td>violet</td><td>colorless</td><td>0.75</td></tr><tr><td>diphenylamine sulfonic acid</td><td>red-violet</td><td>colorless</td><td>0.85</td></tr><tr><td>tris(2,2&#x27;-bipyradine)iron</td><td>pale blue</td><td>red</td><td>1.120</td></tr><tr><td>ferroin</td><td>pale blue</td><td>red</td><td>1.147</td></tr><tr><td>tris(5-nitro-1,10-phenanthroline)iron</td><td>pale blue</td><td>red-violet</td><td>1.25</td></tr></table>

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/c225ea51b5202c55be19b00c36fe2e7d57554de0ad62eea6577fe3ce690b579f.jpg)



Figure 9.4.4 . Diagram showing the relationship between $E$ and an indicator’s color. The ladder diagram defines potentials where $\mathrm { I n } _ { \mathrm { r e d } }$ and $\mathrm { I n } _ { \mathrm { o x } }$ are the predominate species.


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/a07bd73d865ba0030f63f091058241040d856be5f82ca992fa07fc444953fd3a.jpg)



Figure 9.4.5 . Titration curve for titration of $5 0 . 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ F e } ^ { 2 + }$ with $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ C e } ^ { 4 + }$ . The end point transitions for the indicators diphenylamine sulfonic acid and ferroin are superimposed on the titration curve. Because the transition for ferroin is too small to see on the scale of the $\boldsymbol { x }$ -axis—it requires only 1–2 drops of titrant—the color change is expanded to the right.


# Other Methods for Finding the End Point

Another method for locating a redox titration’s end point is a potentiometric titration in which we monitor the change in potential while we add the titrant to the titrand. The end point is found by examining visually the titration curve. The simplest experimental design for a potentiometric titration consists of a Pt indicator electrode whose potential is governed by the titrand’s or the titrant’s redox half-reaction, and a reference electrode that has a fixed potential. Other methods for locating the titration’s end point include thermometric titrations and spectrophotometric titrations. 

You will a further discussion of potentiometry in Chapter 11. 

# Representative Method 9.4.1: Determination of Total Chlorine Residual

The best way to appreciate the theoretical and the practical details discussed in this section is to carefully examine a typical redox titrimetric method. Although each method is unique, the following description of the determination of the total chlorine residual in water provides an instructive example of a typical procedure. The description here is based on Method 4500-Cl B as published in Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater, 20th Ed., American Public Health Association: Washington, D. C., 1998. 

# Description of the Method

The chlorination of a public water supply produces several chlorine-containing species, the combined concentration of which is called the total chlorine residual. Chlorine is present in a variety of chemical states, including the free residual chlorine, which consists of $\mathrm { C l } _ { 2 }$ , HOCl and $0 { \mathrm { C l } } ^ { - }$ , and the combined chlorine residual, which consists of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C l }$ , $\mathrm { N H C l } _ { 2 }$ , and $\mathrm { N C l } _ { 3 }$ . The total chlorine residual is determined by using the oxidizing power of chlorine to convert $\Gamma ^ { - }$ to $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . The amount of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ formed is then determined by titrating with $\mathrm { N a } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 }$ using starch as an indicator. Regardless of its form, the total chlorine residual is reported as if $\mathrm { C l } _ { 2 }$ is the only source of chlorine, and is reported as mg Cl/L. 

# Procedure

Select a volume of sample that requires less than $2 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of $\mathrm { N a } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 }$ to reach the end point. Using glacial acetic acid, acidify the sample to a pH between 3 and 4, and add about 1 gram of KI. Titrate with ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { S } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ until the yellow color of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ begins to disappear. Add 1 mL of a starch indicator solution and continue titrating until the blue color of the starch– $\mathbf { \bar { \mathbf { I } } } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ complex disappears (Figure 9.4.6 ). Use a blank titration to correct the volume of titrant needed to reach the end point for reagent impurities. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/2aedd43cd2daf8293e4abad8a2c556a40544078689f343ed6430192203132751.jpg)



Figure 9.4.6 . Endpoint for the determination of the total chlorine residual. (a) Acidifying the sample and adding KI forms a brown solution of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . (b) Titrating with $\mathrm { N a } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 }$ converts $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ to $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ with the solution fading to a pale yellow color as we approach the end point. (c) Adding starch forms the deep purple starch– $\mathbf { I } _ { \mathbf { 3 } } ^ { - }$ complex. (d) As the titration continues, the end point is a sharp transition from a purple to a colorless solution. The change in color from (c) to (d) typically takes 1–2 drops of titrant.


# Questions

1. Is this an example of a direct or an indirect analysis? 

This is an indirect analysis because the chlorine-containing species do not react with the titrant. Instead, the total chlorine residual oxidizes $\Gamma ^ { - }$ to $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ , and the amount of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ is determined by titrating with ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { S } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ . 

2. Why does the procedure rely on an indirect analysis instead of directly titrating the chlorine-containing species using KI as a titrant? 

Because the total chlorine residual consists of six different species, a titration with $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ does not have a single, well-defined equivalence point. By converting the chlorine residual to an equivalent amount of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ , the indirect titration with ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \cal S } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ has a single, useful equivalence point. Even if the total chlorine residual is from a single species, such as HOCl, a direct titration with KI is impractical. Because the product of the titration, $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ , imparts a yellow color, the titrand’s color would change with each addition of titrant, making it difficult to find a suitable indicator. 

3. Both oxidizing and reducing agents can interfere with this analysis. Explain the effect of each type of interferent on the total chlorine residual. 

An interferent that is an oxidizing agent converts additional $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ to $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . Because this extra $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ requires an additional volume of $\mathrm { N a } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 }$ to reach the end point, we overestimate the total chlorine residual.
