If the interferent is a reducing agent, it reduces back to $\Gamma ^ { - }$ some of the $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ produced by the reaction between the total chlorine residual and iodide; as a result, we underestimate the total chlorine residual. 

# Quantitative Applications

Although many quantitative applications of redox titrimetry have been re- placed by other analytical methods, a few important applications continue to find relevance. In this section we review the general application of redox titrimetry with an emphasis on environmental, pharmaceutical, and industrial applications. We begin, however, with a brief discussion of selecting and characterizing redox titrants, and methods for controlling the titrand’s oxidation state. 

# Adjusting the Titrand's Oxidation State

If a redox titration is to be used in a quantitative analysis, the titrand initially must be present in a single oxidation state. For example, iron is determined by a redox titration in which $\mathsf { C e ^ { 4 + } }$ oxidizes $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ to $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ . Depending on the sample and the method of sample preparation, iron initially may be present in both the $+ 2$ and $+ 3$ oxidation states. Before titrating, we must reduce any $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ to $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ if we want to determine the total concentration of iron in the sample. This type of pretreatment is accomplished using an auxiliary reducing agent or oxidizing agent. 

A metal that is easy to oxidize—such as Zn, Al, and Ag—can serve as an auxiliary reducing agent. The metal, as a coiled wire or powder, is added to the sample where it reduces the titrand. Because any unreacted auxiliary reducing agent will react with the 

titrant, it is removed before we begin the titration by removing the coiled wire or by filtering. 

An alternative method for using an auxiliary reducing agent is to immobilize it in a column. To prepare a reduction column an aqueous slurry of the finally divided metal is packed in a glass tube equipped with a porous plug at the bottom. The sample is placed at the top of the column and moves through the column under the influence of gravity or vacuum suction. The length of the reduction column and the flow rate are selected to ensure the analyte’s complete reduction. 

Two common reduction columns are used. In the Jones reductor the column is filled with amalgamated zinc, $\mathrm { Z n } ( \mathrm { H g } )$ , which is prepared by briefly placing Zn granules in a solution of $\mathrm { H g C l } _ { 2 }$ . Oxidation of zinc 

$$
\mathrm {Z n} (\mathrm {H g}) (s) \rightarrow \mathrm {Z n} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \mathrm {H g} (l) + 2 e ^ {-}
$$

provides the electrons for reducing the titrand. In the Walden reductor the column is filled with granular Ag metal. The solution containing the titrand is acidified with HCl and passed through the column where the oxidation of silver 

$$
\mathrm {A g} (s) + \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} (a q) \rightarrow \mathrm {A g C l} (s) + e ^ {-}
$$

provides the necessary electrons for reducing the titrand. Table 9.4.3 provides a summary of several applications of reduction columns. 


Table 9.4.3 . Examples of Reactions for Reducing a Titrand's Oxidation State Using a Reduction Column


<table><tr><td>oxidized titrand</td><td>Walden reductor</td><td>Jones reductor</td></tr><tr><td>Cr3+</td><td>—</td><td>Cr3+(aq) + e− → Cr2+(aq)</td></tr><tr><td>Cu2+</td><td>Cu2+(aq) + e− → Cu+(aq)</td><td>Cu2+(aq) + 2e− → Cu(s)</td></tr><tr><td>Fe3+</td><td>Fe3+(aq) + e− → Fe2+(aq)</td><td>Fe3+(aq) + e− → Fe2+(aq)</td></tr><tr><td>TiO2+</td><td>—</td><td>TiO2+(aq) + 2H+(aq) + e− → Ti3+(aq) + H2O(l)</td></tr><tr><td>MoO42+</td><td>MoO2+(aq) + e− → MoO2+(aq)</td><td>MoO2+(aq) + 4H+(aq) + 3e− → Mo3+(aq) + 2H2O(l)</td></tr><tr><td>VO4+</td><td>VO2+(aq) + 2H+(aq) + e− → VO2+(aq) + H2O(l)</td><td>VO2+(aq) + 4H+(aq) + 3e− → V2+(aq) + 2H2O(l)</td></tr></table>

Several reagents are used as auxiliary oxidizing agents, including ammonium peroxydisulfate, $\mathrm { ( N H } _ { 4 } ) _ { 2 } S _ { 2 } O _ { 8 } .$ , and hydrogen peroxide, $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 2 }$ . Peroxydisulfate is a powerful oxidizing agent 

$$
\mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {8} ^ {2 -} (a q) + 2 e ^ {-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {S O} _ {4} ^ {2 -} (a q)
$$

that is capable of oxidizing $\mathrm { M n } ^ { 2 + }$ to $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ , ${ \mathrm { C r } } ^ { 3 + }$ to $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } { \bf O } _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ , and ${ \mathsf { C e } } ^ { 3 + }$ to $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ . Excess peroxydisulfate is destroyed by briefly boiling the solution. The reduction of hydrogen peroxide in an acidic solution 

$$
\mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {2} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) + 2 e ^ {-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l)
$$

provides another method for oxidizing a titrand. Excess $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 2 }$ is destroyed by briefly boiling the solution. 

# Selecting and Standardizing a Titrant

If it is to be used quantitatively, the titrant’s concentration must remain stable during the analysis. Because a titrant in a reduced state is susceptible to air oxidation, most redox titrations use an oxidizing agent as the titrant. There are several common oxidizing titrants, including $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ , ${ \mathsf { C e } } ^ { 4 + }$ , $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } 0 _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ , and $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . Which titrant is used often depends on how easily it oxidizes the titrand. A titrand that is a weak reducing agent needs a strong oxidizing titrant if the titration reaction is to have a suitable end point. 

The two strongest oxidizing titrants are $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ and $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 4 ^ { + } }$ , for which the reduction half-reactions are 

$$
\mathrm {M n O} _ {4} ^ {-} (a q) + 8 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) + 5 e ^ {-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {M n} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 4 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l)
$$

$$
\mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} (a q) + e ^ {-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +} (a q)
$$

A solution of $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ in 1 M $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S O } _ { 4 }$ usually is prepared from the primary standard cerium ammonium nitrate, $\mathrm { C e ( N O _ { 3 } ) _ { 4 } { \bullet 2 N H _ { 4 } N O _ { 3 } } }$ . When prepared using a reagent grade material, such as $\mathrm { C e ( O H ) _ { 4 } }$ , the solution is standardized against a primary standard reducing agent such as $\mathrm { N a _ { 2 } C _ { 2 } O _ { 4 } }$ or $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ (prepared from iron wire) using ferroin as an indicator. Despite its availability as a primary standard and its ease of preparation, $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ is not used as frequently as $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ because it is more expensive. 

The standardization reactions are 

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} (a q) + \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} (a q) \rightarrow \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} (a q) + \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +} (a q) \\ 2 \mathrm {C e} ^ {4 +} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {4} (a q) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {C e} ^ {3 +} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {C O} _ {2} (g) + 2 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) \\ \end{array}
$$

A solution of $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ is prepared from ${ \mathrm { K M n O } } _ { 4 }$ , which is not available as a primary standard. An aqueous solution of permanganate is thermodynamically unstable due to its ability to oxidize water. 

$$
4 \mathrm {M n O} _ {4} ^ {-} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightleftharpoons 4 \mathrm {M n O} _ {2} (s) + 3 \mathrm {O} _ {2} (g) + 4 \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

This reaction is catalyzed by the presence of $\mathbf { M n O } _ { 2 }$ , $\mathrm { M n } ^ { 2 + }$ , heat, light, and the presence of acids and bases. A moderately stable solution of permanganate is prepared by boiling it for an hour and filtering through a sintered glass filter to remove any solid $\mathbf { M n O } _ { 2 }$ that precipitates. Standardization is accomplished against a primary standard reducing agent such as $\mathrm { N a _ { 2 } C _ { 2 } O _ { 4 } }$ or $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ (prepared from iron wire), with the pink color of excess $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ signaling the end point. A solution of $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ prepared in this fashion is stable for 1–2 weeks, although you should recheck the standardization periodically. 

The standardization reactions are 

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {M n O} _ {4} ^ {-} (a q) + 5 \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 8 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) \rightarrow \mathrm {M n} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 5 \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} (a q) + 4 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \\ 2 \mathrm {M n O} _ {4} ^ {-} (a q) + 5 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {4} (a q) + 6 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {M n} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 1 0 \mathrm {C O} _ {2} (g) + 8 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \\ \end{array}
$$

Potassium dichromate is a relatively strong oxidizing agent whose principal advantages are its availability as a primary standard and its long term stability when in solution. It is not, however, as strong an oxidizing agent as $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ or $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ , which makes it less useful when the titrand is a weak reducing agent. Its reduction half-reaction is 

$$
\mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7} ^ {2 -} (a q) + 1 4 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) + 6 e ^ {-} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm {C r} ^ {3 +} (a q) + 7 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l)
$$

Although a solution of $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } 0 _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ is orange and a solution of $\mathrm { C r ^ { 3 + } }$ is green, neither color is intense enough to serve as a useful indicator. Diphenylamine sulfonic acid, whose oxidized form is red-violet and reduced form is colorless, gives a very distinct end point signal with $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } { \bf O } _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ . 

Iodine is another important oxidizing titrant. Because it is a weaker oxidizing agent than $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ , $\mathsf { C e ^ { 4 + } }$ , and $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } 0 _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ , it is useful only when the titrand is a stronger reducing agent. This apparent limitation, however, makes $\mathrm { I } _ { 2 }$ a more selective titrant for the analysis of a strong reducing agent in the presence of a weaker reducing agent. The reduction half-reaction for $\mathrm { I } _ { 2 }$ is 

$$
\mathrm {I} _ {2} (a q) + 2 e ^ {-} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

Because iodine is not very soluble in water, solutions are prepared by adding an excess of $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ . The complexation reaction 

$$
\mathrm {I} _ {2} (a q) + \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

increases the solubility of $\mathrm { I } _ { 2 }$ by forming the more soluble triiodide ion, $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . Even though iodine is present as $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ instead of $\mathrm { I } _ { 2 }$ , the number of electrons in the reduction half-reaction is unaffected. 

$$
\mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + 2 e ^ {-} (a q) \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

Solutions of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ normally are standardized against $\mathrm { N a } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 }$ using starch as a specific indicator for $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ 

The standardization reaction is 

$$
\mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3} ^ {2 -} (a q) \rightarrow 3 \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {S} _ {4} \mathrm {O} _ {6} ^ {2 -} (a q)
$$

An oxidizing titrant such as $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ , ${ \mathsf { C e } } ^ { 4 + }$ , $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ , and $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ , is used when the titrand is in a reduced state. If the titrand is in an oxidized state, we can first reduce it with an auxiliary reducing agent and then complete the titration using an oxidizing titrant. Alternatively, we can titrate it using a reducing titrant. Iodide is a relatively strong reducing agent that could serve as a reducing titrant except that its solutions are susceptible to the air-oxidation of $\Gamma ^ { - }$ to $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . 

$$
3 \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + 2 e ^ {-}
$$

A freshly prepared solution of KI is clear, but after a few days it may show a faint yellow coloring due to the presence of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ 

Instead, adding an excess of KI reduces the titrand and releases a stoichiometric amount of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . The amount of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ produced is then determined by a back titration using thiosulfate, $\mathsf { S _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - } }$ , as a reducing titrant. 

$$
2 \mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3} ^ {2 -} (a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {S} _ {4} \mathrm {O} _ {6} ^ {2 -} (a q) + 2 e ^ {-}
$$

Solutions of $\ S _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - }$ are prepared using $\mathrm { N a _ { 2 } S _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } { \bullet } 5 H _ { 2 } O }$ and are standardized before use. Standardization is accomplished by dissolving a carefully weighed portion of the primary standard $\mathrm { K I O } _ { 3 }$ in an acidic solution that contains an excess of KI. The reaction between $\mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ and $\Gamma ^ { - }$ 

$$
\mathrm {I O} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + 8 \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q) + 6 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) \rightarrow 3 \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + 3 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l)
$$

liberates a stoichiometric amount of I-3 . By titrating this $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ with thiosulfate, using starch as a visual indicator, we can determine the concentration of $\mathsf { S } _ { 2 } \mathsf { O } _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - }$ in the titrant. 

The standardization titration is 

$$
\mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3} ^ {2 -} (a q) \rightarrow 3 \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {S} _ {4} \mathrm {O} _ {6} ^ {2 -} (a q)
$$

which is the same reaction used to standardize solutions of $\mathbf { I _ { 3 } ^ { - } }$ . This approach to standardizing solutions of ${ \bf S _ { 2 } O _ { 2 } ^ { 3 - } }$ is similar to that used in the determination of the total chlorine residual outlined in Representative Method 9.4.1. 

Although thiosulfate is one of the few reducing titrants that is not readily oxidized by contact with air, it is subject to a slow decomposition to bisulfite and elemental sulfur. If used over a period of several weeks, a solution of thiosulfate is restandardized periodically. Several forms of bacteria are able to metabolize thiosulfate, which leads to a change in its concentration. This problem is minimized by adding a preservative such as $\mathrm { H g I } _ { 2 }$ to the solution. 

Another useful reducing titrant is ferrous ammonium sulfate, $\mathrm { F e ( N H _ { 4 } ) _ { 2 } ( S O _ { 4 } ) _ { 2 } { \bullet } H _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } }$ in which iron is present in the $+ 2$ oxidation state. A solution of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ is susceptible to air-oxidation, but when prepared in 0.5 M $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S O } _ { 4 }$ it remains stable for as long as a month. Periodic restandardization with $\mathrm { K } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 7 }$ is advisable. Ferrous ammonium sulfate is used as the titrant in a direct analysis of the titrand, or, it is added to the titrand in excess and the amount of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ produced determined by back titrating with a standard solution of $\mathsf { C e } ^ { 4 + }$ or $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } 0 _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ . 

# Inorganic Analysis

One of the most important applications of redox titrimetry is evaluating the chlorination of public water supplies. Representative Method 9.4.1, for example, describes an approach for determining the total chlorine residual using the oxidizing power of chlorine to oxidize $\Gamma ^ { - }$ to $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . The amount of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ is determined by back titrating with $\ S _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - }$ . 

The efficiency of chlorination depends on the form of the chlorinating species. There are two contributions to the total chlorine residual—the free chlorine residual and the combined chlorine residual. The free chlorine residual includes forms of chlorine that are available for disinfecting the water supply. Examples of species that contribute to the free chlorine residual include $\mathrm { C l } _ { 2 }$ , HOCl and OCl . The combined chlorine residual includes those species in which chlorine is in its reduced form and, therefore, no longer– capable of providing disinfection. Species that contribute to the combined chlorine residual are $\mathrm { N H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C l }$ , $\mathrm { N H C l } _ { 2 }$ and $\mathrm { N C l } _ { 3 }$ . 

When a sample of iodide-free chlorinated water is mixed with an excess of the indicator N,N-diethyl- $p$ -phenylenediamine (DPD), the free chlorine oxidizes a stoichiometric portion of DPD to its red-colored form. The oxidized DPD is then back-titrated to its colorless form using ferrous ammonium sulfate as the titrant. The volume of titrant is proportional to the free residual chlorine. 

Having determined the free chlorine residual in the water sample, a small amount of KI is added, which catalyzes the reduction of monochloramine, $\mathrm { N H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C l }$ , and oxidizes a portion of the DPD back to its red-colored form. Titrating the oxidized DPD with ferrous ammonium sulfate yields the amount of $\mathrm { N H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C l }$ in the sample. The amount of dichloramine and trichloramine are determined in a similar fashion. 

The methods described above for determining the total, free, or combined chlorine residual also are used to establish a water supply’s chlorine demand. Chlorine demand is defined as the quantity of chlorine needed to react completely with any substance that can be oxidized by chlorine, while also maintaining the desired chlorine residual. It is determined by adding progressively greater amounts of chlorine to a set of samples drawn from the water supply and determining the total, free, or combined chlorine residual. 

Another important example of redox titrimetry, which finds applications in both public health and environmental analysis, is the determination of dissolved oxygen. In natural waters, such as lakes and rivers, the level of dissolved $\mathrm { O } _ { 2 }$ is important for two reasons: it is the most readily available oxidant for the biological oxidation of inorganic and organic pollutants; and it is necessary for the support of aquatic life. In a wastewater treatment plant dissolved $\mathrm { O } _ { 2 }$ is essential for the aerobic oxidation of waste materials. If the concentration of dissolved $\mathrm { O } _ { 2 }$ falls below a critical value, aerobic bacteria are replaced by anaerobic bacteria, and the oxidation of organic waste produces undesirable gases, such as $\mathrm { C H } _ { 4 }$ and $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \boldsymbol { S }$ . 

One standard method for determining dissolved $\mathrm { O } _ { 2 }$ in natural waters and wastewaters is the Winkler method. A sample of water is collected without exposing it to the atmosphere, which might change the concentration of dissolved $\mathrm { O } _ { 2 }$ . The sample first is treated with a solution of $\mathrm { { \bf M n S O _ { 4 } } }$ and then with a solution of NaOH and KI. Under these alkaline conditions the dissolved oxygen oxidizes $\mathrm { M n } ^ { 2 + }$ to $\mathbf { M n O } _ { 2 }$ . 

$$
2 \mathrm {M n} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 4 \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {O} _ {2} (g) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {M n O} _ {2} (s) + 2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l)
$$

After the reaction is complete, the solution is acidified with $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S O } _ { 4 }$ . Under the now acidic conditions, $\Gamma ^ { - }$ is oxidized to $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ by $\mathbf { M n O } _ { 2 }$ . 

$$
\mathrm {M n O} _ {2} (s) + 3 \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q) + 4 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) \rightarrow \mathrm {M n} ^ {2 +} (a q) + \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l)
$$

The amount of $\mathbf { I _ { 3 } ^ { - } }$ that forms is determined by titrating with $\ S _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - }$ using starch as an indicator. The Winkler method is subject to a variety of interferences and several modifications to the original procedure have been proposed. For example, $\mathbf { N O _ { 2 } ^ { - } }$ interferes because it reduces $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ to $\Gamma ^ { - }$ under acidic conditions. This interference is eliminated by adding sodium azide, $\mathrm { N a N } _ { 3 }$ , which reduces $\mathbf { N O _ { 2 } ^ { - } }$ to $\Nu _ { 2 }$ . Other reducing agents, such as $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ , are eliminated by pretreating the sample with ${ \mathrm { K M n O } } _ { 4 }$ and destroying any excess permanganate with ${ \mathrm { K } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { C } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 4 }$ . 

Another important example of redox titrimetry is the determination of water in nonaqueous solvents. The titrant for this analysis is known as the Karl Fischer reagent and consists of a mixture of iodine, sulfur dioxide, pyridine, and methanol. Because the concentration of pyridine is sufficiently large, $\mathrm { I } _ { 2 }$ and ${ \mathsf { S O } } _ { 2 }$ react with pyridine (py) to form the complexes py $\cdot \mathrm { I } _ { 2 }$ and py• $\mathrm { S O } _ { 2 }$ . When added to a sample that contains water, $\mathrm { I } _ { 2 }$ is reduced to $\Gamma ^ { - }$ and ${ \mathsf { S O } } _ { 2 }$ is oxidized to ${ \mathsf { S O } } _ { 3 }$ . 

$$
\mathrm {p y} \cdot \mathrm {I} _ {2} + \mathrm {p y} \cdot \mathrm {S O} _ {2} + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + 2 \mathrm {p y} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {p y} \cdot \mathrm {H I} + \mathrm {p y} \cdot \mathrm {S O} _ {3}
$$

Methanol is included to prevent the further reaction of $\mathrm { p y } { \bullet } \mathrm { S } 0 _ { 3 }$ with water. The titration’s end point is signaled when the solution changes from the product’s yellow color to the brown color of the Karl Fischer reagent. 

# Organic Analysis

Redox titrimetry also is used for the analysis of organic analytes. One important example is the determination of the chemical oxygen demand (COD) of natural waters and wastewaters. The COD is a measure of the quantity of oxygen necessary to oxidize completely all the organic matter in a sample to $\mathrm { C O } _ { 2 }$ and $_ { \mathrm { H _ { 2 } O } }$ . Because no attempt is made to correct for organic matter that is decomposed biologically, or for slow decomposition kinetics, the COD always overestimates a sample’s true oxygen demand. The determination of COD is particularly important in the management of industrial wastewater treatment facilities where it is used to monitor the release of organic-rich wastes into municipal sewer systems or into the environment. 

A sample’s COD is determined by refluxing it in the presence of excess $\mathrm { K } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 7 }$ , which serves as the oxidizing agent. The solution is acidified with $\mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S O } _ { 4 }$ , using ${ \tt A g } _ { 2 } { \tt S } { \tt O } _ { 4 }$ to catalyze the oxidation of low molecular weight fatty acids. Mercuric sulfate, $\mathrm { H g S O _ { 4 } } ,$ , is added to complex any chloride that is present, which prevents the precipitation of the ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ catalyst as AgCl. Under these conditions, the efficiency for oxidizing organic matter is $9 5 \mathrm { - } 1 0 0 \%$ . After refluxing for two hours, the solution is cooled to room temperature and the excess $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } 0 _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ determined by a back titration using ferrous ammonium sulfate as the titrant and ferroin as the indicator. Because it is difficult to remove completely all traces of organic matter from the reagents, a blank titration is performed. The difference in the amount of ferrous ammonium sulfate needed to titrate the sample and the blank is proportional to the COD. 

Iodine has been used as an oxidizing titrant for a number of compounds of pharmaceutical interest. Earlier we noted that the reaction of $\ S _ { 2 } 0 _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - }$ with $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ produces the tetrathionate ion, $\ S _ { 4 } \mathrm { O } _ { 6 } ^ { 2 - }$ . The tetrathionate ion is actually a dimer that consists of two thiosulfate ions connected through a disulfide (–S–S–) linkage. In the same fashion, $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ is used to titrate mercaptans of the general formula RSH, forming the dimer RSSR as a product. The amino acid cysteine also can be titrated with $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . The product of this titration is cystine, which is a dimer of cysteine. Triiodide also is used for the analysis of ascorbic acid (vitamin C) by oxidizing the enediol functional group to an alpha diketone 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/cf81cc94fc998a78479fb55278a8628eb3e579f54deb3d36873044b500de68cf.jpg)


and for the analysis of reducing sugars, such as glucose, by oxidizing the aldehyde functional group to a carboxylate ion in a basic solution. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/37aa3ceaf1019b2811e5bda4e975ba94e2d6b854f41ec4b14a45865452a5bc36.jpg)


An organic compound that contains a hydroxyl, a carbonyl, or an amine functional group adjacent to an hydoxyl or a carbonyl group can be oxidized using metaperiodate, $\mathbf { I O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ , as an oxidizing titrant. 

$$
\mathrm {I O} _ {4} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) + 2 e ^ {-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {I O} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

A two-electron oxidation cleaves the C–C bond between the two functional groups with hydroxyl groups oxidized to aldehydes or ketones, carbonyl groups oxidized to carboxylic acids, and amines oxidized to an aldehyde and an amine (ammonia if a primary amine). The analysis is conducted by adding a known excess of $\mathrm { I O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ to the solution that contains the analyte and allowing the oxidation to take place for approximately one hour at room temperature. When the oxidation is complete, an excess of KI is added, which converts any unreacted $\mathrm { I O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ to $\mathrm { I O } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ and $\mathbf { I _ { 3 } ^ { - } }$ . 

$$
\mathrm {I O} _ {4} ^ {-} (a q) + 3 \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \rightarrow \mathrm {I O} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {O H} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

The $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ is then determined by titrating with $\ S _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - }$ using starch as an indicator. 

# Quantitative Calculations

The quantitative relationship between the titrand and the titrant is determined by the stoichiometry of the titration reaction. If you are unsure of the balanced reaction, you can deduce its stoichiometry by remembering that the electrons in a redox reaction are conserved. 

#  Example 9.4.2

The amount of Fe in a 0.4891-g sample of an ore is determined by titrating with ${ \bf K } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 7 }$ . After dissolving the sample in HCl, the iron is brought into a $+ 2$ oxidation state using a Jones reductor. Titration to the diphenylamine sulfonic acid end point requires 36.92 mL of 0.02153 M $\mathrm { K _ { 2 } C r _ { 2 } O _ { 7 } }$ . Report the ore’s iron content as %w/w $\mathrm { F e _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } }$ . 

# Solution

Because we are not provided with the titration reaction, we will use a conservation of electrons to deduce the stoichiometry. During the titration the analyte is oxidized from $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ to $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ , and the titrant is reduced from $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } 0 _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ to ${ \mathrm { C r } } ^ { 3 + }$ . Oxidizing $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ to $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ requires a single electron. Reducing $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } 0 _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ , in which each chromium is in the $+ 6$ oxidation state, to $\mathrm { C r ^ { 3 + } }$ requires three electrons per chromium, for a total of six electrons. A conservation of electrons for the titration, therefore, requires that each mole of $\mathrm { K _ { 2 } C r _ { 2 } O _ { 7 } }$ reacts with six moles of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ . 

The moles of $\mathrm { K } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 7 }$ used to reach the end point is 

$$
(0. 0 2 1 5 3 \mathrm {M}) (0. 0 3 6 9 2 \mathrm {L}) = 7. 9 4 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7}
$$

which means the sample contains 

$$
7. 9 4 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7} \times \frac {6 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7}} = 4. 7 6 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}
$$

Thus, the %w/w $\mathrm { F e } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 }$ in the sample of ore is 

$$
4. 7 6 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} \times \frac {1 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {F e} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3}}{2 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}} \times \frac {1 5 9 . 6 9 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {F e} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {F e} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3}} = 0. 3 8 0 8 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {F e} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3}
$$

$$
\frac {0 . 3 8 0 8 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {F e} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3}}{0 . 4 8 9 1 \mathrm {g} \text {s a m p l e}} \times 1 0 0 = 7 7. 8 6 _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3}
$$

Although we can deduce the stoichiometry between the titrant and the titrand in Example 9.4.2 without balancing the titration reaction, the balanced reaction 

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7} (a q) + 6 \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +} (a q) + 1 4 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {C r} ^ {3 +} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {K} ^ {+} (a q) + 6 \mathrm {F e} ^ {3 +} (a q) + 7 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l)
$$

does provide useful information. For example, the presence of $\mathrm { H } ^ { + }$ reminds us that the reaction must take place in an acidic solution. 

#  Exercise 9.4.4

The purity of a sample of sodium oxalate, ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { C } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 4 }$ , is determined by titrating with a standard solution of ${ \mathrm { K M n O } } _ { 4 }$ . If a 0.5116- g sample requires $3 5 . 6 2 \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.0400 M ${ \mathrm { K M n O } } _ { 4 }$ to reach the titration’s end point, what is the %w/w ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { C } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 4 }$ in the sample. 

# Answer

Because we are not provided with a balanced reaction, let’s use a conservation of electrons to deduce the stoichiometry. Oxidizing $\mathrm { C _ { 2 } O _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - } }$ , in which each carbon has a $+ 3$ oxidation state, to $\mathrm { C O } _ { 2 }$ , in which carbon has an oxidation state of $+ 4 ,$ , requires one electron per carbon or a total of two electrons for each mole of $\mathrm { C _ { 2 } O _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - } }$ . Reducing $\bf { M n O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ , in which each manganese is in the $+ 7$ oxidation state, to $\mathrm { { M n ^ { 2 + } } }$ requires five electrons. A conservation of electrons for the titration, therefore, requires that two moles of ${ \mathrm { K M n O } } _ { 4 }$ (10 moles of $e ^ { - }$ ) react with five moles of ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { C } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 4 }$ (10 moles of $e ^ { - }$ ). 

The moles of ${ \mathrm { K M n O } } _ { 4 }$ used to reach the end point is 

$$
(0. 0 4 0 0 \mathrm {M} \mathrm {K M n O} _ {4}) (0. 0 3 5 6 2 \mathrm {L}) = 1. 4 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K M n O} _ {4}
$$

which means the sample contains 

$$
1. 4 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l K M n O _ {4}} \times \frac {5 \mathrm {m o l N a _ {2} C _ {2} O _ {4}}}{2 \mathrm {m o l K M n O _ {4}}} = 3. 5 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l N a _ {2} C _ {2} O _ {4}}
$$

Thus, the %w/w $\mathrm { N a _ { 2 } C _ { 2 } O _ { 4 } }$ in the sample of ore is 

$$
3. 5 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l N a} _ {2} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {4} \times \frac {1 3 4 . 0 0 \mathrm {g N a} _ {2} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {4}}{\mathrm {m o l N a} _ {2} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {4}} = 0. 4 7 6 \mathrm {g N a} _ {2} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {4}
$$

$$
\frac {0 . 4 7 6 \mathrm {g N a} _ {2} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {4}}{0 . 5 1 1 6 \mathrm {g s a m p l e}} \times 1 0 0 = 9 3. 0 _ {2} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {4}
$$

As shown in the following two examples, we can easily extend this approach to an analysis that requires an indirect analysis or a back titration. 

#  Example 9.4.3

A 25.00-mL sample of a liquid bleach is diluted to $1 0 0 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ in a volumetric flask. A 25-mL portion of the diluted sample is transferred by pipet into an Erlenmeyer flask that contains an excess of KI, reducing the $\mathrm { O C l ^ { - } }$ to $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ and producing $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . The liberated $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ is determined by titrating with 0.09892 M ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \cal S } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ , requiring $8 . 9 6 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ to reach the starch indicator end point. Report the %w/v NaOCl in the sample of bleach. 

# Solution

To determine the stoichiometry between the analyte, NaOCl, and the titrant, ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { S } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ , we need to consider both the reaction between OCl and– $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ , and the titration of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ with $\mathrm { N a } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 }$ . 

First, in reducing $0 \mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ to $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ the oxidation state of chlorine changes from $+ 1$ to $^ { - 1 }$ , requiring two electrons. The oxidation of three $\Gamma ^ { - }$ to form $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ releases two electrons as the oxidation state of each iodine changes from $- 1$ in $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ to $- 1 \varLambda$ in $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . A conservation of electrons, therefore, requires that each mole of $0 \mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ produces one mole of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . 

Second, in the titration reaction, $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ is reduced to $\Gamma ^ { - }$ and $\ S _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - }$ is oxidized to $\ S _ { 4 } \mathrm { O } _ { 6 } ^ { 2 - }$ . Reducing $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ to $3 \mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ requires two elections as each iodine changes from an oxidation state of $- 1 \varLambda$ to $^ { - 1 }$ . In oxidizing $\mathrm { \bf S _ { 2 } O _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - } }$ to $\ S _ { 4 } \mathrm { O } _ { 6 } ^ { 2 - }$ , each sulfur changes its oxidation state from $+ 2$ to $+ 2 . 5$ , releasing one electron for each $\ S _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - }$ . A conservation of electrons, therefore, requires that each mole of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ reacts with two moles of $\ S _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - }$ . 

Finally, because each mole of $0 \mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ produces one mole of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ , and each mole of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ reacts with two moles of $\ S _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 } ^ { 2 - }$ , we know that every mole of NaOCl in the sample ultimately results in the consumption of two moles of $\mathrm { N a } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 }$ . 

The moles of $\mathrm { N a } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 }$ used to reach the titration’s end point is 

$$
(0. 0 9 8 9 2 \mathrm {M}) (0. 0 0 8 9 6 \mathrm {L}) = 8. 8 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {N a} _ {2} \mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3}
$$

which means the sample contains 

$$
8. 8 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l N a} _ {2} \mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3} \times \frac {1 \mathrm {m o l N a O C l}}{\mathrm {m o l N a} _ {2} \mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3}} \times \frac {7 4 . 4 4 \mathrm {g N a O C l}}{\mathrm {m o l N a O C l}} = 0. 0 3 2 9 9 \mathrm {g N a O C l}
$$

Thus, the %w/v NaOCl in the diluted sample is 

$$
\frac {0 . 0 3 2 9 9 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {N a O C l}}{2 5 . 0 0 \mathrm {m L}} \times 1 0 0 = 0. 1 3 2
$$

Because the bleach was diluted by a factor of $\pmb { 4 0 } \times$ (25 mL to $1 0 0 0 \mathrm { m L }$ ), the concentration of NaOCl in the bleach is $5 . 2 8 \% \mathrm { w / v . }$ 

The balanced reactions for this analysis are: 

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {O C l} ^ {-} (a q) + 3 \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q) \rightarrow \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} (l) \\ \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3} ^ {2 -} (a q) \rightarrow \mathrm {S} _ {4} \mathrm {O} _ {6} ^ {2 -} (a q) + 3 \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q) \\ \end{array}
$$

#  Example 9.4.4

The amount of ascorbic acid, ${ \mathrm { C } } _ { 6 } { \mathrm { H } } _ { 8 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 6 }$ , in orange juice is determined by oxidizing ascorbic acid to dehydroascorbic acid, ${ \mathrm { C } } _ { 6 } { \mathrm { H } } _ { 6 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 6 } ,$ , with a known amount of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ , and back titrating the excess $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ with ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { S } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ . A $5 . 0 0 \mathrm { - m L }$ sample of filtered orange juice is treated with $5 0 . 0 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of $0 . 0 1 0 2 3  { \mathrm { ~ M ~ I ~ } _ { 3 } } ^ { - }$ . After the oxidation is complete, $1 3 . 8 2 \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.07203 M ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { S } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ is needed to reach the starch indicator end point. Report the concentration ascorbic acid in $\mathrm { m g / 1 0 0 \ m L }$ . 

# Solution

For a back titration we need to determine the stoichiometry between $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ and the analyte, ${ \mathrm { C } } _ { 6 } { \mathrm { H } } _ { 8 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 6 } ,$ and between $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ and the titrant, $\mathrm { N a } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 }$ . The later is easy because we know from Example 9.4.3 that each mole of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ reacts with two moles of $\mathrm { N a } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 }$ . 

In oxidizing ascorbic acid to dehydroascorbic acid, the oxidation state of carbon changes from $+ 2 3$ in ${ \mathrm { C } } _ { 6 } { \mathrm { H } } _ { 8 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 6 }$ to $+ 1$ in ${ \mathrm { C } } _ { 6 } { \mathrm { H } } _ { 6 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 6 }$ . Each carbon releases 1⁄3 of an electron, or a total of two electrons per ascorbic acid. As we learned in Example 9.4.3 , reducing $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ requires two electrons; thus, a conservation of electrons requires that each mole of ascorbic acid consumes one mole of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ . 

The total moles of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ that react with ${ \mathrm { C } } _ { 6 } { \mathrm { H } } _ { 8 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 6 }$ and with $\mathrm { N a } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 3 }$ is 

$$
(0. 0 1 0 2 3 \mathrm {M}) (0. 0 5 0 0 0 \mathrm {L}) = 5. 1 1 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-}
$$

The back titration consumes 

$$
0. 0 1 3 8 2 \mathrm {L} \mathrm {N a} _ {2} \mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3} \times \frac {0 . 0 7 2 0 3 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {N a} _ {2} \mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3}}{\mathrm {L} \mathrm {N a} _ {2} \mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3}} \times \frac {1 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-}}{2 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {N a} _ {2} \mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3}} = 4. 9 7 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-}
$$

Subtracting the moles of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ that react with ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { S } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 3 }$ from the total moles of $\mathbf { I } _ { 3 } ^ { - }$ gives the moles reacting with ascorbic acid. 

$$
5. 1 1 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} - 4. 9 7 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} = 1. 3 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-}
$$

The grams of ascorbic acid in the 5.00-mL sample of orange juice is 

$$
1. 3 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} \times \frac {1 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C} _ {6} \mathrm {H} _ {8} \mathrm {O} _ {6}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-}} \times \frac {1 7 6 . 1 2 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {C} _ {6} \mathrm {H} _ {8} \mathrm {O} _ {6}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C} _ {6} \mathrm {H} _ {8} \mathrm {O} _ {6}} = 2. 4 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {g} \mathrm {C} _ {6} \mathrm {H} _ {8} \mathrm {O} _ {6}
$$

There are 2.43 mg of ascorbic acid in the $5 . 0 0 – \mathrm { m L }$ sample, or 48.6 mg per $1 0 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of orange juice. 

The balanced reactions for this analysis are: 

$$
\mathrm {C} _ {6} \mathrm {H} _ {8} \mathrm {O} _ {6} (a q) + \mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) \rightarrow 3 \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q) + \mathrm {C} _ {6} \mathrm {H} _ {6} \mathrm {O} _ {6} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {H} ^ {+} (a q)
$$

$$
\mathrm {I} _ {3} ^ {-} (a q) + 2 \mathrm {S} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3} ^ {2 -} (a q) \rightarrow \mathrm {S} _ {4} \mathrm {O} _ {6} ^ {2 -} (a q) + 3 \mathrm {I} ^ {-} (a q)
$$

#  Exercise 9.4.5

A quantitative analysis for ethanol, ${ \mathrm { C } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { H } } _ { 6 } { \mathrm { O } }$ , is accomplished by a redox back titration. Ethanol is oxidized to acetic acid, $\mathrm { C _ { 2 } H _ { 4 } O _ { 2 } }$ , using excess dichromate, $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } 0 _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ , which is reduced to ${ \mathrm { C r } } ^ { 3 + }$ . The excess dichromate is titrated with $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ , giving ${ \mathrm { C r } } ^ { 3 + }$ and $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ as products. In a typical analysis, a 5.00-mL sample of a brandy is diluted to $5 0 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ in a volumetric flask. A 10.00- mL sample is taken and the ethanol is removed by distillation and collected in $5 0 . 0 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of an acidified solution of $0 . 0 2 0 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ }$ $\mathrm { K _ { 2 } C r _ { 2 } O 7 }$ . A back titration of the unreacted $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ requires $2 1 . 4 8 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of $0 . 1 0 1 4 \mathrm { ~ M ~ F e } ^ { 2 + }$ . Calculate the %w/v ethanol in the brandy. 

# Answer

For a back titration we need to determine the stoichiometry between $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } 0 _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ and the analyte, ${ \mathrm { C } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { H } } _ { 6 } { \mathrm { O } }$ , and between $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } 0 _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ and the titrant, $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ . In oxidizing ethanol to acetic acid, the oxidation state of carbon changes from $^ { - 2 }$ in $\mathrm { C _ { 2 } H _ { 6 } O }$ to 0 in $\mathrm { C _ { 2 } H _ { 4 } O _ { 2 } }$ . Each carbon releases two electrons, or a total of four electrons per $\mathrm { C _ { 2 } H _ { 6 } O }$ . In reducing $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } { \bf O } _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ , in which each chromium has an oxidation state of $+ 6$ , to $\mathrm { C r } ^ { 3 + }$ , each chromium loses three electrons, for a total of six electrons per $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } 0 _ { 7 } ^ { 2 - }$ . Oxidation of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ to $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ requires one electron. A conservation of electrons requires that each mole of ${ \bf K } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 7 }$ (6 moles of $e ^ { - } )$ reacts with six moles of $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ (6 moles of $e ^ { - }$ ), and that four moles of ${ \bf K } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C r } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 7 }$ (24 moles of $e ^ { - }$ ) react with six moles of $\mathrm { C _ { 2 } H _ { 6 } O }$ (24 moles of $e ^ { - }$ ). 

The total moles of ${ \mathrm { K } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { C r } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 7 }$ that react with $\mathrm { C _ { 2 } H _ { 6 } O }$ and with $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ is 

$$
(0. 0 2 0 0 \mathrm {M} \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7}) (0. 0 5 0 0 0 \mathrm {L}) = 1. 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7}
$$

The back titration with $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ consumes 

$$
(0. 1 0 1 4 \mathrm {M} \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}) (0. 0 2 1 4 8 \mathrm {L}) \times \frac {1 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7}}{6 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {F e} ^ {2 +}} = 3. 6 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7}
$$

Subtracting the moles of $\mathrm { K _ { 2 } C r _ { 2 } O _ { 7 } }$ that react with $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ from the total moles of ${ \mathrm { K } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { C r } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 7 }$ gives the moles that react with the analyte. 

$$
(1. 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7}) - (3. 6 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7}) = 6. 3 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7}
$$

The grams of ethanol in the $1 0 . 0 0 \mathrm { - m L }$ sample of diluted brandy is 

$$
6. 3 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7} \times \frac {6 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {H} _ {6} \mathrm {O}}{4 \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C r} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {7}} \times \frac {4 6 . 0 7 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {H} _ {6} \mathrm {O}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {H} _ {6} \mathrm {O}} = 0. 0 4 4 0 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {H} _ {6} \mathrm {O}
$$

The %w/v $\mathrm { C _ { 2 } H _ { 6 } O }$ in the brandy is 

$$
\frac {0 . 0 4 4 0 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {C} _ {2} \mathrm {H} _ {6} \mathrm {O}}{1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} \text {d i l u t e d b r a n d y}} \times \frac {5 0 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} \text {d i l u t e d b r a n d y}}{5 . 0 0 \mathrm {m L} \text {b r a n d y}} \times 1 0 0 = 4 4. 0 _ {2} \mathrm {H} _ {6} \mathrm {O}
$$

# Evaluation of Redox Titrimetry

The scale of operations, accuracy, precision, sensitivity, time, and cost of a redox titration are similar to those described earlier in this chapter for an acid–base or a complexation titration. As with an acid–base titration, we can extend a redox titration to the analysis of a mixture of analytes if there is a significant difference in their oxidation or reduction potentials. Figure 9.4.7 shows an example of the titration curve for a mixture of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ and $\mathsf { S n } ^ { 2 + }$ using $\mathsf { C e ^ { 4 + } }$ as the titrant. A titration of a mixture of analytes is possible if their standard state potentials or formal potentials differ by at least $2 0 0 \mathrm { m V } .$ 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/fe76155dfa7376d2fc8c613ffe1b6089fa248d9ebe173c72623d4f41e9b9e373.jpg)



Figure 9.4.7 . Titration curve for the titration of $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.0125 M $\mathsf { S n } ^ { 2 + }$ and 0.0250 M $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ with 0.050 M Ce . Both the titrand 4+ and the titrant are 1M in HCl.


This page titled 9.4: Redox Titrations is shared under a CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license and was authored, remixed, and/or curated by David Harvey. 

9.4: Redox Titrations by David Harvey is licensed CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. 

# 9.5: Precipitation Titrations

Thus far we have examined titrimetric methods based on acid–base, complexation, and oxidation–reduction reactions. A reaction in which the analyte and titrant form an insoluble precipitate also can serve as the basis for a titration. We call this type of titration a precipitation titration. 

One of the earliest precipitation titrations—developed at the end of the eighteenth century—was the analysis of $\mathrm { K } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C O } _ { 3 }$ and $\mathrm { K } _ { 2 } \mathrm { S O } _ { 4 }$ in potash. Calcium nitrate, $\mathrm { C a } ( \mathrm { N O } _ { 3 } ) _ { 2 }$ , was used as the titrant, which forms a precipitate of $\mathrm { C a C O } _ { 3 }$ and $\mathrm { C a S O _ { 4 } }$ . The titration’s end point was signaled by noting when the addition of titrant ceased to generate additional precipitate. The importance of precipitation titrimetry as an analytical method reached its zenith in the nineteenth century when several methods were developed for determining ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ and halide ions. 

# Titration Curves

A precipitation titration curve follows the change in either the titrand’s or the titrant’s concentration as a function of the titrant’s volume. As we did for other titrations, we first show how to calculate the titration curve and then demonstrate how we can sketch a reasonable approximation of the titration curve. 

# Calculating the Titration Curve

Let’s calculate the titration curve for the titration of $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.0500 M NaCl with 0.100 M AgNO . The reaction in this case is3 

$$
\mathrm {A g} ^ {+} (a q) + \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} (a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {A g C l} (s)
$$

Because the reaction’s equilibrium constant is so large 

$$
K = \left(K _ {\mathrm {s p}}\right) ^ {- 1} = \left(1. 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 0}\right) ^ {- 1} = 5. 6 \times 1 0 ^ {9}
$$

we may assume that ${ \bf A } { \bf g } ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ react completely. 

By now you are familiar with our approach to calculating a titration curve. The first task is to calculate the volume of ${ \bf A } { \bf g } ^ { + }$ needed to reach the equivalence point. The stoichiometry of the reaction requires that 

$$
\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} = M _ {\mathrm {A g}} V _ {\mathrm {A g}} = M _ {\mathrm {C l}} V _ {\mathrm {C l}} = \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {C l} ^ {-}
$$

Solving for the volume of ${ \bf A } { \bf g } ^ { + }$ 

$$
V _ {e q} = V _ {\mathrm {A g}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {C l}} V _ {\mathrm {C l}}}{M _ {\mathrm {A g}}} = \frac {(0 . 0 5 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}} = 2 5. 0 \mathrm {m L}
$$

shows that we need $2 5 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ to reach the equivalence point. 

Before the equivalence point the titrand, $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ , is in excess. The concentration of unreacted $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ after we add $1 0 . 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ , for example, is 

$$
[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} ] = \frac {(\mathrm {m o l C l} ^ {-}) _ {\mathrm {i n i t i a l}} - (\mathrm {m o l A g} ^ {+}) _ {\mathrm {a d d e d}}}{\mathrm {t o t a l v o l u m e}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {C l}} V _ {\mathrm {C l}} - M _ {\mathrm {A g}} V _ {\mathrm {A g}}}{V _ {\mathrm {C l}} + V _ {\mathrm {A g}}}
$$

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \right] = \frac {(0 . 0 5 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}) - (0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 2. 5 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {M}
$$

which corresponds to a pCl of 1.60. 

At the titration’s equivalence point, we know that the concentrations of ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ are equal. To calculate the concentration of $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ we use the $K _ { \mathrm { s p } }$ for AgCl; thus 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {s p}} = \left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \right] = (x) (x) = 1. 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 0}
$$

Solving for $\boldsymbol { x }$ gives $\left[ \mathsf { C l } ^ { - } \right]$ as $\mathbf { 1 . 3 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } M }$ , or a pCl of 4.89. 

After the equivalence point, the titrant is in excess. We first calculate the concentration of excess ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ and then use the $K _ { \mathrm { s p } }$ expression to calculate the concentration of $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ . For example, after adding $3 5 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of titrant 

$$
[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} ] = \frac {(\mathrm {m o l A g} ^ {+}) _ {\mathrm {a d d e d}} - (\mathrm {m o l C l} ^ {-}) _ {\mathrm {i n i t i a l}}}{\mathrm {t o t a l v o l u m e}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {A g}} V _ {\mathrm {A g}} - M _ {\mathrm {C l}} V _ {\mathrm {C l}}}{V _ {\mathrm {A g}} + V _ {\mathrm {C l}}}
$$

$$
\left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right] = \frac {(0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (3 5 . 0 \mathrm {m L}) - (0 . 0 5 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{3 5 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 1. 1 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {M}
$$

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \right] = \frac {K _ {\mathrm {s p}}}{\left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right]} = \frac {1 . 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 0}}{1 . 1 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2}} = 1. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 8} \mathrm {M}
$$

or a pCl of 7.81. Additional results for the titration curve are shown in Table 9.5.1 and Figure 9.5.1 . 


Table 9.5.1 . Titration of $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.0500 M NaCl with 0.100 M $\mathbf { A g N O _ { 3 } }$


<table><tr><td>volume of AgNO3(mL)</td><td>pCl</td><td>volume of AgNO3(mL)</td><td>pCl</td></tr><tr><td>0.00</td><td>1.30</td><td>30.0</td><td>7.54</td></tr><tr><td>5.00</td><td>1.44</td><td>35.0</td><td>7.82</td></tr><tr><td>10.0</td><td>1.60</td><td>40.0</td><td>7.97</td></tr><tr><td>15.0</td><td>1.81</td><td>45.0</td><td>8.07</td></tr><tr><td>20.0</td><td>2.15</td><td>50.0</td><td>8.14</td></tr><tr><td>25.0</td><td>4.89</td><td></td><td></td></tr></table>

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/74a49a18243b4f1104e6fafb105753f3e3d5dcf007de8a0b0e07d687774f5528.jpg)



Figure 9.5.1 . Titration curve for the titration of 50.0 mL of 0.0500 M NaCl with 0.100 M AgNO . The red points corresponds to3 the data in Table 9.5.1 . The blue line shows the complete titration curve.


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/086fa323c92b5ad905667644563da8a444a8cb6f4bd6a34cc6632daa35e0b159.jpg)


# Exercise 9.5.1

When calculating a precipitation titration curve, you can choose to follow the change in the titrant’s concentration or the change in the titrand’s concentration. Calculate the titration curve for the titration of $5 0 . 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.0500 M $\mathrm { A g N O _ { 3 } }$ with 0.100 M NaCl as pAg versus $V _ { \mathrm { { N a C l } } }$ , and as pCl versus $V _ { \mathrm { { N a C l } } }$ . 

# Answer

The first task is to calculate the volume of NaCl needed to reach the equivalence point; thus 

$$
V _ {e q} = V _ {\mathrm {N a C l}} = \frac {M _ {\mathrm {A g}} V _ {\mathrm {A g}}}{M _ {\mathrm {N a C l}}} = \frac {(0 . 0 5 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}} = 2 5. 0 \mathrm {m L}
$$

Before the equivalence point the titrand, ${ \sf A g } ^ { + }$ , is in excess. The concentration of unreacted ${ \bf A } { \bf g } ^ { + }$ after adding $1 0 . 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of NaCl, for example, is 

$$
[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} ] = \frac {(0 . 0 5 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}) - (0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 1 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 2. 5 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {M}
$$

which corresponds to a pAg of 1.60. To find the concentration of $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ we use the $K _ { \mathrm { s p } }$ for AgCl; thus 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \right] = \frac {K _ {\mathrm {s p}}}{\left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right]} = \frac {1 . 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 0}}{2 . 5 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2}} = 7. 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 9} \mathrm {M}
$$

or a pCl of 8.14. 

At the titration’s equivalence point, we know that the concentrations of ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ and $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ are equal. To calculate their concentrations we use the Ksp expression for $\mathrm { \sf A g C l }$ ; thus 

$$
K _ {\mathrm {s p}} = \left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right] \left[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \right] = (x) (x) = 1. 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 0}
$$

Solving for x gives the concentration of ${ \bf A } { \bf g } ^ { + }$ and the concentration of $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ as $\mathbf { 1 . 3 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } M }$ , or a pAg and a pCl of 4.89. 

After the equivalence point, the titrant is in excess. For example, after adding $3 5 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of titrant 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \right] = \frac {(0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (3 5 . 0 \mathrm {m L}) - (0 . 0 5 0 0 \mathrm {M}) (5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L})}{3 5 . 0 \mathrm {m L} + 5 0 . 0 \mathrm {m L}} = 1. 1 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {M}
$$

or a pCl of 1.93. To find the concentration of ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ we use the $K _ { \mathrm { s p } }$ for AgCl; thus 

$$
\left[ \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \right] = \frac {K _ {\mathrm {s p}}}{\left[ \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \right]} = \frac {1 . 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 0}}{1 . 1 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2}} = 1. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 8} \mathrm {M}
$$

or a pAg of 7.82. The following table summarizes additional results for this titration. 

<table><tr><td>volume of NaCl (mL)</td><td>pAg</td><td>pCl</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>1.30</td><td>—</td></tr><tr><td>5.00</td><td>1.44</td><td>8.31</td></tr><tr><td>10.0</td><td>1.60</td><td>8.14</td></tr><tr><td>15.0</td><td>1.81</td><td>7.93</td></tr><tr><td>20.0</td><td>2.15</td><td>7.60</td></tr><tr><td>25.0</td><td>4.89</td><td>4.89</td></tr><tr><td>30.0</td><td>7.54</td><td>2.20</td></tr><tr><td>35.0</td><td>7.82</td><td>1.93</td></tr><tr><td>40.0</td><td>7.97</td><td>1.78</td></tr><tr><td>45.0</td><td>8.07</td><td>1.68</td></tr><tr><td>50.0</td><td>8.14</td><td>1.60</td></tr></table>

# Sketching the Titration Curve

To evaluate the relationship between a titration’s equivalence point and its end point we need to construct only a reasonable approximation of the exact titration curve. In this section we demonstrate a simple method for sketching a precipitation titration curve. Our goal is to sketch the titration curve quickly, using as few calculations as possible. Let’s use the titration of $5 0 . 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.0500 M NaCl with $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { M A g N O _ { 3 } }$ . 

This is the same example that we used in developing the calculations for a precipitation titration curve. You can review the results of that calculation in Table 9.5.1 and Figure 9.5.1 . 

We begin by calculating the titration’s equivalence point volume, which, as we determined earlier, is $2 5 . 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ . Next we draw our axes, placing pCl on the y-axis and the titrant’s volume on the $\boldsymbol { x }$ -axis. To indicate the equivalence point’s volume, we draw a 

vertical line that intersects the $x \cdot$ -axis at $2 5 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of $\mathrm { A g N O _ { 3 } }$ . Figure 9.5.2 a shows the result of this first step in our sketch. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/c7ac062ae18560173a96730f47287b6a6ac6346a8b807cb598854fb2424f3db9.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/060a9ddc6828b5ea9722af2a4122023f3d46b98dc14079bc347c88f49ee8db5a.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/6cf29141091742f13130f4f02777c0d467c672b36f4cd209ba7b9cc2e909aea8.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/ffd341b05e2d63b4e259bb2601d8b0925273871dcef92d35b1a5f2678b553f90.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/759f8dfefc5fd9c197ceeeb73728822df355301232c4c68bd2d6ab123ea6fefd.jpg)


![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/4f4aa43f911e44aa949aecd5f54ca49670252492e5a083e455b2265e2d344b8c.jpg)



Figure 9.5.2 . Illustrations showing the steps in sketching an approximate titration curve for the titration of $5 0 . 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of $0 . 0 5 0 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ }$ NaCl with $0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ A g N O _ { 3 } }$ (a) locating the equivalence point volume; (b) plotting two points before the equivalence point; (c) plotting two points after the equivalence point; (d) preliminary approximation of titration curve using straight-lines; (e) final approximation of titration curve using a smooth curve; (f) comparison of approximate titration curve (solid black line) and exact titration curve (dashed red line). See the text for additional details. A better fit is possible if the two points before the equivalence point are further apart—for example, 0 mL and 20 mL—and the two points after the equivalence point are further apart.


Before the equivalence point, $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ is present in excess and pCl is determined by the concentration of unreacted $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } } .$ . As we learned earlier, the calculations are straightforward. Figure 9.5.2 b shows pCl after adding $1 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ and 20.0 mL of $\mathrm { A g N O } _ { 3 }$ . 

After the equivalence point, ${ \bf A } { \bf g } ^ { + }$ is in excess and the concentration of $\Sigma \mathrm { l ^ { - } }$ is determined by the solubility of AgCl. Again, the calculations are straightforward. Figure 9.5.2 c shows pCl after adding $3 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ and $4 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of $\mathrm { A g N O } _ { 3 }$ . 

Next, we draw a straight line through each pair of points, extending them through the vertical line that represents the equivalence point’s volume (Figure 9.5.2 d). Finally, we complete our sketch by drawing a smooth curve that connects the three straight-line segments (Figure 9.5.2 e). A comparison of our sketch to the exact titration curve (Figure 9.5.2 f) shows that they are in close agreement. 

# Selecting and Evaluating the End Point

At the beginning of this section we noted that the first precipitation titration used the cessation of precipitation to signal the end point. At best, this is a cumbersome method for detecting a titration’s end point. Before precipitation titrimetry became practical, 

better methods for identifying the end point were necessary. 

# Finding the End Point With an Indicator

There are three general types of indicators for a precipitation titration, each of which changes color at or near the titration’s equivalence point. The first type of indicator is a species that forms a precipitate with the titrant. In the Mohr method for $\Sigma \mathrm { l ^ { - } }$ using ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ as a titrant, for example, a small amount of ${ \mathrm { K } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { C r } } 0 _ { 4 }$ is added to the titrand’s solution. The titration’s end point is the formation of a reddish-brown precipitate of ${ \bf A } { \bf g } _ { 2 } { \bf C r } { \bf O } _ { 4 }$ . 

The Mohr method was first published in 1855 by Karl Friedrich Mohr. 

Because $\mathrm { C r O } _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - }$ imparts a yellow color to the solution, which might obscure the end point, only a small amount of ${ \mathrm { K } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { C r } } 0 _ { 4 }$ is added. As a result, the end point is always later than the equivalence point. To compensate for this positive determinate error, an analytefree reagent blank is analyzed to determine the volume of titrant needed to affect a change in the indicator’s color. Subtracting the end point for the reagent blank from the titrand’s end point gives the titration’s end point. Because $\mathrm { C r O } _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - }$ is a weak base, the titrand’s solution is made slightly alkaline. If the pH is too acidic, chromate is present as $\bf { H C r O } _ { 4 } ^ { - }$ instead of $\mathrm { C r O } _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - }$ , and the ${ \bf A } { \bf g } _ { 2 } { \bf C r } { \bf O } _ { 4 }$ end point is delayed. The pH also must be less than 10 to avoid the precipitation of silver hydroxide. 

A second type of indicator uses a species that forms a colored complex with the titrant or the titrand. In the Volhard method for ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ using KSCN as the titrant, for example, a small amount of $\mathsf { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ is added to the titrand’s solution. The titration’s end point is the formation of the reddish-colored $\mathrm { F e } ( \mathsf { S C N } ) ^ { 2 + }$ complex. The titration is carried out in an acidic solution to prevent the precipitation of $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 3 + }$ as $\mathrm { F e ( O H ) _ { 3 } }$ . 

The Volhard method was first published in 1874 by Jacob Volhard. 

The third type of end point uses a species that changes color when it adsorbs to the precipitate. In the Fajans method for $\Sigma ^ { \mathrm { l ^ { - } } }$ using ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ as a titrant, for example, the anionic dye dichlorofluoroscein is added to the titrand’s solution. Before the end point, the precipitate of AgCl has a negative surface charge due to the adsorption of excess Cl . Because dichlorofluoroscein also carries a– negative charge, it is repelled by the precipitate and remains in solution where it has a greenish-yellow color. After the end point, the surface of the precipitate carries a positive surface charge due to the adsorption of excess ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ . Dichlorofluoroscein now adsorbs to the precipitate’s surface where its color is pink. This change in the indicator’s color signals the end point. 

The Fajans method was first published in the 1920s by Kasimir Fajans. 

# Finding the End Point Potentiometrically

Another method for locating the end point is a potentiometric titration in which we monitor the change in the titrant’s or the titrand’s concentration using an ion-selective electrode. The end point is found by visually examining the titration curve. For a discussion of potentiometry and ion-selective electrodes, see Chapter 11. 

# Quantitative Applications

Although precipitation titrimetry rarely is listed as a standard method of analysis, it is useful as a secondary analytical method to verify other analytical methods. Most precipitation titrations use ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ as either the titrand or the titrant. A titration in which ${ \tt A } { \tt g } ^ { + }$ is the titrant is called an argentometric titration. Table 9.5.2 provides a list of several typical precipitation titrations. 

Table 9.5.2 . Representative Examples of Precipitation Titrations 

<table><tr><td>titrand</td><td>titrant</td><td>end point</td></tr><tr><td>AsO3-4</td><td>AgNO3and KSCN</td><td>Volhard</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2">Br-</td><td>AgNO3</td><td>Mohr or Fajans</td></tr><tr><td>AgNO3and KSCN</td><td>Volhard</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2">Cl-</td><td>AgNO3</td><td>Mohr or Fajarns</td></tr><tr><td>AgNO3and KSCN</td><td>Volhard*</td></tr><tr><td>CO3-</td><td>AgNO3and KSCN</td><td>Volhard*</td></tr><tr><td>C2O42-</td><td>AgNO3and KSCN</td><td>Volhard*</td></tr><tr><td>CrO42-</td><td>AgNO3and KSCN</td><td>Volhard*</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2">I-</td><td>AgNO3</td><td>Fajans</td></tr><tr><td>AgNO3and KSCN</td><td>Volhard</td></tr><tr><td>PO3-4</td><td>AgNO3and KSCN</td><td>Volhard*</td></tr><tr><td>S2-</td><td>AgNO3and KSCN</td><td>Volhard*</td></tr><tr><td>SCN-</td><td>AgNO3and KSCN</td><td>Volhard*</td></tr></table>


When two titrants are listed $\mathrm { \sf R P N O _ { 3 } }$ and KSCN), the analysis is by a back titration; the first titrant, $\mathrm { A g N O } _ { 3 }$ , is added in excess and the excess is titrated using the second titrant, KSCN. For those Volhard methods identified with an asterisk $( ^ { * } )$ , the precipitated silver salt is removed before carrying out the back titration. 


# Quantitative Calculations

The quantitative relationship between the titrand and the titrant is determined by the stoichiometry of the titration reaction. If you are unsure of the balanced reaction, you can deduce the stoichiometry from the precipitate’s formula. For example, in forming a precipitate of $\mathrm { A g } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C r } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 4 } .$ , each mole of $\mathsf { C r O } _ { 4 } ^ { 2 - }$ reacts with two moles of ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ . 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/33829ca5a8f92a050175e090ff07e295b2c4ed8fdddd4edaf1cd0429c5e85d6e.jpg)


# Example 9.5.1

A mixture containing only KCl and NaBr is analyzed by the Mohr method. A 0.3172-g sample is dissolved in $5 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of water and titrated to the $\mathrm { A g } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C r } { \mathrm { O } } _ { 4 }$ end point, requiring $3 6 . 8 5 \mathrm { m L }$ of $0 . 1 1 2 0 \mathrm { M A g N O _ { 3 } }$ . A blank titration requires $0 . 7 1 \mathrm { m L }$ of titrant to reach the same end point. Report the %w/w KCl in the sample. 

# Solution

To find the moles of titrant reacting with the sample, we first need to correct for the reagent blank; thus 

$$
V _ {\mathrm {A g}} = 3 6. 8 5 \mathrm {m L} - 0. 7 1 \mathrm {m L} = 3 6. 1 4 \mathrm {m L}
$$

$$
(0. 1 1 2 0 \mathrm {M}) (0. 0 3 6 1 4 \mathrm {L}) = 4. 0 4 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {A g N O} _ {3}
$$

Titrating with $\mathrm { A g N O _ { 3 } }$ produces a precipitate of AgCl and AgBr. In forming the precipitates, each mole of KCl consumes one mole of $\mathsf { A g N O } _ { 3 }$ and each mole of NaBr consumes one mole of $\mathrm { A g N O } _ { 3 }$ ; thus 

$$
\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K C l} + \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {N a B r} = 4. 0 4 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {A g N O} _ {3}
$$

We are interested in finding the mass of KCl, so let’s rewrite this equation in terms of mass. We know that 

$$
\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K C l} = \frac {\mathrm {g} \mathrm {K C l}}{7 4 . 5 5 1 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {K C l} / \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K C l}}
$$

$$
\mathrm {m o l N a B r} = \frac {\mathrm {g N a B r}}{1 0 2 . 8 9 \mathrm {g N a B r / m o l N a B r}}
$$

which we substitute back into the previous equation 

$$
\frac {\mathrm {g K C l}}{7 4 . 5 5 1 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {K C l} / \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K C l}} + \frac {\mathrm {g N a B r}}{1 0 2 . 8 9 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {N a B r} / \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {N a B r}} = 4. 0 4 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3}
$$

Because this equation has two unknowns—g KCl and g NaBr—we need another equation that includes both unknowns. A simple equation takes advantage of the fact that the sample contains only KCl and NaBr; thus, 

$$
\mathrm {g N a B r} = 0. 3 1 7 2 \mathrm {g} - \mathrm {g K C l}
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {g K C l}}{7 4 . 5 5 1 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {K C l} / \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K C l}} + \frac {0 . 3 1 7 2 \mathrm {g} - \mathrm {g} \mathrm {K C l}}{1 0 2 . 8 9 \mathrm {g} \mathrm {N a B r} / \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {N a B r}} = 4. 0 4 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3}
$$

$$
1. 3 4 1 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} (\mathrm {g K C l}) + 3. 0 8 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} - 9. 7 1 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} (\mathrm {g K C l}) = 4. 0 4 8 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3}
$$

$$
3. 6 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} (\mathrm {g K C l}) = 9. 6 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}
$$

The sample contains $0 . 2 6 2 { \bf \tt g }$ of KCl and the %w/w KCl in the sample is 

$$
\frac {0 . 2 6 2 \mathrm {g K C l}}{0 . 3 1 7 2 \mathrm {g s a m p l e}} \times 1 0 0 = 8 2. 6
$$

The analysis for $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ using the Volhard method requires a back titration. A typical calculation is shown in the following example. 

#  Example 9.5.2

The %w/w I in a 0.6712-g sample is determined by a Volhard titration. After adding – $5 0 . 0 0 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.05619 M $\mathrm { A g N O } _ { 3 }$ and allowing the precipitate to form, the remaining silver is back titrated with 0.05322 M KSCN, requiring $3 5 . 1 4 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ to reach the end point. Report the %w/w $\Gamma ^ { - }$ in the sample. 

# Solution

There are two precipitates in this analysis: $\mathrm { A g N O _ { 3 } }$ and $\Gamma ^ { - }$ form a precipitate of AgI, and $\mathrm { A g N O } _ { 3 }$ and KSCN form a precipitate of AgSCN. Each mole of $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ consumes one mole of $\mathrm { A g N O _ { 3 } }$ and each mole of KSCN consumes one mole of $\mathrm { A g N O } _ { 3 }$ ; thus 

$$
\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {A g N O} _ {3} = \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} ^ {-} + \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K S C N}
$$

Solving for the moles of $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ we find 

$$
\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} ^ {-} = \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {A g N O} _ {3} - \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K S C N} = M _ {\mathrm {A g}} V _ {\mathrm {A g}} - M _ {\mathrm {K S C N}} V _ {\mathrm {K S C N}}
$$

$$
\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} ^ {-} = (0. 0 5 6 1 9 \mathrm {M}) (0. 0 5 0 0 \mathrm {L}) - (0. 0 5 3 2 2 \mathrm {M}) (0. 0 3 5 1 4 \mathrm {L}) = 9. 3 9 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}
$$

The %w/w $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ in the sample is 

$$
\frac {\left(9 . 3 9 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} ^ {-}\right) \times \frac {1 2 6 . 9 \mathrm {g I} ^ {-}}{\mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {I} ^ {-}}}{0 . 6 7 1 2 \mathrm {g s a m p l e}} \times 1 0 0 = 1 7. 7 6 ^ {-}
$$

#  Exercise 9.5.2

A 1.963-g sample of an alloy is dissolved in $\mathrm { H N O } _ { 3 }$ and diluted to volume in a 100-mL volumetric flask. Titrating a 25.00-mL portion with 0.1078 M KSCN requires $2 7 . 1 9 \mathrm { m L }$ to reach the end point. Calculate the %w/w Ag in the alloy. 

# Answer

The titration uses 

$$
(0. 1 0 7 8 \mathrm {M K S C N}) (0. 0 2 7 1 9 \mathrm {L}) = 2. 9 3 1 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l} \mathrm {K S C N}
$$

The stoichiometry between $\mathsf { S C N ^ { - } }$ and ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ is 1:1; thus, there are 

$$
2. 9 3 1 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m o l A g} ^ {+} \times \frac {1 0 7 . 8 7 \mathrm {g A g}}{\mathrm {m o l A g}} = 0. 3 1 6 2 \mathrm {g A g}
$$

in the 25.00 mL sample. Because this represents 1⁄4 of the total solution, there are $\mathbf { 0 . 3 1 6 2 \times 4 }$ or $1 . 2 6 5 \mathrm { \bf g A g }$ in the alloy. The %w/w Ag in the alloy is 

$$
\frac {1 . 2 6 5 \mathrm {g A g}}{1 . 9 6 3 \mathrm {g s a m p l e}} \times 1 0 0 = 6 4. 4 4
$$

# Evaluation of Precipitation Titrimetry

The scale of operations, accuracy, precision, sensitivity, time, and cost of a precipitation titration is similar to those described elsewhere in this chapter for acid–base, complexation, and redox titrations. Precipitation titrations also can be extended to the analysis of mixtures provided there is a significant difference in the solubilities of the precipitates. Figure 9.5.3 shows an example of a titration curve for a mixture of $\mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ and $\mathrm { C l ^ { - } }$ using ${ \bf A 8 } ^ { + }$ as a titrant. 

![image](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2026-03-29/7450c696-9771-4b4e-984b-bd9052f6ba47/84e764650818a6956b4df4a590eae9bac438b610e7f8f46c52b6f7415b7c8da4.jpg)



Figure 9.5.3 . Titration curve for the titration of a $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ mixture of $0 . 0 5 0 0 \mathrm { M } \mathrm { I } ^ { - }$ and 0.0500 M Cl using – $0 . 1 0 0  { \mathrm { ~ M ~ A g } } ^ { + }$ as a titrant. The red arrows show the end points. Note that the end point for $\Gamma ^ { - }$ is earlier than the end point for $\Sigma \mathrm { I ^ { - } }$ because AgI is less soluble than AgCl.

# 9.6: Problems

Some of the problems that follow require one or more equilibrium constants or standard state potentials. For your convenience, here are hyperlinks to the appendices containing these constants: 

Appendix 10: Solubility Products 

Appendix 11: Acid Dissociation Constants 

Appendix 12: Metal-Ligand Formation Constants 

Appendix 13: Standard State Reduction Potentials 

1. Calculate or sketch titration curves for the following acid–base titrations. 

(a) 25.0 mL of 0.100 M NaOH with 0.0500 M HCl 

(b) 50.0 mL of 0.0500 M HCOOH with 0.100 M NaOH 

(c) 50.0 mL of 0.100 M $\mathrm { N H } _ { 3 }$ with 0.100 M HCl 

(d) 50.0 mL of 0.0500 M ethylenediamine with 0.100 M HCl 

(e) 50.0 mL of 0.0400 M citric acid with 0.120 M NaOH 

(f) $5 0 . 0 \mathrm { m L }$ of 0.0400 M ${ \mathrm { H } } _ { 3 } { \mathrm { P O } } _ { 4 }$ with 0.120 M NaOH 

2. Locate the equivalence point(s) for each titration curve in problem 1 and, where feasible, calculate the pH at the equivalence point. What is the stoichiometric relationship between the moles of acid and the moles of base for each of these equivalence points? 

3. Suggest an appropriate visual indicator for each of the titrations in problem 1. 

4. To sketch the titration curve for a weak acid we approximate the pH at $1 0 \%$ of the equivalence point volume as $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } } - 1$ , and the pH at $9 0 \%$ of the equivalence point volume as $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } } + 1$ . Show that these assumptions are reasonable. 

5. Tartaric acid, $\mathrm { H _ { 2 } C _ { 4 } H _ { 4 } O _ { 6 } } ,$ , is a diprotic weak acid with a $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a 1 } }$ of 3.0 and a $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a } 2 }$ of 4.4. Suppose you have a sample of impure tartaric acid (purity $> 8 0 \%$ ), and that you plan to determine its purity by titrating with a solution of 0.1 M NaOH using an indicator to signal the end point. Describe how you will carry out the analysis, paying particular attention to how much sample to use, the desired pH range for the indicator, and how you will calculate the %w/w tartaric acid. Assume your buret has a maximum capacity of 50 mL.

7. Schwartz published the following simulated data for the titration of a $\mathbf { 1 . 0 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 } M }$ solution of a monoprotic weak acid $( \mathrm { p } K _ { \mathrm { a } } =$ 8.16) with $\mathbf { 1 . 0 0 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } M N a C }$ H [Schwartz, L. M. J. Chem. Educ. 1992, 69, 879–883]. The simulation assumes that a 50-mL pipet is used to transfer a portion of the weak acid solution to the titration vessel. A calibration of the pipet shows that it delivers a volume of only $4 9 . 9 4 ~ \mathrm { m L }$ . Prepare normal, first derivative, second derivative, and Gran plot titration curves for this data, and determine the equivalence point for each. How do these equivalence points compare to the expected equivalence point? Comment on the utility of each titration curve for the analysis of very dilute solutions of very weak acids.
